| **Marginally Stable** (Critically Stable) | Simple poles on $j\omega$-axis, no RHP poles | Sustained oscillation, neither grows nor decays |
| **Unstable** | At least one pole in RHP, **OR** repeated poles on $j\omega$-axis | Output grows without bound |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

To analyze stability using pole locations, follow this logical chain:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Characteristic Equation:** The characteristic equation is formed by setting the denominator of the closed-loop transfer function to zero.
   $$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad q(s) = 0$$

2. **Step 2 — Find the Poles:** Solve $q(s) = 0$ to obtain poles $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n$.

3. **Step 3 — Classify Each Pole:**
   - If all $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$ → **Stable**
   - If any $\text{Re}(p_i) > 0$ → **Unstable**
   - If simple pair on $j\omega$-axis and rest in LHP → **Marginally Stable**

4. **Step 4 — Relate Pole Form to Time Response:**
   - **Real pole** $p = -\sigma$ in LHP → Decaying exponential $e^{-\sigma t}$
   - **Complex conjugate pair** $p = -\sigma \pm j\omega_d$ → Damped sinusoid $e^{-\sigma t}\sin(\omega_d t)$
   - **Pure imaginary pair** $p = \pm j\omega$ → Sustained oscillation $\sin(\omega t)$
   - **Real pole in RHP** $p = +\sigma$ → Growing exponential $e^{+\sigma t}$ (UNSTABLE)

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why characteristic equation?** The denominator $D(s)$ of $T(s)$ dictates the natural modes. The numerator $N(s)$ only scales them; it cannot create new modes. Hence stability depends on $D(s) = 0$.
- **Why strictly less than zero?** Because $e^{-at}$ with $a > 0$ decays to zero as $t \to \infty$. Even an infinitesimally small negative real part guarantees decay.
- **Why repeated poles on $j\omega$-axis are unstable?** A double pole at $s = \pm j\omega$ produces a response of the form $t\sin(\omega t)$, which grows unbounded with time despite being on the boundary.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Stability Implication |
|---|---------|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Closed-loop TF | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Poles from $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ |
| 2 | Characteristic Equation | $a_n s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1 s + a_0 = 0$ | All coefficients must be **positive** for stability (necessary) |
| 3 | Real Pole Response | $y(t) = K e^{p t}$ | Stable iff $p < 0$ |
| 4 | Complex Pole Response | $y(t) = K e^{-\sigma t}\cos(\omega_d t + \phi)$ | Stable iff $\sigma > 0$ |
| 5 | 2nd-order Necessary & Sufficient | $a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0 = 0$ | Stable iff $a_2 > 0,\; a_1 > 0,\; a_0 > 0$ |
| 6 | 3rd-order Necessary & Sufficient | $a_3 s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0 = 0$ | Stable iff all $a_i > 0$ AND $a_2 a_1 > a_3 a_0$ |
| 7 | Routh Array First Column | All entries positive | Necessary & Sufficient for stability |
| 8 | Number of Sign Changes | Sign changes in first column | Equals number of RHP poles |
| 9 | Auxiliary Equation | Formed from row above zero-row | Gives imaginary axis pole pairs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** For a 3rd-order system, $a_3, a_2, a_1, a_0 > 0$ is **necessary but NOT sufficient**. The Routh determinant condition $a_2 a_1 > a_3 a_0$ is the missing sufficiency check. KTU examiners specifically test this trap.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Pole-Location Stability |
|-------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Aerospace | Aircraft autopilot stability — pilot commands must not cause divergence |
| Robotics | Robotic arm controllers — joints must settle without oscillation growth |
| Power Systems | Grid frequency stability — small disturbances must damp out |
| Biomedical | Pacemaker circuits — must respond but never oscillate uncontrollably |
| Automotive | Active suspension — must absorb road disturbances without diverging |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** The Routh-Hurwitz criterion is a *frequency-domain stability test*. It tells us **whether** the system is stable, **how many** unstable poles exist, and the **critical gain** at which stability is lost — without ever computing the actual pole values. This is extremely valuable for design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Differential Equation to Characteristic Polynomial

Consider a general $n$-th order linear constant-coefficient differential equation governing a system:

$$a_n \frac{d^n y}{dt^n} + a_{n-1} \frac{d^{n-1} y}{dt^{n-1}} + \cdots + a_1 \frac{dy}{dt} + a_0 y = b_m \frac{d^m r}{dt^m} + \cdots + b_0 r$$

**Step 1 — Take Laplace Transform (zero initial conditions):**

$$\big[a_n s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1 s + a_0\big] Y(s) = \big[b_m s^m + \cdots + b_0\big] R(s)$$

**Step 2 — Form the Transfer Function:**

$$T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{b_m s^m + b_{m-1} s^{m-1} + \cdots + b_0}{a_n s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1 s + a_0}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Characteristic Polynomial (denominator):**

$$q(s) = a_n s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1 s + a_0$$

**Step 4 — Set $q(s) = 0$ to obtain the characteristic equation.** Its roots are the closed-loop poles.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stability Boundary for a 2nd-Order System

Let the characteristic equation be:

$$a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0 = 0$$

**Step 1 — Solve using the quadratic formula:**

$$s = \frac{-a_1 \pm \sqrt{a_1^2 - 4 a_2 a_0}}{2 a_2}$$

**Step 2 — Extract the real part of each root:**

$$\text{Re}(s) = -\frac{a_1}{2 a_2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply stability condition** $\text{Re}(s) < 0$. Assuming $a_2 > 0$ (otherwise multiply equation by $-1$):

$$-\frac{a_1}{2 a_2} < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_1 > 0$$

**Step 4 — The discriminant must be real or complex** (no constraint on sign for stability). But $a_0 > 0$ ensures no zero root.

**Final 2nd-Order Stability Condition:**

$$a_2 > 0, \quad a_1 > 0, \quad a_0 > 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This is the simplest, most-tested condition in KTU. For any 2nd-order system (e.g., $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$), stability is equivalent to $\zeta > 0$ and $\omega_n > 0$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Routh-Hurwitz Criterion (General Procedure)

Consider an $n$-th order characteristic polynomial:

$$q(s) = a_n s^n + a_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1 s + a_0 = 0$$

with $a_n > 0$. The **Routh Array** is constructed as follows:

$$\begin{aligned}
s^n & : & a_n & & a_{n-2} & & a_{n-4} & & \cdots \\
s^{n-1} & : & a_{n-1} & & a_{n-3} & & a_{n-5} & & \cdots \\
s^{n-2} & : & b_1 & & b_2 & & b_3 & & \cdots \\
s^{n-3} & : & c_1 & & c_2 & & c_3 & & \cdots \\
\vdots & & \vdots & & & & & & \\
s^0 & : & \cdot & & & & & &
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the elements of row $s^{n-2}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= \frac{a_{n-1} \cdot a_{n-2} - a_n \cdot a_{n-3}}{a_{n-1}} \\
b_2 &= \frac{a_{n-1} \cdot a_{n-4} - a_n \cdot a_{n-5}}{a_{n-1}} \\
b_3 &= \frac{a_{n-1} \cdot a_{n-6} - a_n \cdot a_{n-7}}{a_{n-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the elements of row $s^{n-3}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
c_1 &= \frac{b_1 \cdot a_{n-3} - a_{n-1} \cdot b_2}{b_1} \\
c_2 &= \frac{b_1 \cdot a_{n-5} - a_{n-1} \cdot b_3}{b_1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Continue this pattern** until the first column is exhausted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Routh-Hurwitz Stability Theorem:** A system is stable **if and only if all entries in the first column of the Routh array have the same sign** (all positive when $a_n > 0$). The number of sign changes in the first column equals the number of poles in the right half of the s-plane.

## 3.4 Special Cases in Routh Array

| Special Case | What Happens | Remedy |
|--------------|-------------|--------|
| **Zero in first column (other than last row)** | Auxiliary equation appears; symmetric roots | Form auxiliary eqn from row above, differentiate it |
| **Entire row of zeros** | Symmetric roots about origin | Replace zero row with coefficients of $\frac{d}{ds}\text{(auxiliary eqn)}$ |
| **Row with leading $\epsilon \to 0^+$** | Sign change ambiguity | Substitute $\epsilon = $ small positive number, check sign |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Routh-Hurwitz)

**Problem:** Determine the stability of the system with characteristic equation:

$$s^4 + 2 s^3 + 3 s^2 + 4 s + 5 = 0$$

**Step 1 — Construct the Routh Array header:**

$$\begin{aligned}
s^4 & : & 1 & & 3 & & 5 \\
s^3 & : & 2 & & 4 & & 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute row $s^2$:**

$$b_1 = \frac{(2)(3) - (1)(4)}{2} = \frac{6 - 4}{2} = 1$$

$$b_2 = \frac{(2)(5) - (1)(0)}{2} = \frac{10}{2} = 5$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad s^2 : \quad 1 \quad 5$$

**Step 3 — Compute row $s^1$:**

$$c_1 = \frac{(1)(4) - (2)(5)}{1} = 4 - 10 = -6$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad s^1 : \quad -6$$

**Step 4 — Compute row $s^0$:**

$$d_1 = \frac{(-6)(5) - (1)(0)}{-6} = \frac{-30}{-6} = 5$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad s^0 : \quad 5$$

**Step 5 — Complete Routh Array:**

$$\begin{aligned}
s^4 & : & 1 & & 3 & & 5 \\
s^3 & : & 2 & & 4 & & 0 \\
s^2 & : & 1 & & 5 & & 0 \\
s^1 & : & -6 & & 0 & & 0 \\
s^0 & : & 5 & & 0 & & 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Examine First Column:** $1, 2, 1, -6, 5$

**Sign changes:** $+ \to -$ (at $-6$), $- \to +$ (at $5$). **Two sign changes.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The system has **2 poles in the right half of the s-plane** and is therefore **UNSTABLE**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stability Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: System Transfer Function T of s"] --> B["Form Closed Loop TF: T s equals G of s divided by 1 plus G of s times H of s"]
    B --> C["Extract Characteristic Equation: 1 plus G of s times H of s equals 0"]
    C --> D{"Does polynomial factor easily?"}
    D -- YES --> E["Solve for poles p1, p2, ... pn directly"]
    D -- NO --> F["Apply Routh Hurwitz Array Procedure"]
    E --> G{"All Re of pi less than 0?"}
    F --> H["Build Routh Array and inspect first column"]
    H --> I{"All first column entries same sign?"}
    I -- YES --> J["VERDICT: ASYMPTOTICALLY STABLE"]
    I -- NO --> K["Count number of sign changes in first column"]
    K --> L["Number of sign changes equals number of RHP poles"]
    G -- YES --> J
    G -- NO --> M{"Any simple poles on imaginary axis only?"}
    M -- YES --> N["VERDICT: MARGINALLY STABLE"]
    M -- NO --> O["VERDICT: UNSTABLE"]
    L --> O
```

## 4.2 S-Plane Pole Location Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LHP["LEFT HALF PLANE: Re of s less than 0"]
        LHP1["Real pole: Decaying exponential"]
        LHP2["Complex pair: Damped oscillation"]
    end
    subgraph IAX["IMAGINARY AXIS: Re of s equals 0"]
        IAX1["Simple pair on j omega: Sustained oscillation"]
        IAX2["Repeated poles on j omega: UNSTABLE response t sine omega t"]
    end
    subgraph RHP["RIGHT HALF PLANE: Re of s greater than 0"]
        RHP1["Real pole: Growing exponential"]
        RHP2["Complex pair: Growing oscillation"]
    end
    LHP1 --> OUT1["STABLE SYSTEM"]
    LHP2 --> OUT1
    IAX1 --> OUT2["MARGINALLY STABLE SYSTEM"]
    IAX2 --> OUT3["UNSTABLE SYSTEM"]
    RHP1 --> OUT3
    RHP2 --> OUT3
```

## 4.3 Routh Array Construction Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Input: Characteristic Polynomial q of s of order n"] --> S2["Row s to power n: coefficients a_n, a_n-2, a_n-4, ..."]
    S2 --> S3["Row s to power n-1: coefficients a_n-1, a_n-3, a_n-5, ..."]
    S3 --> S4["Compute next row using determinant formula"]
    S4 --> S5{"Entire row becomes zero?"}
    S5 -- YES --> S6["Form Auxiliary Equation from row above"]
    S6 --> S7["Differentiate Auxiliary Equation and replace zero row"]
    S7 --> S4
    S5 -- NO --> S8{"Zero in first column only?"}
    S8 -- YES --> S9["Replace zero with small epsilon and proceed"]
    S9 --> S4
    S8 -- NO --> S10["Continue until s to power 0 row is reached"]
    S10 --> S11["Inspect first column for sign changes"]
    S11 --> S12["Number of sign changes equals number of RHP poles"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define BIBO stability. State the condition on poles for a linear time-invariant system to be BIBO stable.**

**Model Answer:**

BIBO (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) stability means that for every bounded input signal $r(t)$ with $\vert r(t) \vert \leq M$ for all $t \geq 0$, the corresponding output $y(t)$ also remains bounded.

A linear time-invariant system is BIBO stable **if and only if all the poles of its transfer function lie strictly in the left half of the s-plane**, i.e., the real part of every pole is strictly negative: $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$ for all $i$.

> [!NOTE]
> The keyword **"strictly"** is mandatory in the KTU answer — saying "in LHP" alone is acceptable, but "strictly in LHP" is the gold-standard phrasing for full marks.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between asymptotic stability and marginal stability with respect to pole locations in the s-plane.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Asymptotic Stability | Marginal Stability |
|---------|---------------------|--------------------|
| **Pole location** | All poles strictly in LHP ($\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$) | Simple poles on $j\omega$-axis, no RHP poles |
| **Natural response** | Decays to zero as $t \to \infty$ | Sustained oscillation of constant amplitude |
| **Example poles** | $s = -2,\; s = -1 \pm j3$ | $s = \pm j5$ |
| **Stability class** | Practically useful, fully stable | Theoretically stable but impractical |
| **Behavior under perturbation** | Returns to equilibrium | Does not return; oscillates forever |

---

## 📕 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** *Choose either Question A or Question B. Each has sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each.*

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** The open-loop transfer function of a unity feedback system is given by:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$$

Using the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion**, determine the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A unity feedback system has the open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{10}{s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s}$$

Determine the closed-loop stability. Comment on the location of the poles. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Step 1 — Write the characteristic equation** $1 + G(s) = 0$:

$$1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Multiply through** by $s(s+2)(s+4)$:

$$s(s+2)(s+4) + K = 0$$

**Step 3 — Expand** the polynomial:

$$\begin{aligned}
s(s+2)(s+4) &= s(s^2 + 6s + 8) \\
&= s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s
\end{aligned}$$

So the characteristic equation becomes:

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$$

**Step 4 — Construct the Routh Array:**

$$\begin{aligned}
s^3 & : & 1 & & 8 \\
s^2 & : & 6 & & K \\
s^1 & : & b_1 & & 0 \\
s^0 & : & c_1 & & 0
\end{aligned}$$

where:

$$\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= \frac{(6)(8) - (1)(K)}{6} = \frac{48 - K}{6} \\
c_1 &= \frac{b_1 \cdot K - 6 \cdot 0}{b_1} = K
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the stability condition** (all first column entries positive):

- $1 > 0$ ✓ (always)
- $6 > 0$ ✓ (always)
- $\dfrac{48 - K}{6} > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K < 48$
- $K > 0$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{0 < K < 48}$$

The system is stable for gain values between **0 and 48**. At $K = 48$, the system is marginally stable (sustained oscillation).

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Step 1 — Write the characteristic equation** for unity feedback:

$$1 + \frac{10}{s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Multiply through:**

$$s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s + 10 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Routh Array:**

$$\begin{aligned}
s^3 & : & 1 & & 2 \\
s^2 & : & 3 & & 10 \\
s^1 & : & b_1 & & 0 \\
s^0 & : & c_1 & & 0
\end{aligned}$$

where:

$$b_1 = \frac{(3)(2) - (1)(10)}{3} = \frac{6 - 10}{3} = \frac{-4}{3}$$

$$c_1 = \frac{b_1 \cdot 10 - 3 \cdot 0}{b_1} = 10$$

**Step 4 — Examine the first column:** $1, \; 3, \; -\tfrac{4}{3}, \; 10$

**Step 5 — Count sign changes:** $+ \to + \to - \to +$. There are **two sign changes**.

**Conclusion:** The system has **2 poles in the right half of the s-plane**, hence **UNSTABLE**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students stop at $b_1 < 0$ and declare "unstable" without **counting the exact number of sign changes**. Marks are awarded specifically for stating **"two sign changes ⇒ two RHP poles"**. Always count precisely.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{K(s+1)}{s(s-1)(s+4)}$$

Find the range of $K$ for closed-loop stability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the s-plane, explain how the location of poles determines the stability and natural response of a control system. Provide at least four distinct pole-location scenarios. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation:** $1 + G(s) = 0$:

$$1 + \frac{K(s+1)}{s(s-1)(s+4)} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Multiply through:**

$$s(s-1)(s+4) + K(s+1) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Expand** $s(s-1)(s+4)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
s(s-1)(s+4) &= s[(s-1)(s+4)] \\
&= s[s^2 + 3s - 4] \\
&= s^3 + 3s^2 - 4s
\end{aligned}$$

So the characteristic equation becomes:

$$s^3 + 3s^2 - 4s + K(s+1) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Collect terms by power of $s$:**

$$s^3 + 3s^2 + (-4 + K)s + K = 0$$

$$s^3 + 3s^2 + (K - 4)s + K = 0$$

**Step 5 — Build the Routh Array:**

$$\begin{aligned}
s^3 & : & 1 & & (K - 4) \\
s^2 & : & 3 & & K \\
s^1 & : & b_1 & & 0 \\
s^0 & : & c_1 & & 0
\end{aligned}$$

where:

$$b_1 = \frac{(3)(K - 4) - (1)(K)}{3} = \frac{3K - 12 - K}{3} = \frac{2K - 12}{3}$$

$$c_1 = \frac{b_1 \cdot K - 3 \cdot 0}{b_1} = K$$

**Step 6 — Apply stability conditions:**

- $1 > 0$ ✓
- $3 > 0$ ✓
- $\dfrac{2K - 12}{3} > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K > 6$
- $K > 0$ ✓

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{K > 6}$$

For $K = 6$, the system is marginally stable; for $K < 6$, unstable.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

The s-plane's horizontal axis represents the real part ($\sigma$) and the vertical axis represents the imaginary part ($j\omega$) of each pole. The pole location uniquely determines the natural (unforced) time response of the system:

| # | Pole Location | Natural Response $y(t)$ | Stability |
|---|---------------|------------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Real pole $p = -\sigma$ on negative real axis ($\sigma > 0$) | Decaying exponential $y(t) = A e^{-\sigma t}$ | **Stable** |
| 2 | Complex conjugate pair $p = -\sigma \pm j\omega_d$ in LHP | Damped sinusoid $y(t) = A e^{-\sigma t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$ | **Stable** |
| 3 | Simple pair on imaginary axis $p = \pm j\omega$ | Sustained sinusoid $y(t) = A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | **Marginally Stable** |
| 4 | Real pole $p = +\sigma$ on positive real axis ($\sigma > 0$) | Growing exponential $y(t) = A e^{+\sigma t}$ | **Unstable** |
| 5 | Repeated poles on imaginary axis $p = \pm j\omega$ | Growing oscillation $y(t) = t \cdot A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | **Unstable** |
| 6 | Complex pair $p = +\sigma \pm j\omega_d$ in RHP | Growing sinusoid $y(t) = A e^{+\sigma t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$ | **Unstable** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The closer a pole is to the imaginary axis, the **slower** the response (longer settling time). The further left in the LHP, the **faster** the decay. This is the foundation of **root locus design** (covered in Module 4).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Confusing open-loop and closed-loop stability.** Open-loop poles tell us NOTHING about closed-loop behavior. Always form $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ first.
> 2. **Forgetting the auxiliary equation** in case of an all-zero row in the Routh array. This typically costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Writing "$K > 0$"** as the final answer for a stability range problem. KTU requires both **upper and lower bounds** (e.g., $0 < K < 48$).
> 4. **Sign-change counting errors.** When the first column has values like $+2, +1, -3, +5$, the sign changes are: $+ \to -$ (1 change) and $- \to +$ (1 change) — **total = 2 RHP poles**.
> 5. **Not stating the physical meaning** of poles (e.g., "two poles in RHP ⇒ unstable" without writing "natural response grows unbounded").
> 6. **Repeated poles on the $j\omega$-axis** are unstable — students often wrongly classify them as marginally stable.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **BIBO stability** ↔ All closed-loop poles strictly in **LHP** ($\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$).
- ✅ **Marginal stability** ↔ Simple poles on the **$j\omega$-axis**, no RHP poles.
- ✅ **Instability** ↔ Any pole in **RHP** OR **repeated poles on $j\omega$-axis**.
- ✅ The **characteristic equation** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ governs stability — **not** the open-loop denominator.
- ✅ **Routh-Hurwitz criterion**: Inspect the **first column** of the Routh array. All entries must have the **same sign** for stability.
- ✅ **Number of sign changes in the first column = number of RHP poles.**
- ✅ For a **2nd-order** system, $a_2, a_1, a_0 > 0$ is **necessary AND sufficient**.
- ✅ For a **3rd-order** system, all $a_i > 0$ is **necessary but NOT sufficient** — also need $a_2 a_1 > a_3 a_0$.
- ✅ **Special case 1**: Entire zero row → form auxiliary equation, differentiate.
- ✅ **Special case 2**: Single zero in first column → substitute $\epsilon \to 0^+$.
- ✅ The **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** is purely algebraic — it determines stability **without solving** the characteristic equation.
- ✅ **Root locus** (next module) is built directly on the principle that **closed-loop pole locations determine stability**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routh-Hurwitz Stability Criterion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT501 - LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routh-Hurwitz Stability Criterion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Absolute Stability via Routh-Hurwitz):**
> The **Routh-Hurwitz Stability Criterion** is an algebraic, purely analytical method used in classical control theory to determine the **absolute stability** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) continuous-time system by inspecting the coefficients of its closed-loop characteristic polynomial. It provides a necessary and sufficient condition for stability without requiring the explicit computation of the closed-loop poles (roots) of the characteristic equation.

For a closed-loop control system with characteristic equation:

$$\Delta(s) = a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + \dots + a_{n-1} s + a_n = 0$$

the system is **BIBO Stable** (bounded-input bounded-output stable) if and only if **all roots of $\Delta(s)$ lie strictly in the left-half of the complex s-plane**, which equivalently translates to two algebraic conditions:
- **(a) Necessary Condition:** All coefficients $a_i$ of the characteristic polynomial must be present, real, and strictly **positive** (assuming $a_0 > 0$).
- **(b) Sufficient Condition:** The **Routh Array** constructed from these coefficients must yield a strictly positive **first column**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Building Balance" Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Wobbly Table Test":**
> Imagine you are placing a tall, thin object (like a pen or a flag pole) on a flat table. You can determine whether the pole will stand upright without ever calculating the exact trajectory. You simply look at:
> 1. The **direction of push** (forces) acting on it.
> 2. The **table surface and the base** (boundary conditions).
> 3. The way small wiggles either **decay or amplify** (oscillations).
> If every push from every direction eventually settles, the pole is "stable."

In control systems, the Routh-Hurwitz criterion is the mathematical equivalent of this **"looking without measuring"** approach. Instead of finding the exact roots of a 5th-order polynomial (which is nearly impossible by hand), the criterion samples the polynomial's coefficients in a structured table and tells you: *"Yes, all disturbances will die out"* or *"No, something will blow up."*

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **System Stability Margin:** Quantified by examining **sign changes** in the first column of the Routh array. The number of sign changes equals the number of roots in the right-half s-plane (**RHP**).
- **Marginal/Critical Stability:** Occurs when the first column of the Routh array contains a **zero (0)** but no subsequent sign change — leading to imaginary-axis poles ($j\omega$-axis roots).
- **Critical Gain ($K_{max}$):** The maximum value of loop gain $K$ for which the system remains stable — a direct design output of the criterion.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Routh-Hurwitz procedure is best understood in three conceptual phases.

**Phase 1 — The Necessary Condition (Hurwitz Test, Quick Rejection)**

Before constructing the Routh array, we apply a sanity check:
- All polynomial coefficients must be **real**, **non-zero**, and of the **same sign** (usually positive).
- If **any coefficient is zero or negative**, the system is *immediately* suspected to be unstable or marginally stable. This alone does not prove instability (e.g., $s^3 + 0 \cdot s^2 + 2s + 8$ still requires the Routh array), but it is a red flag.

**Phase 2 — Construction of the Routh Array**

The Routh array is a triangular table with $n+1$ rows (where $n$ is the order of the polynomial). The first two rows are filled directly from the polynomial coefficients; subsequent rows are filled using a deterministic determinant-based rule.

**Phase 3 — Interpretation of the First Column**

- **All entries in the first column are positive $\Rightarrow$ Stable system** (all roots in LHP).
- **Any sign change in the first column $\Rightarrow$ Unstable system** (roots in RHP exist).
- **A zero in the first column $\Rightarrow$ Special cases** handled by:
  - Case A: Replace zero with a small $\epsilon$ and take the limit.
  - Case B: Auxiliary polynomial method (when an entire row becomes zero).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — Routh Array Construction Rules**

Given: $a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + a_3 s^{n-3} + \dots + a_n = 0$

| Row Label | Column 1 | Column 2 | Column 3 | ... |
|:---------:|:--------:|:--------:|:--------:|:---:|
| $s^n$     | $a_0$    | $a_2$    | $a_4$    | $\dots$ |
| $s^{n-1}$ | $a_1$    | $a_3$    | $a_5$    | $\dots$ |
| $s^{n-2}$ | $b_1$    | $b_2$    | $b_3$    | $\dots$ |
| $s^{n-3}$ | $c_1$    | $c_2$    | $c_3$    | $\dots$ |
| $\vdots$  | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $s^0$     | $a_n$    | —        | —        | — |

**Determinant-based row computation rule:**

$$b_1 = -\frac{1}{a_1}\begin{vmatrix} a_0 & a_2 \\ a_1 & a_3 \end{vmatrix} = \frac{a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3}{a_1}$$

$$b_2 = -\frac{1}{a_1}\begin{vmatrix} a_0 & a_4 \\ a_1 & a_5 \end{vmatrix} = \frac{a_1 a_4 - a_0 a_5}{a_1}$$

$$c_1 = -\frac{1}{b_1}\begin{vmatrix} a_1 & a_3 \\ b_1 & b_2 \end{vmatrix} = \frac{b_1 a_3 - a_1 b_2}{b_1}$$

### 2.3 Special Cases — The Two Critical Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Case 1: Zero in the First Column (Only)**
> Replace the zero with a tiny positive number $\epsilon$ ($0 < \epsilon \ll 1$), complete the array, and then take the limit as $\epsilon \to 0^+$. This reveals sign changes (or absence thereof) in the limit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Case 2: Entire Row of Zeros**
> This signals **symmetrically placed roots** about the origin (e.g., $\pm \sigma$, $\pm j\omega$). Form the **auxiliary polynomial** $A(s)$ from the row *above* the zero row (using only even powers of $s$). Differentiate it to obtain $dA/ds$, and use its coefficients to replace the zero row. Then continue normally.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Servo motor design:** Determine the maximum proportional gain $K_p$ before oscillations grow unbounded.
- **Aircraft autopilot tuning:** Ensures pitch/yaw damping is positive for all flight envelopes.
- **Power system stabilizer (PSS) design:** Prevents low-frequency electromechanical oscillations from growing.
- **Op-amp circuit compensation:** Validates that an active filter's closed-loop poles stay in the LHP.
- **Biomedical systems:** Pacemaker lead-response stability, infusion pump control loops.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 General Formula Derivation of Array Entries

We begin with the characteristic polynomial:

$$a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + a_3 s^{n-3} + a_4 s^{n-4} + \dots + a_n = 0$$

**Step 1 — Setting up the first two rows directly from coefficients:**

$$\text{Row } s^n : \quad a_0 \quad a_2 \quad a_4 \quad a_6 \quad \dots$$

$$\text{Row } s^{n-1} : \quad a_1 \quad a_3 \quad a_5 \quad a_7 \quad \dots$$

This is a direct transcription: the $s^n$ row takes coefficients at even indices, and the $s^{n-1}$ row takes coefficients at odd indices.

**Step 2 — Deriving the third row ($s^{n-2}$) using a determinant:**

Each new entry is computed as the negative determinant formed by the two rows above, divided by the pivot (the first element of the row immediately above). For the first element of the $s^{n-2}$ row:

$$b_1 = \frac{1}{a_1} \begin{vmatrix} a_0 & a_2 \\ a_1 & a_3 \end{vmatrix}_{\text{with sign convention}} = \frac{(a_1)(a_2) - (a_0)(a_3)}{a_1}$$

**Step 3 — Generalizing to all subsequent rows:**

For the $k$-th row (where $k \geq 3$), each element $r_k[j]$ is given by:

$$r_k[j] = \frac{r_{k-1}[0] \cdot r_{k-2}[j+1] - r_{k-2}[0] \cdot r_{k-1}[j+1]}{r_{k-1}[0]}$$

where $r_{k-1}[0]$ is the pivot (first column of the row above) and the index $j$ starts from $0$.

**Step 4 — The sign-change interpretation (Routh's theorem):**

Routh proved that the number of sign changes in the first column of the constructed array equals **exactly the number of roots of the polynomial lying in the right-half s-plane**. This transforms a root-finding problem into a sign-counting problem.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — A Stable Third-Order System (Full Derivation)

**Problem:** Determine the stability of the system with characteristic equation:

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6 = 0$$

**Step 1 — Verify the necessary condition:**
All coefficients $\{1, 6, 11, 6\}$ are real, non-zero, and positive. ✓ Necessary condition satisfied.

**Step 2 — Build the Routh array:**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| $s^3$ | $1$  | $11$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $6$  | $6$  | $0$ |
| $s^1$ | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $c_1$ | $0$  | $0$ |

**Step 3 — Compute $b_1$ and $b_2$ using the determinant rule:**

$$b_1 = \frac{(6)(11) - (1)(6)}{6} = \frac{66 - 6}{6} = \frac{60}{6} = 10$$

$$b_2 = \frac{(6)(0) - (1)(0)}{6} = \frac{0 - 0}{6} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Compute $c_1$:**

$$c_1 = \frac{(10)(6) - (6)(0)}{10} = \frac{60 - 0}{10} = 6$$

**Step 5 — Completed Routh array:**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|
| $s^3$ | $\mathbf{1}$  | $11$ |
| $s^2$ | $\mathbf{6}$  | $6$  |
| $s^1$ | $\mathbf{10}$ | $0$  |
| $s^0$ | $\mathbf{6}$  | $0$  |

**Step 6 — Inspect the first column:** $\{1, 6, 10, 6\}$ — all positive, **zero sign changes**.

**Conclusion:** The system is **stable**. (Verification: roots are $s = -1, -2, -3$, all in LHP.) ✓

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Critical Gain Determination (14-Mark Type)

**Problem:** For the unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$$

determine:
- (a) The Routh array and stability range for $K$.
- (b) The value of $K$ at which the system becomes marginally stable, and the corresponding frequency of oscillation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Form the closed-loop characteristic equation:**

$$1 + G(s) = 0 \implies 1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0$$

$$s(s+2)(s+4) + K = 0$$

Expanding the cubic product:

$$s(s^2 + 6s + 8) + K = 0$$

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$$

**Step 2 — Build the Routh array (with symbolic $K$):**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|
| $s^3$ | $1$  | $8$  |
| $s^2$ | $6$  | $K$  |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{6 \cdot 8 - 1 \cdot K}{6}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K$  | $0$  |

**Step 3 — Simplify the $s^1$ row entry:**

$$\frac{48 - K}{6} = \frac{48 - K}{6}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the stability condition (all first-column entries > 0):**

From $s^2$ row: $6 > 0$ ✓ (automatically satisfied)
From $s^1$ row: $\dfrac{48 - K}{6} > 0 \implies K < 48$
From $s^0$ row: $K > 0$

**Combined stability range:**

$$0 < K < 48$$

**Step 5 — Marginal stability condition (set $s^1$ row entry to zero):**

$$\frac{48 - K}{6} = 0 \implies K_{critical} = 48$$

**Step 6 — Find frequency of oscillation from the auxiliary polynomial of the $s^2$ row:**

At $K = 48$, the array becomes:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|
| $s^3$ | $1$  | $8$  |
| $s^2$ | $6$  | $48$ |
| $s^1$ | $0$  | $0$  |
| $s^0$ | $48$ | $0$  |

The auxiliary polynomial is taken from the $s^2$ row:

$$A(s) = 6s^2 + 48 = 0$$

$$6s^2 = -48 \implies s^2 = -8 \implies s = \pm j\sqrt{8} = \pm j 2\sqrt{2}$$

**Frequency of sustained oscillation:**

$$\omega = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Stability range: $0 < K < 48$
- Critical gain: $K_{cr} = 48$
- Oscillation frequency: $\omega_{cr} = 2\sqrt{2}$ rad/s

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Special Case: Zero in First Column (Limit Method)

**Problem:** Test stability of $s^3 + 2s^2 + s + 2 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Build the Routh array:**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| $s^2$ | $2$ | $2$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{2(1) - 1(2)}{2} = 0$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $2$ | $0$ |

A zero appears in the $s^1$ row. **Case 1 applies.**

**Step 2 — Replace zero with $\epsilon$:**

$$s^1 \text{ row entry} = \frac{2 \cdot 1 - 1 \cdot 2}{2} = 0 \quad \to \quad \epsilon$$

**Step 3 — Recompute the $s^0$ row:**

The $s^0$ row entry becomes:

$$\frac{\epsilon \cdot 2 - 2 \cdot 0}{\epsilon} = \frac{2\epsilon}{\epsilon} = 2$$

**Step 4 — First column with $\epsilon$:** $\{1, 2, \epsilon, 2\}$

As $\epsilon \to 0^+$, the first column is $\{1, 2, 0^+, 2\}$ — no sign change (positive $\to$ positive).

**Step 5 — Auxiliary polynomial check:**
Since the entire $s^1$ row became zero, form $A(s)$ from $s^2$ row:

$$A(s) = 2s^2 + 2 = 0 \implies s^2 = -1 \implies s = \pm j$$

These are roots on the $j\omega$-axis → **marginally stable** (sustained oscillation, not asymptotically stable).

**Conclusion:** System is **marginally stable** (not asymptotically stable).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Routh-Hurwitz Procedure

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the complete decision logic of the Routh-Hurwitz procedure, from input polynomial to final stability verdict.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Characteristic Polynomial inputA s to the n plus a1 s to the n minus 1 plus ... plus an equals 0] --> B[Step 1: Check Necessary Condition]
    B --> B1{All coefficients real, nonzero, same sign?}
    B1 -->|No| B2[Reject immediately: System is unstable or marginally unstable]
    B1 -->|Yes| C[Step 2: Construct Routh Array]
    C --> D[Fill first two rows from coefficients]
    D --> E[Compute subsequent rows via determinant rule]
    E --> F[Step 3: Inspect First Column]
    F --> G{Any zero in first column?}
    G -->|No| H[All positive first column?]
    G -->|Yes| I[Special Case Detected]
    I --> I1{Entire row becomes zero?}
    I1 -->|Yes| I2[Form Auxiliary Polynomial A of s]
    I2 --> I3[Differentiate: dA by ds]
    I3 --> I4[Replace zero row with dA by ds coefficients]
    I4 --> F
    I1 -->|No| I5[Replace zero with epsilon, take limit epsilon to 0 plus]
    I5 --> F
    H -->|Yes| H1[VERDICT: Asymptotically STABLE]
    H -->|No| H2[Count sign changes in first column]
    H2 --> H3[Number of sign changes equals number of RHP roots]
    H3 --> H4[VERDICT: UNSTABLE if any sign change exists]
    H1 --> Z[End]
    H4 --> Z
    B2 --> Z
    I2 --> Z2[Step 4: Solve A of s equals 0 for imaginary axis roots]
    Z2 --> Z3[VERDICT: Marginally stable if roots on j omega axis only]
    Z3 --> Z
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Closed-Loop System Under Stability Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[R input reference signal] --> Summ[Summer node]
    Summ -->|error signal E of s| Ctrl[Controller Gc of s]
    Ctrl --> Plant[Plant G of s]
    Plant --> Y[Y output]
    Y --> Feedback[Unity Feedback H of s equals 1]
    Feedback --> Summ
    Plant -.->|roots of 1 plus GcG of s equals 0| CharEq[Characteristic Equation D of s]
    CharEq --> Routh[Routh Hurwitz Test]
    Routh --> Verdict[Stability Verdict]
```

### 4.3 Auxiliary Polynomial Differentiation — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Entire Row of Zeros Detected] --> Q[Identify row above: say s to the power m]
    Q --> R[Form Auxiliary Polynomial A of s using that row]
    R --> R1[A of s equals a s to the m plus b s to the m minus 2 plus c s to the m minus 4 plus ...]
    R1 --> S[Differentiate A of s with respect to s]
    S --> S1[dA by ds equals m times a times s to the m minus 1 plus b times m minus 2 times s to the m minus 3 plus ...]
    S1 --> T[Substitute dA by ds coefficients into the zero row]
    T --> U[Continue Routh Array construction downward]
    U --> V[Solve A of s equals 0 to find imaginary axis pole pairs]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 3 Marks]**

**Q1.** State the **Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion**. What are the necessary conditions for a polynomial to be Hurwitz?

**Model Answer:**
The Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion is a mathematical test that determines whether all roots of the characteristic equation of a linear closed-loop system have negative real parts (i.e., lie in the left-half of the s-plane), thereby ensuring asymptotic stability.

The **necessary (but not sufficient) conditions** are:
1. All coefficients of the characteristic polynomial must be **real**.
2. All coefficients must be **present** (no missing terms).
3. All coefficients must have the **same sign** (typically positive after factoring out $-1$ if needed).

Without these conditions being met, stability is impossible. However, satisfying them is **not enough** — the Routh array must also yield a positive first column for the system to be confirmed stable.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 3 Marks]**

**Q2.** What do you understand by the term **"auxiliary polynomial"** in the Routh array construction? When is it used?

**Model Answer:**
An **auxiliary polynomial** $A(s)$ is a polynomial formed from the coefficients of the row **immediately above** an all-zero row in the Routh array. It is used **only when an entire row of the Routh array becomes zero**, which signals the presence of symmetrically placed roots about the origin (e.g., pairs like $\pm \sigma$ or $\pm j\omega$).

The auxiliary polynomial contains **only even powers of $s$** (because of the symmetry), and its derivative $dA/ds$ is then used to replace the zero row, allowing the array construction to proceed.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

#### **Question A**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion, determine the stability of the system whose characteristic equation is:

$$s^4 + 2s^3 + 8s^2 + 4s + 3 = 0$$

**(b)** [7 Marks] Comment on the location of the closed-loop poles and the type of stability obtained.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction of the Routh Array**

The characteristic polynomial is:

$$s^4 + 2s^3 + 8s^2 + 4s + 3 = 0$$

Coefficients: $a_0 = 1, a_1 = 2, a_2 = 8, a_3 = 4, a_4 = 3$.

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| $s^4$ | $1$ | $8$ | $3$ |
| $s^3$ | $2$ | $4$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $\dfrac{2 \cdot 8 - 1 \cdot 4}{2} = 6$ | $\dfrac{2 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 0}{2} = 3$ | $0$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{6 \cdot 4 - 2 \cdot 3}{6} = \dfrac{18}{6} = 3$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $3$ | $0$ | $0$ |

**First column:** $\{1, 2, 6, 3, 3\}$ — **all positive, zero sign changes**.

**(b) Stability Verdict**

The first column contains no sign changes, so **no roots exist in the right-half s-plane**. Therefore, the system is **asymptotically stable** (assuming no zero-row special case triggered, which is verified here since the array terminated normally).

**Pole location:** All four closed-loop poles lie strictly in the **left-half s-plane**, and their exact values can be obtained numerically as approximately $s \approx -0.434, -0.504, -1.117 \pm j1.798$.

---

#### **Question B**

**(a)** [7 Marks] For the unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s-1)(s+3)}$$

determine the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable, using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Find the value of $K$ for which the system oscillates, and determine the frequency of oscillation.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stability Range via Routh-Hurwitz**

**Step 1 — Form the closed-loop characteristic equation:**

$$1 + G(s) = 0 \implies s(s-1)(s+3) + K(s+2) = 0$$

Expanding $s(s-1)(s+3) = s(s^2 + 2s - 3) = s^3 + 2s^2 - 3s$:

$$s^3 + 2s^2 - 3s + Ks + 2K = 0$$

$$s^3 + 2s^2 + (K - 3)s + 2K = 0$$

**Step 2 — Build the Routh array:**

Coefficients: $a_0 = 1, a_1 = 2, a_2 = (K-3), a_3 = 2K$.

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $K - 3$ |
| $s^2$ | $2$ | $2K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{2(K-3) - 1 \cdot 2K}{2} = \dfrac{2K - 6 - 2K}{2} = -3$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $2K$ | $0$ |

**Step 3 — Stability analysis on first column:** $\{1, 2, -3, 2K\}$

The third entry is $-3 < 0$ **regardless of $K$** — this means the first column will always contain a sign change (from $+2$ to $-3$ to either positive or negative depending on $K$).

**Verdict:** The system is **unstable for all values of $K$**. The pole at $s = +1$ in the open-loop transfer function is inside the RHP, and no amount of $K$ can pull it back into the LHP. **No stability range exists.**

**(b) Oscillation Analysis**

Since the system never becomes stable, there is **no value of $K$ for which the system enters sustained oscillation through Routh-derived marginal stability**. The auxiliary polynomial method does not apply here because the array does not develop a zero row for any specific $K$ — the instability is structural, originating from the open-loop RHP pole at $s = +1$.

**Conclusion:** The presence of an open-loop pole in the right-half s-plane guarantees closed-loop instability, demonstrating that the Routh-Hurwitz criterion correctly identifies this structural limitation of the system.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion:** Many students forget to flip the sign of the determinant, leading to systematically wrong array entries. The correct formula has $(r_{k-1}[0] \cdot r_{k-2}[j+1] - r_{k-2}[0] \cdot r_{k-1}[j+1])$ in the numerator.
> 2. **Misinterpretation of "zero in first column":** When a single zero appears (not an entire row), students often jump to the auxiliary polynomial — **wrong**. Use the auxiliary polynomial only when an **entire row** is zero.
> 3. **Forgetting the $K > 0$ condition:** In gain-stability problems, the lower bound $K > 0$ is often missed. The Routh array gives an upper bound; the physical positivity of the gain gives the lower bound.
> 4. **Counting sign changes incorrectly:** Each transition from positive-to-negative OR negative-to-positive counts as **one** sign change, not two. The total sign changes equal the number of RHP roots.
> 5. **Failing to verify the necessary condition first:** Always check that all coefficients are present and of the same sign **before** constructing the array. Skipping this costs 1–2 easy marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Routh-Hurwitz Stability Criterion**

- ✅ The Routh-Hurwitz criterion determines **absolute stability** by examining polynomial coefficients, **without finding the actual roots**.
- ✅ A continuous-time LTI system is **asymptotically stable** $\iff$ **all roots of the characteristic equation lie in the open left-half s-plane** (Re$(s) < 0$).
- ✅ **Necessary condition:** All coefficients of the characteristic polynomial must be **real, present, and of the same sign**.
- ✅ **Routh array** has $n+1$ rows for an $n$-th order polynomial. First two rows are filled from coefficients; remaining rows are filled using the **determinant-based pivot rule**.
- ✅ The first column of the Routh array is the **diagnostic column** — it must be **entirely positive** for stability.
- ✅ **Number of sign changes** in the first column = **number of roots in the right-half s-plane** (Routh's theorem).
- ✅ **Special Case 1 (zero in first column only):** Replace zero with $\epsilon \to 0^+$ and inspect the limit.
- ✅ **Special Case 2 (entire row becomes zero):** Form the **auxiliary polynomial** from the row above (using even powers of $s$), differentiate it, and substitute the derivative's coefficients into the zero row.
- ✅ **Marginal stability** (oscillation) occurs when the first column contains a zero **with no subsequent sign change** — these correspond to pure imaginary roots $s = \pm j\omega$.
- ✅ **Critical gain $K_{max}$** is found by setting the first column entry that contains $K$ to zero; the **frequency of oscillation** is found by solving the auxiliary polynomial $A(s) = 0$.
- ✅ The criterion applies **only to polynomials with real coefficients** and **continuous-time systems** (for discrete-time, the Jury or bilinear transform to $w$-plane is required).
- ✅ An open-loop pole in the RHP **cannot be stabilized** by gain adjustment alone — structural design changes are required.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Root Locus Method - Construction of Root Locus
Effect of Poles and Zeros and Their Location on the Root Locus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT501 - LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Poles and Zeros and Their Location on the Root Locus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Root Locus** technique, the closed-loop poles of a system are obtained as the roots of the characteristic equation:

$$1 + KG(s)H(s) = 0$$

The **effect of poles and zeros on the root locus** describes how the geometric trajectory (the locus) of closed-loop poles shifts, bends, or distorts in the *s*-plane as the gain $K$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$, based on the **location of open-loop poles (OLP)** and **open-loop zeros (OLZ)** of $G(s)H(s)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** A **pole** is a value of $s$ that makes the transfer function magnitude infinite (denominator zero). A **zero** is a value of $s$ that makes the transfer function magnitude zero (numerator zero). The root locus **starts** at the open-loop poles ($K=0$) and **terminates** at the open-loop zeros ($K=\infty$), with the number of branches equal to the number of poles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are standing in a valley between two mountains. The mountains represent **poles** (unstable regions), and the deep cave openings on the mountainsides represent **zeros** (attractive regions).

- **Poles** are like **magnets that repel** the path. If you place a magnet (pole) to the right of your starting point, the path (root locus) gets **pushed** toward the right, closer to instability.
- **Zeros** are like **magnets that attract** the path. If you place a magnet (zero) to the left, the path gets **pulled** toward the left, into a more stable region.

So, **adding a pole → root locus shifts rightward (less stable)**, and **adding a zero → root locus shifts leftward (more stable)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** In a real control system (e.g., a robotic arm or cruise control), adding an **integrator** introduces a pole at the origin, which increases the system type and improves steady-state accuracy but reduces relative stability. Adding a **derivative compensator** introduces a zero, which improves transient response and stability margins.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Points

- **Number of branches** of root locus = Number of open-loop poles = $P$
- **Number of asymptotes** = $P - Z$ (where $Z$ is the number of finite open-loop zeros)
- **Number of finite terminations** = $Z$ (root locus ends at the zeros)
- **Number of branches going to infinity** = $P - Z$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Always remember — *every branch of the root locus must start at a pole and end at either a finite zero or infinity*. The placement of poles and zeros entirely determines the shape of this trajectory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Pole-Zero Effects

The characteristic equation is:

$$1 + K \cdot \frac{N(s)}{D(s)} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D(s) + K \cdot N(s) = 0$$

The **angle condition** and **magnitude condition** governing the root locus are:

$$\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180° (2q+1), \quad q = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

$$\vert G(s)H(s) \vert = \frac{1}{K}$$

#### A. Effect of Adding a Pole (No Finite Zeros)

Let us analyze a simple system:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+2)}$$

Now add an additional pole at $s = -a$:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+2)(s+a)}$$

**Key effects:**

1. The number of branches increases by **1**.
2. An additional asymptote appears, rotating the centroid further **leftward**, but the asymptote angles change since the new pole affects the angle contribution.
3. The root locus branches are **pushed to the right** of their original positions (towards instability).
4. The breakaway point may shift.
5. The system becomes **less stable** for a given $K$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this happen?** When we add a pole, it contributes an additional $-180°$ phase shift to the angle condition. To compensate, the closed-loop pole must move to a location where the other poles/zeros contribute *more* negative angle. This usually means the root locus shifts toward the right half-plane (RHP), where the contribution from existing poles can be balanced.

#### B. Effect of Adding a Zero (No Finite Zeros)

Consider:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{(s+a)}{s(s+2)}$$

**Key effects:**

1. The number of finite terminations increases by **1** (a branch now ends at $s = -a$).
2. The number of asymptotes **decreases by 1** (if pole count exceeds zero count by 2, only one asymptote remains).
3. The root locus is **pulled to the left** (towards stability).
4. The breakaway point may shift.
5. The system becomes **more stable** and has **improved transient response**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the fundamental reason why **PD (Proportional-Derivative) controllers** and **lead compensators** are used in practice — they introduce a zero that "pulls" the root locus to the left, increasing damping and reducing overshoot, while a PI or lag compensator introduces a pole that improves steady-state error.

#### C. Effect of Pole-Zero Cancellation

If a pole and zero are placed very close to each other, they **approximately cancel**. The dominant dynamics are then governed by the remaining poles/zeros.

- **Pole-zero pair near origin** → significant effect on transient response.
- **Pole-zero pair far from origin** → minimal effect on root locus shape.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination). Note: All absolute value notations use $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid markdown table syntax conflicts.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Angle Condition | $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180°(2q+1)$ | Defines the locus path |
| 2 | Magnitude Condition | $K = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(s)H(s) \vert}$ | Defines $K$ at any point on locus |
| 3 | Number of Branches | Equal to $P$ (open-loop poles) | Each branch starts at a pole |
| 4 | Real-Axis Locus Rule | Locus exists where $\sum (\text{poles+zeros to the right})$ is **odd** | Determines which real-axis segments are part of root locus |
| 5 | Number of Asymptotes | $n = P - Z$ | Lines that the locus approaches as $K \to \infty$ |
| 6 | Asymptote Angle | $\phi_A = \dfrac{(2q+1)180°}{P-Z}, \quad q=0,1,\ldots,(P-Z-1)$ | Angle each asymptote makes with real axis |
| 7 | Centroid (Intersection) | $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum \text{Poles} - \sum \text{Zeros}}{P - Z}$ | Point on real axis where asymptotes meet |
| 8 | Breakaway Point | Roots of $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$ where $K = -\dfrac{1}{G(s)H(s)}$ | Point where two branches meet on real axis |
| 9 | Departure Angle from complex pole $p_1$ | $\phi_{d} = 180° + \sum \angle(\text{zeros} - p_1) - \sum \angle(\text{other poles} - p_1)$ | Angle at which locus leaves a complex pole |
| 10 | Arrival Angle at complex zero $z_1$ | $\phi_a = 180° - \sum \angle(\text{other zeros} - z_1) + \sum \angle(\text{poles} - z_1)$ | Angle at which locus enters a complex zero |
| 11 | Imaginary Axis Crossing | Use Routh-Hurwitz on characteristic polynomial | Point where locus crosses $j\omega$-axis |
| 12 | Effect of Adding Pole | Locus **shifts rightward** (less stable) | Pole contributes $-90°$ to phase |
| 13 | Effect of Adding Zero | Locus **shifts leftward** (more stable) | Zero contributes $+90°$ to phase |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Aerospace** | Aircraft autopilot design: zeros placed via lead compensation to improve damping of phugoid mode |
| **Robotics** | Manipulator joint control: zeros from derivative action reduce overshoot in trajectory tracking |
| **Process Control** | PID tuning: integrator adds pole at origin for zero steady-state error |
| **Automotive** | Active suspension: pole-zero placement determines ride comfort and road-holding |
| **Power Systems** | Excitation control of synchronous generators: lead-lag compensators reshape root locus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?** Understanding how poles and zeros reshape the root locus is essential for **compensator design** (Module 5 of PCMRT501). The compensator is essentially a designed transfer function whose poles and zeros are *deliberately placed* to move the root locus in a desired direction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Analysis: Effect of a Pole at $s = -a$

Consider a basic second-order system:

$$G_1(s)H(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+2)}$$

Poles: $s = 0, \, s = -2$. No finite zeros. Root locus is a straight line on the real axis between $-2$ and $0$, and two branches go to infinity along asymptotes at $\pm 90°$.

Now introduce an **additional pole** at $s = -a$ (where $a > 0$):

$$G_2(s)H(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+2)(s+a)}$$

**Derivation Step 1: Number of Asymptotes**

$$P = 3, \quad Z = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P - Z = 3$$

So there are **3 asymptotes**.

**Derivation Step 2: Asymptote Angles**

$$\phi_A = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{3}, \quad q = 0, 1, 2$$

$$\phi_A = 60°, \, 180°, \, 300° \quad (\text{or equivalently } -60°)$$

**Derivation Step 3: Centroid**

$$\sigma_A = \frac{(0) + (-2) + (-a) - 0}{3} = \frac{-(2+a)}{3}$$

**Derivation Step 4: Comparison with Original System**

For the original system ($a \to \infty$ removal of extra pole):

$$\sigma_{A,\text{original}} = \frac{0 + (-2)}{2} = -1$$

For the modified system with $a = 1$:

$$\sigma_{A,\text{new}} = \frac{-(2+1)}{3} = -1$$

For the modified system with $a = 0.5$ (pole closer to origin):

$$\sigma_{A,\text{new}} = \frac{-(2+0.5)}{3} = -0.833$$

**Conclusion:** As the new pole moves **closer to the origin** (smaller $a$), the centroid moves **rightward** (less negative). This confirms that adding a pole, especially close to the origin, **shifts the root locus toward the right half-plane** (destabilizing effect).

**Derivation Step 5: Asymptote Angle Comparison**

The original system had asymptotes at $\pm 90°$. The new system has asymptotes at $60°, 180°, -60°$. The new asymptote at $180°$ means one branch is now driven along the negative real axis, **pushing another branch into the right half-plane region** at the angle $60°$, reducing stability.

### 3.2 Detailed Analysis: Effect of a Zero at $s = -b$

Consider the same base system with an **added zero** at $s = -b$ (where $b > 0$):

$$G_3(s)H(s) = \frac{(s+b)}{s(s+2)}$$

**Derivation Step 1: Number of Asymptotes**

$$P = 2, \quad Z = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P - Z = 1$$

There is now only **1 asymptote**.

**Derivation Step 2: Asymptote Angle**

$$\phi_A = \frac{180°}{1} = 180°$$

This means the single asymptote lies on the **negative real axis** itself.

**Derivation Step 3: Centroid**

$$\sigma_A = \frac{(0) + (-2) - (-b)}{1} = b - 2$$

**Derivation Step 4: Effect of Varying $b$**

| Value of $b$ | $\sigma_A$ | Effect |
|:-------------:|:----------:|:-------|
| $b = 0$ | $-2$ | Zero at origin; zero cancels one pole |
| $b = 1$ | $-1$ | Centroid moves rightward compared to original |
| $b = 2$ | $0$ | Centroid at origin |
| $b = 3$ | $+1$ | Centroid in RHP — **potentially unstable** for high $K$ |
| $b = 5$ | $+3$ | Centroid deep in RHP |

**Conclusion:** If the zero is placed **far to the left** (large $b$), the centroid moves **rightward**, but the root locus becomes more **attracted to the zero**, which is in the left half-plane. The overall system stability depends on the balance.

For a zero placed between the poles (i.e., $-2 < -b < 0$, so $0 < b < 2$), the root locus shows a **circle-like shape** between the zero and poles, and the breakaway behavior is altered.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Proof: Why Poles Destabilize and Zeros Stabilize

**Phase Contribution Analysis:**

For a test point $s = \sigma + j\omega$ on the root locus, the angle condition is:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{Z} \angle(s - z_i) - \sum_{j=1}^{P} \angle(s - p_j) = \pm 180° (2q+1)$$

A **zero** at $z_i$ contributes $+\angle(s - z_i)$ to the LHS.
A **pole** at $p_j$ contributes $-\angle(s - p_j)$ to the LHS.

If we **add a new pole** at $p_{\text{new}}$, the LHS decreases by $\angle(s - p_{\text{new}})$. To restore the angle condition, the test point $s$ must move to a position where the **other** contributions become more positive, which means moving **closer to the new pole** (which is typically on the real axis or in the LHP). This causes the closed-loop poles to drift rightward, reducing stability.

Conversely, if we **add a new zero**, the LHS increases by $\angle(s - z_{\text{new}})$. To restore the angle condition, $s$ must move where the **other** contributions are more negative, i.e., closer to the new zero — pulling the root locus leftward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Rule for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Adding a pole** → Root locus branches are **attracted toward the new pole** (rightward, less stable).
> - **Adding a zero** → Root locus branches are **attracted toward the new zero** (leftward, more stable).
> - A **pole-zero pair** close together → approximately **cancels** each other; the dominant dynamics are governed by remaining poles/zeros.
> - The **dominant poles** (closest to the imaginary axis) determine the transient response.

### 3.4 Comprehensive Example: Pole-Zero Movement Effects

Let us trace the closed-loop pole location for $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+4)}$ as we change $K$.

**Characteristic equation:**

$$s^2 + 4s + K = 0$$

**Closed-loop poles:**

$$s_{1,2} = -2 \pm \sqrt{4 - K}$$

- For $K = 0$: $s_{1,2} = 0, -4$ (at the open-loop poles). ✓
- For $K = 3$: $s_{1,2} = -2 \pm 1 = -1, -3$ (real, distinct, in LHP — stable).
- For $K = 4$: $s_{1,2} = -2$ (double pole, critically damped).
- For $K = 5$: $s_{1,2} = -2 \pm j$ (complex, in LHP — underdamped, still stable).

Now add a **zero at $s = -2$**:

$$G(s) = \frac{(s+2)}{s(s+4)}$$

**Characteristic equation:**

$$s^2 + 4s + K(s+2) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s^2 + (4+K)s + 2K = 0$$

**Closed-loop poles:**

$$s_{1,2} = \frac{-(4+K) \pm \sqrt{(4+K)^2 - 8K}}{2}$$

For $K = 5$:

$$s_{1,2} = \frac{-9 \pm \sqrt{81 - 40}}{2} = \frac{-9 \pm \sqrt{41}}{2} = \frac{-9 \pm 6.4}{2}$$

$$s_1 = -1.3, \quad s_2 = -7.7$$

**Comparison:**
- Without zero (K=5): $s = -2 \pm j1$ (oscillatory, less damped).
- With zero (K=5): $s = -1.3, -7.7$ (real, no oscillation, but the dominant pole at $-1.3$ gives a settling time of $\approx 3$ seconds).

This shows the zero **dragged the root locus to the left** along the real axis, **eliminating oscillation** in this case.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The effect of poles and zeros is **strongly gain-dependent**. A zero may stabilize for low $K$ but destabilize for high $K$ (if placed improperly). Always verify the **$j\omega$-axis crossing** using Routh-Hurwitz.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Pole-Zero Effect on Root Locus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Open Loop Transfer Function G sub s times H sub s] --> B{Identify Poles and Zeros}
    B --> C[Pole at s equals zero]
    B --> D[Pole at s equals negative a]
    B --> E[Zero at s equals negative b]
    C --> F[Add pole: Loc shifts rightward]
    C --> G[Add zero: Loc shifts leftward]
    D --> F
    D --> G
    E --> F
    E --> G
    F --> H[Less stable system. More oscillation. Higher overshoot]
    G --> I[More stable system. Better damping. Lower overshoot]
    H --> J[May need lead compensator]
    I --> K[Design verified for given K range]
    J --> L[Re check root locus shape]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Final Compensator Design]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Root Locus Behavior Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Original System: 1 over s times s plus 2"]
        P1A[Pole at 0]
        P1B[Pole at negative 2]
        R1A[Branch to infinity at positive 90 deg]
        R1B[Branch to infinity at negative 90 deg]
    end
    subgraph S2["With Added Pole at s equals negative 1"]
        P2A[Pole at 0]
        P2B[Pole at negative 1]
        P2C[Pole at negative 2]
        R2A[Asymptote at 60 deg]
        R2B[Asymptote at 180 deg]
        R2C[Asymptote at negative 60 deg]
    end
    subgraph S3["With Added Zero at s equals negative 3"]
        P3A[Pole at 0]
        P3B[Pole at negative 2]
        Z3A[Zero at negative 3]
        R3A[Single asymptote at 180 deg]
        R3B[Branch attracted to zero]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> T1[Effect: Loc pushed RIGHT, less stable]
    S3 --> T2[Effect: Loc pulled LEFT, more stable]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Compensator Design via Pole-Zero Placement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Uncompensated System with Poor Root Locus] --> B{Analyze Desired Response}
    B --> C[Settling Time Requirement]
    B --> D[Percent Overshoot Requirement]
    B --> E[Steady State Error Requirement]
    C --> F[Place Compensator Zero on Desired Dominant Pole Location]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Add Integrator. Pole at origin. for zero SSE]
    F --> H[Place Compensator Pole to Cancel Unwanted OLP]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Verify New Root Locus]
    I --> J{Dominant poles meet specs?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Design Complete]
    J -->|No| L[Adjust Compensator Pole Zero Locations]
    L --> I
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow: Pole-Zero → Root Locus → System Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Reference Signal] --> B[Controller: G sub c sub s]
    B --> C[Plant: G sub p sub s]
    C --> D[Sensor: H sub s]
    D --> E[Feedback Comparator]
    E --> B
    B --> F[Open Loop Transfer Function]
    F --> G[Characteristic Equation: 1 plus K G H equals 0]
    G --> H[Root Locus Plot in s plane]
    H --> I{Pole Location Analysis}
    I -->|Pole in LHP| J[Stable System]
    I -->|Pole on Imaginary Axis| K[Marginally Stable]
    I -->|Pole in RHP| L[Unstable System]
    J --> M[Transient Response: Settling Time, Overshoot]
    K --> M
    L --> N[System Requires Redesign]
    N --> O[Modify Controller Pole Zero Placement]
    O --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** In examination answers, **always sketch the root locus** before and after adding pole/zero to show the **directional shift**. This visual proof carries significant marks (typically 2-3 marks out of 7 in a sub-question).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is the effect of adding a pole to the open-loop transfer function on the root locus? Justify your answer using the angle condition.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Adding an open-loop pole to $G(s)H(s)$ has the following effects on the root locus:

1. **Rightward Shift:** The new pole contributes an additional phase angle of $-\angle(s - p_{\text{new}})$ to the angle condition. To satisfy $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180°(2q+1)$, the test point must move toward the new pole, which is typically located in the LHP. This causes the entire root locus to shift **rightward**, making the closed-loop system **less stable** (or potentially unstable for high $K$).

2. **Increased Number of Branches and Asymptotes:** The number of branches equals the total open-loop poles, which increases by 1. Consequently, the number of asymptotes $(P - Z)$ also increases by 1, with new asymptote angles computed as $\phi_A = (2q+1)180° / (P-Z)$.

3. **Engineering Example:** Adding an integrator (pole at origin) to a Type-0 system creates a Type-1 system, which **reduces steady-state error** but **increases overshoot** and reduces relative stability.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1M) + Phase angle justification (1M) + Consequence (1M).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*State and explain the rules for constructing root locus on the real axis. How does the location of zeros affect the real-axis segments of the root locus?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Real-Axis Root Locus Rule (1 Mark):**
A point on the real axis lies on the root locus (for $K > 0$) if and only if the **total number of real poles and real zeros to the right of that point is odd**.

**Explanation (1 Mark):**
This is because each real pole or zero to the right of a test point contributes either $0°$ or $180°$ to the total angle. The sum of all such contributions is $180°$ (modulo $360°$) only when the count is odd.

**Effect of Zeros on Real-Axis Segments (1 Mark):**
- Zeros placed on the real axis **terminate** the root locus branches. A branch of the root locus starting from the rightmost pole ends at the leftmost zero.
- Zeros placed to the **left of all poles** extend the real-axis root locus segment further left, thereby **increasing the stability margin**.
- Zeros placed **between poles** create break-in or breakaway behavior, splitting the real-axis locus into multiple segments.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$:

(i) Find the number of asymptotes, their angles, and the centroid.

(ii) Determine the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion.

(iii) Comment on the effect of adding a zero at $s = -3$ on the root locus and stability of the system.

**Model Solution (a):**

**(i) Asymptotes, Angles, and Centroid (3 Marks):**

Open-loop poles: $s = 0, -2, -4$. No finite zeros.
- $P = 3, \quad Z = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P - Z = 3$ asymptotes.

**Asymptote angles:**

$$\phi_A = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{3}, \quad q = 0, 1, 2$$

$$\phi_A = 60°, \quad 180°, \quad 300° \;\; (\text{i.e., } +60°, +180°, -60°)$$

**Centroid:**

$$\sigma_A = \frac{\sum \text{Poles} - \sum \text{Zeros}}{P - Z} = \frac{(0) + (-2) + (-4) - 0}{3} = \frac{-6}{3} = -2$$

**(ii) Stability Range using Routh-Hurwitz (2 Marks):**

Characteristic equation:

$$1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0$$

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$$

**Routh Array:**

| $s^3$ | 1 | 8 |
|-------|---|---|
| $s^2$ | 6 | $K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\frac{48 - K}{6}$ | 0 |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | 0 |

For stability, all first-column elements must be positive:

$$\frac{48 - K}{6} > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K < 48$$

$$K > 0$$

**Range of stable K:** $0 < K < 48$.

**(iii) Effect of Adding Zero at $s = -3$ (2 Marks):**

With the zero added:

$$G_{\text{new}}(s)H(s) = \frac{K(s+3)}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$$

- Number of finite zeros: 1. Number of asymptotes: $3 - 1 = 2$.
- Asymptote angles: $\pm 90°$.
- Centroid: $\sigma_A = \dfrac{0 + (-2) + (-4) - (-3)}{2} = \dfrac{-3}{2} = -1.5$.
- The root locus is **pulled leftward** toward the new zero at $s = -3$.
- Stability range **expands** significantly (the new system is stable for a much larger range of $K$).
- The dominant poles can be placed in a more desirable location for transient response.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the zero added in part (a), find the angle of departure from the complex poles (assuming they are now complex) and determine if the system can achieve a damping ratio $\zeta \geq 0.5$ for some value of $K$.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Angle of Departure (3 Marks):**
The complex poles will be at $s = \sigma \pm j\omega$ for some $K > 48$ (where the system becomes unstable). For a system to have a damping ratio $\zeta \geq 0.5$, the closed-loop poles must lie within a cone of angle $\pm 60°$ from the negative real axis (since $\cos\theta = \zeta$).

The angle of the line from origin to the desired pole location is $\theta = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$. For $\zeta = 0.5$, $\theta = 60°$.

Using the angle condition, the locus from any complex pole $p_1 = \sigma_p + j\omega_p$ departs at:

$$\phi_d = 180° + \sum \angle(z_i - p_1) - \sum \angle(p_j - p_1), \quad j \neq 1$$

**Damping Ratio Analysis (4 Marks):**
The line $\zeta = 0.5$ corresponds to the locus in the *s*-plane where the ratio $\sigma/\omega = -\cot(60°) = -0.577$, or $\omega = -\sqrt{3}\sigma$.

Substituting $s = \sigma + j\omega = \sigma - j\sqrt{3}\sigma$ into the characteristic equation $s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K(s+3) = 0$ and solving for the intersection with the $\zeta = 0.5$ line gives the maximum allowable $K$ before instability.

For small $K$, the complex poles are deep in the LHP, and $\zeta$ is large. As $K$ increases, the complex poles approach the imaginary axis, and $\zeta$ decreases. The system achieves $\zeta = 0.5$ for a specific range of $K$ values, and the student is expected to compute this range graphically or via numerical substitution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **forget the minus sign** in the centroid formula. The centroid is $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum \text{Poles} - \sum \text{Zeros}}{P - Z}$, NOT the average of all poles and zeros. Also, in the Routh array, the $s^1$ row element must be calculated as $\dfrac{b_1 c_2 - b_2 c_1}{b_1}$ where rows are $b$ and $c$. Students commonly write the wrong sign and lose 2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)}$$

(i) Sketch the root locus qualitatively and identify all key features (asymptotes, centroid, real-axis segments, breakaway points).

(ii) Find the value of $K$ at which the system becomes marginally stable.

(iii) Determine the range of $K$ for which the damping ratio $\zeta \geq 0.707$ (i.e., the closed-loop poles lie within the $45°$ cone from the origin).

**Model Solution (a):**

**(i) Root Locus Features (3 Marks):**

- Open-loop poles: $s = 0, -1, -3$. Open-loop zero: $s = -2$.
- $P = 3, \quad Z = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P - Z = 2$ asymptotes.
- Asymptote angles: $\phi_A = \pm 90°$.
- Centroid: $\sigma_A = \dfrac{0 + (-1) + (-3) - (-2)}{2} = \dfrac{-2}{2} = -1$.
- Real-axis segments: The locus exists on segments where the count of poles+zeros to the right is odd.
  - Between $-1$ and $0$: 1 pole to the right (at 0). Count = 1 (odd). **Locus exists.**
  - Between $-2$ and $-1$: 2 poles to the right (at $0, -1$) and 0 zeros. Count = 2 (even). **No locus.**
  - Between $-3$ and $-2$: 3 poles to the right. Count = 3 (odd). **Locus exists.**
  - To the left of $-3$: 3 poles + 1 zero = 4 to the right. Count = 4 (even). **No locus.**
- Breakaway point: Between $-1$ and $0$, where two branches meet before going to complex plane.
- The zero at $s = -2$ attracts the branch starting from $s = -3$.

**(ii) Marginal Stability (2 Marks):**

Characteristic equation:

$$1 + \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)} = 0$$

$$s(s+1)(s+3) + K(s+2) = 0$$

$$s^3 + 4s^2 + 3s + K(s+2) = 0$$

$$s^3 + 4s^2 + (3+K)s + 2K = 0$$

**Routh Array:**

| $s^3$ | 1 | $3+K$ |
|-------|---|--------|
| $s^2$ | 4 | $2K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\frac{12 - 2K}{4}$ | 0 |
| $s^0$ | $2K$ | 0 |

For marginal stability: $\frac{12 - 2K}{4} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = 6$.

**Value of $K$ for marginal stability: $K = 6$.**

**(iii) Damping Ratio $\zeta \geq 0.707$ (2 Marks):**

For $\zeta = 0.707$, $\cos\theta = 0.707 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = 45°$. The line is $\omega = -\sigma$.

Substitute $s = \sigma - j\sigma$ (i.e., $s = \sigma(1 - j)$) into the characteristic equation and solve for real $\sigma$ and $K$. The student should identify the range of $K$ where the closed-loop poles lie within the $45°$ cone.

This requires solving the complex equation:

$$[\sigma(1-j)]^3 + 4[\sigma(1-j)]^2 + (3+K)[\sigma(1-j)] + 2K = 0$$

Separate real and imaginary parts, solve simultaneously. The answer typically yields a maximum $K$ value beyond which the damping ratio drops below $0.707$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the effect of the following modifications on the root locus and stability of the above system:

(i) Replacing the zero at $s = -2$ with a pole at $s = -2$.

(ii) Adding an additional pole at $s = -10$ and an additional zero at $s = -1$.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Replacing zero with pole (3 Marks):**

New transfer function: $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)}$.

- Now $P = 4, Z = 0$. Four asymptotes at $\pm 45°, \pm 135°$.
- Centroid: $\sigma_A = \dfrac{0 + (-1) + (-2) + (-3)}{4} = -1.5$.
- The root locus is pushed **rightward** (centroid moves from $-1$ to $-1.5$ compared to original? No, wait — actually without the zero, the centroid moves further from origin. The system is **less stable** for the same $K$).
- Original system was stable for $0 < K < 6$ (actually, in original with zero, $K_{\max} = 6$). New system has different stability range.
- **Conclusion:** Removing the zero and adding a pole **destabilizes** the system. This is the well-known destabilizing effect of poles vs. stabilizing effect of zeros.

**(ii) Adding pole at $-10$ and zero at $-1$ (4 Marks):**

New transfer function: $G(s) = \dfrac{K(s+1)}{s(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)} = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+3)}$ (after pole-zero cancellation at $s = -1$).

- The pole and zero **cancel** because they are at the same location $s = -1$.
- Effective system: $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+3)}$, which is the same as if no compensator was added!
- **Conclusion:** Placing a pole-zero pair at the same location is a **trivial** modification — it has no effect on the system. To be useful, the pole-zero pair must be placed at **different locations**, with the zero typically **closer to the origin** (in the LHP) than the pole to achieve a lead-compensator effect.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes on This Question:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Centroid:** Many students write $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum \text{Poles} + \sum \text{Zeros}}{P - Z}$. The correct formula has a **minus** sign for zeros: $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum \text{Poles} - \sum \text{Zeros}}{P - Z}$.
> 2. **Real-Axis Rule Confusion:** Students often say "locus is on real axis to the LEFT of the leftmost pole" — this is **only true when there are no zeros**. With zeros, the rule is more complex (count of poles+zeros to the right must be odd).
> 3. **Asymptote Angle Calculation:** When $P - Z > 2$, students forget to compute all angles for $q = 0, 1, \ldots, P-Z-1$.
> 4. **Pole-Zero Cancellation:** Students do not recognize that pole-zero cancellation leads to a **lower-order equivalent system**, missing 2-3 marks in the problem.
> 5. **Drawing the Locus:** KTU examiners award 1-2 marks specifically for a **correctly drawn** root locus sketch. Students often omit this entirely.

---

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- [ ] **Root Locus Definition:** A plot of closed-loop pole locations in the *s*-plane as gain $K$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$.
- [ ] **Starting Points ($K=0$):** Open-loop poles of $G(s)H(s)$.
- [ ] **Ending Points ($K=\infty$):** Open-loop zeros (finite) or infinity (along asymptotes).
- [ ] **Number of Branches:** Equal to the number of open-loop poles $P$.
- [ ] **Angle Condition:** $\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180°(2q+1)$.
- [ ] **Magnitude Condition:** $K = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(s)H(s) \vert}$.
- [ ] **Real-Axis Rule:** Locus exists on the real axis where the count of real poles and real zeros to the right is **odd**.
- [ ] **Number of Asymptotes:** $P - Z$.
- [ ] **Asymptote Angles:** $\phi_A = \dfrac{(2q+1)180°}{P-Z}, \quad q = 0, 1, \ldots, P-Z-1$.
- [ ] **Centroid Formula:** $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum \text{Poles} - \sum \text{Zeros}}{P - Z}$.
- [ ] **Effect of Adding Pole:** Locus shifts **rightward** (destabilizing), number of asymptotes increases by 1, more branches added.
- [ ] **Effect of Adding Zero:** Locus shifts **leftward** (stabilizing), number of asymptotes decreases by 1, branch terminates at the zero.
- [ ] **Pole-Zero Cancellation:** If a pole and zero coincide, they cancel; the effective system order reduces.
- [ ] **Dominant Poles:** Poles closest to the $j\omega$-axis dominate the transient response. They determine settling time and overshoot.
- [ ] **$j\omega$-Axis Crossing:** Found by Routh-Hurwitz; indicates the boundary of stability.
- [ ] **Angle of Departure:** $\phi_d = 180° + \sum \angle(\text{zeros} - p_1) - \sum \angle(\text{other poles} - p_1)$.
- [ ] **Compensator Insight:** A **lead compensator** has a zero closer to origin than pole (improves transient response). A **lag compensator** has a pole closer to origin than zero (improves steady-state error).
- [ ] **Stability Limit:** Determined by $K_{\max}$ from Routh-Hurwitz when locus crosses $j\omega$-axis.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 Scheme:** Always show **magnitude and angle conditions** explicitly in derivations; always sketch the locus qualitatively before numerical calculations.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked about pole-zero effects, **always quantify** the effect — don't just say "shifts rightward." Specify the **change in centroid, change in asymptote angles, and change in stability range**. Examiners award full marks only for such complete answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
