topics.title: Frequency Response Representation and Polar Plot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT501 - LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frequency Response Representation and Polar Plot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Frequency Response

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Response** is defined as the **steady-state response** of a stable, linear, time-invariant (LTI) system when subjected to a **sinusoidal input** of constant amplitude and variable frequency $\omega$.

If the input to a system is a sinusoid $r(t) = R \sin(\omega t)$, then the steady-state output is of the form:

$$c_{ss}(t) = C \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

The **Sinusoidal Transfer Function** (or Frequency Transfer Function) is obtained by substituting $s = j\omega$ into the open-loop transfer function $G(s)$:

$$G(j\omega) = G(s)\Big\vert_{s = j\omega}$$

This complex quantity encodes both the magnitude ratio $M(\omega) = \vert G(j\omega) \vert$ and the phase shift $\phi(\omega) = \angle G(j\omega)$ introduced by the system at every frequency $\omega$.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Audio Equalizer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of your music system's **graphic equalizer (bass, mid, treble sliders)**. When you boost bass, you are essentially amplifying low-frequency signals while attenuating high frequencies. The graph you see on the equalizer — magnitude plotted against frequency — is conceptually identical to the **frequency response plot** of a control system.

Similarly, a control system does not treat all input frequencies equally:
- It **amplifies** some frequencies (resonance in second-order systems).
- It **attenuates** others (high-frequency noise rejection).
- It **phase-shifts** signals depending on how the energy-storing elements (inductors, capacitors, masses) respond.

A **Polar Plot** is simply a compact graphical way of representing this magnitude-and-phase information on a single sheet: the radial distance from the origin equals the magnitude $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$, and the angular position equals the phase angle $\angle G(j\omega)$.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **$\omega$** — Angular frequency in **rad/sec** (independent variable).
> - **$\vert G(j\omega) \vert$** — Magnitude ratio (dimensionless, often expressed in **dB**).
> - **$\angle G(j\omega)$** — Phase angle in **degrees** or **radians**.
> - **Range of $\omega$** — Typically **0 to $\infty$** (sometimes $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ for full Nyquist).
> - **Polar Plot** — Plot of $G(j\omega)$ in the complex $s$-plane as $\omega$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Use Frequency Response?

Frequency domain analysis is preferred over time-domain analysis in many industrial applications because:

1. **Stability assessment** (Bode, Nyquist, Root Locus techniques) becomes graphical.
2. **Noise rejection** characteristics can be directly visualized.
3. **Experimental measurement** is straightforward — apply a sine wave, measure amplitude ratio and phase shift.
4. **Mathematical convenience** — differentiation becomes $j\omega$, integration becomes $1/(j\omega)$.

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundation

For a linear system with transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{K(1 + s\tau_a)(1 + s\tau_b)\cdots}{s^M (1 + sT_1)(1 + sT_2)\cdots (1 + 2\zeta s/\omega_n + s^2/\omega_n^2)}$$

Substituting $s = j\omega$ gives the **Sinusoidal Transfer Function**:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K(1 + j\omega\tau_a)(1 + j\omega\tau_b)\cdots}{(j\omega)^M (1 + j\omega T_1)(1 + j\omega T_2)\cdots}$$

The system behavior is fully described by the magnitude and phase at every frequency.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the high-yield reference for all polar plot derivations. Memorize these forms.**

| # | Transfer Function $G(s)$ | $G(j\omega)$ Form | Magnitude $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ | Phase $\angle G(j\omega)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K$ (Pure Gain) | $K$ | $K$ | $0^{\circ}$ |
| 2 | $j\omega$ (Pure Derivative) | $j\omega$ | $\omega$ | $+90^{\circ}$ |
| 3 | $1/(j\omega)$ (Pure Integral) | $1/(j\omega)$ | $1/\omega$ | $-90^{\circ}$ |
| 4 | $1/(1 + j\omega T)$ (1st order lag) | $1/(1 + j\omega T)$ | $1/\sqrt{1 + \omega^2 T^2}$ | $-\tan^{-1}(\omega T)$ |
| 5 | $1 + j\omega T$ (1st order lead) | $1 + j\omega T$ | $\sqrt{1 + \omega^2 T^2}$ | $+\tan^{-1}(\omega T)$ |
| 6 | $1/[1 - \omega^2/\omega_n^2 + j(2\zeta\omega/\omega_n)]$ (2nd order) | $1/[1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2 + j 2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)]$ | $1/\sqrt{[1-(\omega/\omega_n)^2]^2 + [2\zeta\omega/\omega_n]^2}$ | $-\tan^{-1}\!\left[\frac{2\zeta\omega/\omega_n}{1-(\omega/\omega_n)^2}\right]$ |

### 2.4 Polar Plot Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Polar Plot** is the locus of the tip of the phasor $G(j\omega)$ in the complex plane as $\omega$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$. The radial coordinate (distance from origin) equals the magnitude $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$, and the angular coordinate equals the phase angle $\angle G(j\omega)$.

### 2.5 Real-Time Variation of $G(j\omega)$

For plotting, observe the **two extreme frequencies** and the **phase at $\omega = 0^+$ and $\omega = \infty$**:

| System Type | Behavior at $\omega \to 0^+$ | Behavior at $\omega \to \infty$ |
|---|---|---|
| Type 0 ($M=0$) | $\vert G \vert = K$, $\angle G = 0^{\circ}$ | $\angle G = -90^{\circ} \times n$ (depends on order) |
| Type 1 ($M=1$) | $\vert G \vert \to \infty$, $\angle G = -90^{\circ}$ | $\angle G \to -(90^{\circ} \times (n-M))$ |
| Type 2 ($M=2$) | $\vert G \vert \to \infty$, $\angle G = -180^{\circ}$ | $\angle G \to -(90^{\circ} \times (n-M))$ |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Mechanical engineering:** Vibration analysis of rotors, machine tool chatter prediction.
- **Electrical engineering:** Filter design (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass).
- **Aerospace:** Flutter analysis, autopilot stability verification.
- **Process control:** Tuning PI/PID controllers by observing phase lag at crossover frequency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: General Procedure to Sketch a Polar Plot

**Step 1 — Identify the Open-Loop Transfer Function:**
Write $G(s)$ in time-constant form with $K$, poles, zeros.

**Step 2 — Substitute $s = j\omega$:**
Replace every $s$ with $j\omega$ to obtain $G(j\omega)$.

**Step 3 — Compute $G(j\omega)$ at $\omega = 0^+$ and $\omega = \infty$:**
- At $\omega = 0^+$: substitute the smallest positive frequency. (For Type 0, this gives $K$ on the positive real axis.)
- At $\omega = \infty$: the highest-power terms of $j\omega$ dominate. The phase tends to $-90^{\circ}(n-M)$ where $n$ = order of denominator and $M$ = type number.

**Step 4 — Real and Imaginary Separation:**
Express $G(j\omega) = X(\omega) + jY(\omega)$ where $X$ and $Y$ are real functions of $\omega$.

**Step 5 — Find the Intersection with Real and Imaginary Axes:**
Set $Y(\omega) = 0$ → find $\omega$ where the curve crosses the real axis.
Set $X(\omega) = 0$ → find $\omega$ where the curve crosses the imaginary axis.

**Step 6 — Determine Direction of Plotting:**
- If $\angle G(j\omega)$ decreases (becomes more negative) as $\omega$ increases → plot **clockwise**.
- Convention: Plot from $\omega = 0$ to $\omega = \infty$ unless otherwise specified.

**Step 7 — Locate the Mirror Image:**
For plotting the negative-$\omega$ half (when needed), reflect the polar plot across the real axis.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Type 0, First-Order System

Consider:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{1 + sT}$$

**Substituting $s = j\omega$:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K}{1 + j\omega T}$$

**Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate $(1 - j\omega T)$:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K(1 - j\omega T)}{(1 + j\omega T)(1 - j\omega T)}$$

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K(1 - j\omega T)}{1 + \omega^2 T^2}$$

**Separating Real and Imaginary parts:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K}{1 + \omega^2 T^2} - j\frac{K\omega T}{1 + \omega^2 T^2}$$

Let $X = \dfrac{K}{1 + \omega^2 T^2}$ and $Y = -\dfrac{K\omega T}{1 + \omega^2 T^2}$.

**Finding the geometric locus (the famous semicircle proof):**

Note that:

$$\frac{Y}{X} = -\omega T \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega T = -\frac{Y}{X}$$

Squaring and using $X = K/(1+\omega^2T^2)$, we get $1 + \omega^2T^2 = K/X$. Substituting:

$$\omega^2 T^2 = \frac{K - X}{X} = \frac{Y^2}{X^2}$$

$$Y^2 = X(K - X) = KX - X^2$$

$$X^2 + Y^2 - KX = 0$$

**Completing the square:**

$$\left(X - \frac{K}{2}\right)^2 + Y^2 = \left(\frac{K}{2}\right)^2$$

This is the equation of a **circle of radius $K/2$ centered at $(K/2, 0)$**, lying in the lower half plane (because $Y < 0$ for all $\omega > 0$).

**Behavior at extremes:**

- $\omega = 0$: $G(j0) = K$ — start at $(K, 0)$.
- $\omega = \infty$: $G(j\infty) = 0\angle -90^{\circ}$ — end at origin.

The locus is a **semicircle in the 4th quadrant**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation — Type 1, Second-Order System

Consider:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(1 + sT)}$$

**Substituting $s = j\omega$:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K}{j\omega(1 + j\omega T)} = \frac{K}{j\omega - \omega^2 T}$$

**Multiply by the conjugate of the denominator:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K(-j\omega - \omega^2 T)}{(j\omega - \omega^2 T)(-j\omega - \omega^2 T)}$$

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{-Kj\omega - K\omega^2 T}{\omega^2 + \omega^4 T^2} = \frac{-K\omega^2 T - jK\omega}{\omega^2(1 + \omega^2 T^2)}$$

**Separating:**

$$X(\omega) = \frac{-KT}{1 + \omega^2 T^2}, \qquad Y(\omega) = \frac{-K}{\omega(1 + \omega^2 T^2)}$$

**Phase angle:**

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(\omega T)$$

**Asymptotic behavior:**

- $\omega \to 0^+$: $\angle G \to -90^{\circ}$, $\vert G \vert \to \infty$. The locus approaches infinity along the negative imaginary axis.
- $\omega \to \infty$: $\angle G \to -180^{\circ}$, $\vert G \vert \to 0$. The locus ends at origin tangent to the positive real axis from below.

**Crossing the negative real axis:**
Set $Y(\omega) = 0$ — but $Y$ is always negative for $\omega > 0$. So we examine when the curve crosses the **negative real axis** by tracking the real part $X(\omega)$ at $\omega = 1/T$:

$$X(1/T) = \frac{-KT}{1 + 1} = -\frac{KT}{2}$$

So the curve crosses the negative real axis at $-(KT/2,\, 0)$.

**Conclusion:** The polar plot starts from $-\infty$ along the $-j\omega$ axis, sweeps clockwise, crosses the negative real axis at $-KT/2$, and terminates at the origin with tangent parallel to the positive real axis.

---

### 3.4 Worked Derivation — Type 2, Second-Order System

Consider:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s^2(1 + sT)}$$

**Substituting $s = j\omega$:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K}{(j\omega)^2(1 + j\omega T)} = \frac{K}{-\omega^2(1 + j\omega T)}$$

**Multiplying by conjugate:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K(1 - j\omega T)}{-\omega^2(1 + \omega^2 T^2)} = \frac{-K(1 - j\omega T)}{\omega^2(1 + \omega^2 T^2)}$$

**Separating:**

$$X(\omega) = \frac{-K}{\omega^2(1 + \omega^2 T^2)}, \qquad Y(\omega) = \frac{+K T}{\omega(1 + \omega^2 T^2)}$$

**Phase angle:**

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -180^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(\omega T)$$

**Behavior:**
- $\omega \to 0^+$: phase $\to -180^{\circ}$, magnitude $\to \infty$. Tangent at $\omega = 0^+$ is parallel to the negative imaginary axis (because $Y$ approaches $\infty$ faster than $X$).
- $\omega \to \infty$: phase $\to -270^{\circ}$, magnitude $\to 0$.

The plot starts from $\infty$ along the $-j\omega$ axis (at $\omega = 0^+$, but rotated by $180^{\circ}$ so it approaches from the $-180^{\circ}$ direction), curves clockwise, and spirals into the origin.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Identification of Endpoints

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix during exams to immediately identify the **starting point**, **ending point**, and **tangent direction** without doing a full derivation.

| System Type | Open-Loop Form | $\omega = 0^+$ Point | $\omega = \infty$ Point | Tangent at $\infty$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Type 0, Order 1 | $K/(1+sT)$ | $(K, 0)$ | $(0, 0)$ | Along $-90^{\circ}$ |
| Type 0, Order 2 | $K/(1+sT_1)(1+sT_2)$ | $(K, 0)$ | $(0, 0)$ | Along $-180^{\circ}$ |
| Type 1, Order 2 | $K/[s(1+sT)]$ | $\infty \angle -90^{\circ}$ | $(0, 0)$ | Along $-180^{\circ}$ (neg. real axis) |
| Type 1, Order 3 | $K/[s(1+sT_1)(1+sT_2)]$ | $\infty \angle -90^{\circ}$ | $(0, 0)$ | Along $-270^{\circ}$ |
| Type 2, Order 3 | $K/[s^2(1+sT)]$ | $\infty \angle -180^{\circ}$ | $(0, 0)$ | Along $-270^{\circ}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Frequency Response Analysis

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the complete functional pipeline used to extract a polar plot from a transfer function.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step1 Start with Transfer Function Gs"] --> B["Step2 Identify Type and Order"]
    B --> C["Step3 Substitute s equals j omega"]
    C --> D["Step4 Compute Magnitude and Phase"]
    D --> E["Step5 Separate Real and Imaginary Parts"]
    E --> F["Step6 Evaluate at omega zero plus"]
    F --> G["Step7 Evaluate at omega infinity"]
    G --> H["Step8 Find Axis Crossing Points"]
    H --> I["Step9 Determine Tangent Direction"]
    I --> J["Step10 Plot the Locus in Polar Form"]
    J --> K["Step11 End Frequency Response Polar Plot"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Interpretation of Polar Plot Features

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Input G of j omega"] --> B1["Extract Magnitude Ratio"]
    A1 --> C1["Extract Phase Shift"]
    B1 --> D1["Stability Margin Assessment"]
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1["Gain Margin Determination"]
    D1 --> F1["Phase Margin Determination"]
    E1 --> G1["Decision Stable or Unstable System"]
    F1 --> G1
```

### 4.3 Visual Reference — Typical Polar Plot Shapes by System Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Open Loop Transfer Function"] --> B{"Identify System Type"}
    B --> C["Type 0 Order 1"]
    B --> D["Type 0 Order 2"]
    B --> E["Type 1 Order 2"]
    B --> F["Type 2 Order 3"]
    C --> C1["Semicircle in Fourth Quadrant"]
    D --> D1["Quadratic Curve Crossing Negative Real Axis"]
    E --> E1["Starts at Minus Infinity Along Negative Imaginary Axis"]
    F --> F1["Starts at Minus Infinity Along Negative Real Axis"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Aid:** When the curve **encircles** the critical point $(-1, j0)$ in the clockwise direction, the corresponding closed-loop system is **unstable**. When it does not encircle $-1$, the system is **stable** (this is the essence of the Nyquist criterion — covered in Module 4).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define frequency response. What is meant by sinusoidal transfer function?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **frequency response** of a linear time-invariant system is the steady-state response of the system when subjected to a sinusoidal input of unit amplitude, plotted as a function of the input frequency $\omega$.
>
> The **sinusoidal transfer function** is obtained by substituting $s = j\omega$ in the system's transfer function $G(s)$, yielding a complex function $G(j\omega)$ whose magnitude gives the amplitude ratio and whose angle gives the phase shift introduced by the system at frequency $\omega$.
>
> *(1 mark for frequency response definition, 1 mark for sinusoidal TF definition, 1 mark for the magnitude/phase interpretation.)*

---

**Q2. What is a polar plot? On which axes are the magnitude and phase angle represented?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> A **polar plot** is the locus of the tip of the phasor $G(j\omega)$ in the complex $s$-plane as $\omega$ varies from $0$ to $\infty$.
>
> - **Radial distance** from the origin equals the magnitude $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$.
> - **Angular position** with respect to the positive real axis equals the phase angle $\angle G(j\omega)$.
>
> The plot is drawn on the **complex $G(j\omega)$ plane** (also called the $G$-plane), which is the same as the $s$-plane when $s$ is restricted to the imaginary axis $s = j\omega$.
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for magnitude/phase mapping, 1 mark for the complex plane description.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format (2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]*

**(a)** Sketch the polar plot for the transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(1 + sT)}, \quad K, T > 0$$

Show clearly the starting point, ending point, and the point where it crosses the real axis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same system, determine the **gain margin** and **phase margin** if $K = 2$ and $T = 1$. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Sketching the Polar Plot:**

**Step 1 — Substitute $s = j\omega$:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K}{j\omega(1 + j\omega T)}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude and Phase:**

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{K}{\omega\sqrt{1 + \omega^2 T^2}}$$

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(\omega T)$$

**Step 3 — Endpoint Analysis:**

- At $\omega \to 0^+$: $\vert G \vert \to \infty$, $\angle G \to -90^{\circ}$. The plot starts at infinity along the **negative imaginary axis**.
- At $\omega \to \infty$: $\vert G \vert \to 0$, $\angle G \to -180^{\circ}$. The plot ends at the **origin** with tangent parallel to the positive real axis (approach from below).

**Step 4 — Real-Axis Crossing:**

Separating real and imaginary parts:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{-KT}{1 + \omega^2 T^2} - j\frac{K}{\omega(1 + \omega^2 T^2)}$$

Set the imaginary part equal to zero to find the real-axis crossing. The imaginary part is always negative for $\omega > 0$, so we instead track the **minimum of the real part** (which occurs at $\omega = 1/T$):

$$X_{\min} = X(1/T) = \frac{-KT}{2}$$

So the curve crosses the negative real axis at $-(KT/2,\, 0)$.

**Plot Description:**

The polar plot begins from $-\infty$ along the $-j\omega$ direction, sweeps clockwise through the 3rd quadrant, crosses the negative real axis at $-(KT/2,\, 0)$, and spirals into the origin along the positive real axis direction.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for magnitude/phase equations, 2 marks for endpoint analysis, 2 marks for axis-crossing calculation, 1 mark for plot sketch.)*

---

**Part (b) — Gain Margin and Phase Margin:**

Given: $K = 2$, $T = 1$.

**Step 1 — Phase Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{pc}$):**

Phase crossover is where $\angle G(j\omega) = -180^{\circ}$:

$$-90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc} T) = -180^{\circ}$$

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) = 90^{\circ} \implies \omega_{pc} \to \infty$$

Since $\omega_{pc} \to \infty$, the magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ is zero, which gives:

$$\text{Gain Margin } GM = \infty \quad \text{(dB)}$$

**Step 2 — Gain Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{gc}$):**

This is where $\vert G(j\omega) \vert = 1$:

$$\frac{2}{\omega\sqrt{1 + \omega^2}} = 1$$

$$4 = \omega^2(1 + \omega^2)$$

Let $u = \omega^2$:

$$u^2 + u - 4 = 0$$

$$u = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 16}}{2} = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{17}}{2} = \frac{-1 + 4.123}{2} \approx 1.5615$$

$$\omega_{gc} = \sqrt{1.5615} \approx 1.2496 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 3 — Phase Margin:**

$$PM = 180^{\circ} + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$$

$$\angle G(j1.2496) = -90^{\circ} - \tan^{-1}(1.2496) = -90^{\circ} - 51.34^{\circ} = -141.34^{\circ}$$

$$PM = 180^{\circ} - 141.34^{\circ} = 38.66^{\circ}$$

**Final Answer:**

- Gain Margin $GM = \infty$ dB (conditionally stable margin).
- Phase Margin $PM \approx 38.66^{\circ}$.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for $\omega_{pc}$ identification, 2 marks for $\omega_{gc}$ quadratic, 2 marks for PM calculation, 1 mark for correct final statement.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** With reference to frequency response analysis, explain how the polar plot differs from the Bode plot. State any **three advantages** of polar plots. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Sketch the polar plot for $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{(1 + s)(1 + 0.5s)}$ and determine the frequency at which it crosses the imaginary axis. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Polar Plot vs Bode Plot:**

| Aspect | Polar Plot | Bode Plot |
|---|---|---|
| Axes | Magnitude $\vert G \vert$ (radial) and Phase $\angle G$ (angular) | Two separate plots: $\vert G \vert$ in dB vs $\log\omega$, and $\angle G$ vs $\log\omega$ |
| Format | Single curve on polar/Cartesian complex plane | Two semi-log plots |
| Frequency Range | $\omega$ linear | $\omega$ logarithmic |
| Stability Information | Encroachment on $-1$ point directly visible | Gain and phase margins read off individually |

**Three Advantages of Polar Plots:**

1. **Direct stability insight:** The relationship of the curve to the $(-1, j0)$ point is visually obvious, leading directly to the Nyquist stability criterion.
2. **Closed-loop frequency response** can be read using $M$-circles and $N$-circles directly from the polar plot, which is more cumbersome on a Bode plot.
3. **Single-curve representation:** Both magnitude and phase are captured in **one graph**, making it compact for comparison of multiple systems.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for differences table, 4 marks for the three advantages with elaboration.)*

---

**Part (b) — Polar Plot of $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{(1 + s)(1 + 0.5s)}$:**

**Step 1 — Substitute $s = j\omega$:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{(1 + j\omega)(1 + j0.5\omega)}$$

**Step 2 — Denominator expansion:**

$$(1 + j\omega)(1 + j0.5\omega) = 1 + j0.5\omega + j\omega + j^2 0.5\omega^2 = (1 - 0.5\omega^2) + j(1.5\omega)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the conjugate $(1 - 0.5\omega^2) - j(1.5\omega)$:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10[(1 - 0.5\omega^2) - j(1.5\omega)]}{(1 - 0.5\omega^2)^2 + (1.5\omega)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Separate:**

$$X(\omega) = \frac{10(1 - 0.5\omega^2)}{(1 - 0.5\omega^2)^2 + 2.25\omega^2}$$

$$Y(\omega) = \frac{-15\omega}{(1 - 0.5\omega^2)^2 + 2.25\omega^2}$$

**Step 5 — Endpoints:**

- $\omega = 0$: $X = 10$, $Y = 0$ → starts at $(10, 0)$.
- $\omega \to \infty$: $X \to 0$, $Y \to 0$ → ends at origin.
- Phase at $\omega = \infty$: $\angle G \to -180^{\circ}$ (approach origin from below the negative real axis).

**Step 6 — Imaginary-Axis Crossing (Setting $X(\omega) = 0$):**

$$1 - 0.5\omega^2 = 0 \implies \omega^2 = 2 \implies \omega = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 7 — Y-coordinate at this frequency:**

$$Y(\sqrt{2}) = \frac{-15 \cdot 1.4142}{(1 - 0.5 \cdot 2)^2 + 2.25 \cdot 2} = \frac{-21.213}{0 + 4.5} = -4.714$$

**Plot Description:**

The polar plot starts at $(10, 0)$ on the positive real axis, sweeps clockwise through the 4th quadrant, crosses the negative imaginary axis at $(0, -4.714)$ when $\omega = \sqrt{2}$ rad/sec, continues through the 3rd quadrant, and terminates at the origin with tangent parallel to the negative real axis.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for substitution and separation, 2 marks for endpoints, 2 marks for imaginary-axis crossing frequency, 1 mark for sketch.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
>
> Students commonly **lose marks** on the following points during university exams:
>
> 1. **Forgetting the conjugate multiplication** when separating $G(j\omega)$ into real and imaginary parts — this yields an incorrect $X(\omega)$ and $Y(\omega)$.
> 2. **Incorrect endpoint phase calculation:** At $\omega = 0^+$, do not write "magnitude is $K$" for Type 1 and Type 2 systems. The magnitude is $\infty$ at $\omega = 0^+$.
> 3. **Direction of the plot:** Always verify that the phase is becoming more negative as $\omega$ increases. If not, the curve may be plotted counter-clockwise by mistake.
> 4. **Crossing axis confusion:** Many students confuse "real axis crossing" (where $Y = 0$) with "imaginary axis crossing" (where $X = 0$). Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Units of $\omega$:** Always express in **rad/sec**, not Hz, unless the question explicitly asks for Hz.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- **Frequency response** = steady-state response to sinusoidal input $r(t) = R\sin\omega t$.
- **Sinusoidal TF** = $G(s)\vert_{s=j\omega}$ — a complex number with magnitude and phase.
- **Polar plot** = magnitude vs phase plotted in the complex $G(j\omega)$ plane.
- **Frequency sweep:** always from $\omega = 0$ to $\omega = \infty$ unless stated otherwise.
- **For $1/(1 + sT)$:** locus is a **semicircle** of radius $K/2$ centered at $(K/2, 0)$, lying in 4th quadrant.
- **For $K/[s(1+sT)]$:** locus starts at $\infty \angle -90^{\circ}$, crosses negative real axis at $-(KT/2,\, 0)$, ends at origin.
- **For $K/[s^2(1+sT)]$:** locus starts at $\infty \angle -180^{\circ}$, ends at origin from $-270^{\circ}$ direction.
- **Type 0 systems:** polar plot starts on the positive real axis at $(K, 0)$.
- **Type 1 systems:** polar plot starts at infinity along the negative imaginary axis ($\angle = -90^{\circ}$).
- **Type 2 systems:** polar plot starts at infinity along the negative real axis ($\angle = -180^{\circ}$).
- **At $\omega \to \infty$:** phase always $\to -90^{\circ}(n - M)$ where $n$ is system order and $M$ is type number.
- **Stability hint:** if the curve encircles $(-1, 0)$ clockwise, the closed-loop is unstable (formalized in Nyquist criterion, Module 4).
- **Gain margin (GM):** reciprocal of magnitude at phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$, expressed in dB.
- **Phase margin (PM):** $180^{\circ} + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$, evaluated at gain crossover $\omega_{gc}$.
- **Always** multiply by the complex conjugate to isolate real and imaginary parts.
- **Always** verify the direction (clockwise vs counter-clockwise) by checking monotonicity of phase.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relative Stability: Gain Margin and Phase Margin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT501 - LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Relative Stability: Gain Margin and Phase Margin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Relative Stability** is a measure of **how close** a closed-loop control system is to the boundary of instability (the verge of oscillation), whereas absolute stability only confirms *whether* the system is stable or not.

**Gain Margin (GM)** is defined as the factor by which the open-loop gain may be multiplied before the closed-loop system reaches the verge of instability. Mathematically, it is measured at the **phase crossover frequency (ω_pc)** — the frequency at which the open-loop phase angle equals **−180°**.

**Phase Margin (PM)** is defined as the additional amount of phase lag (in degrees) required to bring the open-loop frequency response to the point of −180° at the **gain crossover frequency (ω_gc)** — the frequency at which the open-loop magnitude equals **unity (0 dB)**.

In compact mathematical form:

$$\text{GM} = \frac{1}{\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert}$$

$$\text{PM} = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **minimum-phase system**, the closed-loop system is stable if and only if:
> - **GM > 1** (equivalently **GM_dB > 0 dB**), AND
> - **PM > 0°**
>
> The system is **marginally stable** when GM = 1 (0 dB) or PM = 0°.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car along a narrow mountain road. The road has no barrier on the cliff edge. **Absolute stability** answers the question: *"Is the car still on the road?"* — Yes or No.

**Relative stability** answers: *"How far is the car from the cliff edge?"* — that extra distance is your **safety margin**.

- **Gain Margin (GM)** is like the *extra distance* between the car's wheels and the cliff edge — if a sudden wind gust increases your speed (gain), how much more can you tolerate before falling off?
- **Phase Margin (PM)** is like the *extra turning angle* the steering wheel has before the wheels lock and the car skids off the cliff.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Examiner Favorites):** A well-designed practical control system targets:
> - **GM ≥ 6 dB** (gain can be doubled before instability)
> - **PM = 30° to 60°** (typically **45°** is the golden design target for good transient response)

A large margin = safe but sluggish response. A small margin = fast but dangerously close to instability. **Engineering is the art of choosing the optimum trade-off.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Five Logical Steps

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Open-Loop Transfer Function:** Start with $G(s)H(s)$ of the unity (or non-unity) feedback system.
2. **Step 2 — Substitute $s = j\omega$:** Replace $s$ with $j\omega$ to obtain the frequency response $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$.
3. **Step 3 — Find $\omega_{pc}$:** Solve $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$.
4. **Step 4 — Compute GM:** Evaluate $\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert$ and take reciprocal. Convert to dB if required.
5. **Step 5 — Find $\omega_{gc}$ and PM:** Solve $\left\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \right\vert = 1$ (0 dB), then compute $\text{PM} = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$.

### 2.2 Why Both GM and PM?

A single margin is **insufficient** because:

- A system may have infinite GM but finite PM (or vice versa).
- Real-world disturbances affect **both gain and phase** simultaneously (component aging, temperature drift, parameter variations).
- Both metrics must be examined for a comprehensive robustness picture.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Boundary Condition** | **Typical Engineering Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase Crossover Frequency | $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$ | $\omega_{pc} > 0$ | rad/sec |
| Gain Crossover Frequency | $\left\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \right\vert = 1$ | $\omega_{gc} > 0$ | rad/sec |
| Gain Margin (linear) | $\text{GM} = \dfrac{1}{\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert}$ | Stable: GM $>$ 1 | dimensionless |
| Gain Margin (dB) | $\text{GM}_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert$ | Stable: GM$_{dB}$ $>$ 0 | decibels (dB) |
| Phase Margin | $\text{PM} = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ | Stable: PM $>$ 0° | degrees (°) |
| Resonance Peak $M_r$ vs PM | $M_r \approx \dfrac{1}{\sin(\text{PM})}$ for $0° < \text{PM} < 90°$ | Valid approx | dimensionless |
| Damping Ratio vs PM | $\zeta \approx \dfrac{\text{PM}}{100°}$ (valid for $0 < \zeta < 0.7$) | Empirically valid | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Trap (Frequently Tested):**
> - If $\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ is given as a *negative* value like $-125°$, then $\text{PM} = 180° + (-125°) = +55°$ (positive → stable).
> - If the expression returns a *negative* PM, the system is **unstable**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace flight control systems** rely on PM ≥ 45° to handle atmospheric turbulence without pilot-induced oscillations.
- **DC-DC power converters** in electric vehicles use GM ≥ 10 dB to survive load transients.
- **Robotic manipulator servos** maintain PM ≈ 60° to prevent overshoot and mechanical fatigue.
- **Analog op-amp circuits** are designed with phase margin so that no parasitic capacitance can push them into oscillation.

In every modern feedback amplifier, IC chip, and embedded motor controller, **gain and phase margins are the universal language of robust design.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation: Complete GM and PM Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU Standard):**
A unity-feedback system has the open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$$

Determine the **Gain Margin**, **Phase Margin**, **Gain Crossover Frequency**, and **Phase Crossover Frequency**. Comment on the stability of the closed-loop system.

---

#### Step 1: Convert to Frequency Response

Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(j\omega+2)(j\omega+5)}$$

#### Step 2: Separate Magnitude and Phase

$$\left\vert G(j\omega) \right\vert = \frac{10}{\omega\sqrt{4+\omega^{2}}\sqrt{25+\omega^{2}}}$$

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{5}\right)$$

> The −90° comes from the $j\omega$ term in the denominator.

---

#### Step 3: Find the Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{pc}$

Set the phase equal to −180°:

$$-90° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = -180°$$

$$\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = 90°$$

Apply the identity $\tan(A+B) \to \infty$ implies $\tan A \cdot \tan B = 1$:

$$\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right)\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) = 1$$

$$\frac{\omega_{pc}^{2}}{10} = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{pc}^{2} = 10$$

$$\boxed{\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 \text{ rad/sec}}$$

---

#### Step 4: Calculate Gain Margin (GM)

Evaluate the magnitude at $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10}$:

$$\left\vert G(j\sqrt{10}) \right\vert = \frac{10}{\sqrt{10}\cdot\sqrt{4+10}\cdot\sqrt{25+10}}$$

$$= \frac{10}{\sqrt{10}\cdot\sqrt{14}\cdot\sqrt{35}}$$

$$= \frac{10}{\sqrt{10 \times 14 \times 35}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{4900}} = \frac{10}{70} = \frac{1}{7}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{GM} = \frac{1}{\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert} = 7 \quad \text{(linear)}$$

$$\text{GM}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(7) \approx 16.90 \text{ dB}$$

---

#### Step 5: Find the Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$

Set magnitude equal to 1:

$$\frac{10}{\omega_{gc}\sqrt{4+\omega_{gc}^{2}}\sqrt{25+\omega_{gc}^{2}}} = 1$$

Square both sides:

$$\omega_{gc}^{2}(4+\omega_{gc}^{2})(25+\omega_{gc}^{2}) = 100$$

Let $x = \omega_{gc}^{2}$. Then:

$$x(4+x)(25+x) = 100$$

$$x(x^{2} + 29x + 100) = 100$$

$$x^{3} + 29x^{2} + 100x - 100 = 0$$

Solve numerically by trial:

| Trial $x$ | Computation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| $x = 0.80$ | $0.512 + 18.56 + 80 - 100$ | $-0.928$ |
| $x = 0.81$ | $0.531 + 19.02 + 81 - 100$ | $+0.551$ |
| $x = 0.808$ | $0.527 + 18.96 + 80.8 - 100$ | $+0.290$ |
| $x = 0.804$ | $0.519 + 18.74 + 80.4 - 100$ | $-0.339$ |

Interpolating linearly:

$$x \approx 0.806 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{gc} = \sqrt{0.806} \approx 0.898 \text{ rad/sec}$$

---

#### Step 6: Calculate Phase Margin (PM)

Evaluate the phase angle at $\omega_{gc} = 0.898$ rad/sec:

$$\angle G(j0.898) = -90° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.898}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.898}{5}\right)$$

$$= -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.449) - \tan^{-1}(0.1796)$$

$$= -90° - 24.20° - 10.19°$$

$$= -124.39°$$

Therefore:

$$\text{PM} = 180° + (-124.39°) = 55.61°$$

---

#### Step 7: Stability Conclusion

| **Parameter** | **Value** | **Status** |
|---|---|---|
| Phase Crossover $\omega_{pc}$ | 3.162 rad/sec | — |
| Gain Crossover $\omega_{gc}$ | 0.898 rad/sec | $\omega_{gc} < \omega_{pc}$ ✓ |
| Gain Margin (linear) | 7 | $> 1$ ✓ |
| Gain Margin (dB) | 16.90 dB | $> 0$ dB ✓ |
| Phase Margin | 55.61° | $> 0°$ ✓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Verdict:** The closed-loop system is **absolutely stable** and **relatively well-damped** with a comfortable safety margin (PM ≈ 55°). The system will exhibit a well-damped transient response with acceptable overshoot.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Margins on Time Response

| **PM** | **Damping Ratio $\zeta$ (approx.)** | **% Overshoot** | **Transient Character** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0° | 0 | > 80% | Sustained oscillation |
| 15° | 0.15 | ~ 60% | Highly oscillatory |
| 30° | 0.30 | ~ 35% | Underdamped |
| 45° | 0.42 | ~ 23% | Moderately damped (typical design) |
| 60° | 0.55 | ~ 10% | Well-damped |
| 75° | 0.70 | ~ 2% | Overdamped, sluggish |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **highly valued** in KTU 14-mark derivations, where students are asked to relate frequency-domain margins to time-domain specifications.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Procedure Flowchart — Computing GM and PM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Step A: Obtain open-loop transfer function G of s H of s"] --> stepB["Step B: Substitute s equals j omega"]
    stepB --> stepC["Step C: Find phase crossover omega pc by setting phase equal to minus 180 degrees"]
    stepB --> stepD["Step D: Find gain crossover omega gc by setting magnitude equal to 0 dB"]
    stepC --> stepE["Step E: Compute Gain Margin GM equals 1 divided by magnitude at omega pc"]
    stepD --> stepF["Step F: Compute Phase Margin PM equals 180 plus phase at omega gc"]
    stepE --> stepG{"GM greater than 0 dB AND PM greater than 0 degrees"}
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG -->|YES| stepH["System is STABLE with good relative stability margins"]
    stepG -->|NO| stepI["System is UNSTABLE or MARGINALLY STABLE — Redesign required"]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Stability Margins

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref["Reference Input R of s"] --> sumNode{"Summing Junction"}
    sumNode --> controller["Controller Gc of s"]
    controller --> plant["Plant G of s"]
    plant --> outNode["Output Y of s"]
    outNode --> sensor["Feedback Sensor H of s"]
    sensor --> sumNode
    plant -.-> branch1["Frequency Response G of j omega H of j omega"]
    branch1 --> plotNode["Bode or Nyquist Plot"]
    plotNode --> gmExtract["Extract GM at omega pc where phase equals minus 180"]
    plotNode --> pmExtract["Extract PM at omega gc where magnitude equals 0 dB"]
    gmExtract --> stabCheck{"Stability Check"}
    pmExtract --> stabCheck
    stabCheck -->|Both Positive| safe["Safe Operating Region"]
    stabCheck -->|Either Negative| danger["Critical Instability Region"]
```

### 4.3 Region Map — Stability Zones in the GM–PM Plane

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    regionA["Region 1: GM greater than 6 dB and PM between 30 to 60 degrees — OPTIMUM DESIGN ZONE"]
    regionB["Region 2: GM greater than 0 but PM less than 30 degrees — UNDERDAMPED oscillatory"]
    regionC["Region 3: PM greater than 0 but GM less than 0 dB — UNSTABLE in gain sense"]
    regionD["Region 4: GM less than 0 dB and PM less than 0 degrees — FULLY UNSTABLE"]
    regionE["Region 5: PM greater than 60 degrees — OVERDAMPED sluggish response"]
    regionA --> regionB
    regionB --> regionC
    regionC --> regionD
    regionB --> regionE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** A well-designed system always lies in **Region 1** (OPTIMUM DESIGN ZONE). Drifting into Region 4 means the closed-loop poles have migrated to the right-half of the s-plane — the system will oscillate with growing amplitude and likely destroy itself or the plant.
