## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define Gain Margin and Phase Margin. Explain their physical significance in determining the relative stability of a control system.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
**Gain Margin (GM)** is the factor by which the open-loop gain can be increased at the phase crossover frequency ($\omega_{pc}$, where phase = −180°) before the closed-loop system reaches the verge of instability.

$$\text{GM} = \frac{1}{\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert}$$

**Phase Margin (PM)** is the additional phase lag, measured at the gain crossover frequency ($\omega_{gc}$, where magnitude = 1), required to bring the open-loop phase to −180°.

$$\text{PM} = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

**Physical significance:** A system with a **larger GM** can tolerate greater parameter variations (such as amplifier gain drift) before becoming unstable. A **larger PM** indicates lower overshoot and better damping in the transient response. Together they quantify how robustly the system remains stable under disturbances and component aging.

---

**Q2. A unity-feedback system has GM = 8 dB and PM = 25°. Comment on its stability and suggest whether the design is acceptable.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
- Since **GM = 8 dB > 0 dB** and **PM = 25° > 0°**, the closed-loop system is **absolutely stable**.
- However, a PM of 25° is **below the typical design target** of 30°–60°. The system will exhibit **noticeable oscillatory behaviour** with an estimated overshoot of approximately 40–50%.
- The **GM of 8 dB** is acceptable (above the 6 dB practical minimum).
- **Conclusion:** The design is stable but **not robustly damped**; reducing the gain or adding a phase-lead compensator is recommended to raise the PM to about 45° for smoother transient behaviour.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> **[KTU End Semester Examination — Module 3 Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A** `[14 Marks]`

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expressions for **Gain Margin** and **Phase Margin** starting from the open-loop frequency response function. Sketch a polar (Nyquist) plot marking both quantities clearly. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

Consider a unity-feedback closed-loop system with open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$. The closed-loop characteristic equation is:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad G(s)H(s) = -1$$

In the frequency domain:

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -1 + j0$$

This means the Nyquist plot must pass through the **critical point (-1, j0)** for the system to be marginally stable. To quantify the *proximity* to this point, two measures are defined.

**Derivation of Gain Margin:**

Let $\omega_{pc}$ be the frequency where $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$. At this frequency:

$$G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert + j0$$

The distance from the origin to the Nyquist curve at $\omega_{pc}$ is $\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert$. The **Gain Margin** is defined as the reciprocal of this magnitude:

$$\boxed{\text{GM} = \frac{1}{\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert}}$$

In decibels:

$$\boxed{\text{GM}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert}\right) = -20\log_{10}\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert}$$

**Derivation of Phase Margin:**

Let $\omega_{gc}$ be the frequency where $\left\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \right\vert = 1$. At this frequency, the Nyquist curve crosses the unit circle. The angle made with the negative real axis is:

$$\theta = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

This angle is defined as the **Phase Margin**:

$$\boxed{\text{PM} = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})}$$

**Nyquist Sketch Description:**

```
                 Im axis
                   |
                   |
   Im<0 -------- (Origin) -------- Im>0
                   |
        (-1,0) ← Critical Point
        ↑       ↗
        GM      PM arc (angle measured from negative real axis)
                   |
                 Im<0
```

The distance from the (-1,0) point to the magnitude intercept at $\omega_{pc}$ along the negative real axis represents GM; the angular sweep from the negative real axis to the magnitude intercept at $\omega_{gc}$ represents PM.

---

**(b)** For the open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$, determine the **Gain Margin, Phase Margin, gain crossover frequency, and phase crossover frequency**. State whether the closed-loop system is stable. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:** *(Complete derivation as detailed in Section 3.1)*

**Step 1:** Frequency response:

$$\left\vert G(j\omega) \right\vert = \frac{10}{\omega\sqrt{4+\omega^{2}}\sqrt{25+\omega^{2}}}, \quad \angle G(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{5}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Phase crossover — solving $\angle G(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$:

$$\omega_{pc}^{2} = 10 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{pc} = 3.162 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 3:** Gain margin:

$$\left\vert G(j\sqrt{10}) \right\vert = \frac{10}{\sqrt{10}\cdot\sqrt{14}\cdot\sqrt{35}} = \frac{1}{7}$$

$$\text{GM} = 7 \quad (\text{linear}) \quad \text{or} \quad \text{GM}_{dB} = 16.90 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4:** Gain crossover — solving $\left\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \right\vert = 1$ numerically:

$$\omega_{gc}^{2}(4+\omega_{gc}^{2})(25+\omega_{gc}^{2}) = 100 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{gc} \approx 0.898 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 5:** Phase margin:

$$\angle G(j0.898) = -90° - 24.20° - 10.19° = -124.39°$$

$$\text{PM} = 180° + (-124.39°) = 55.61°$$

**Step 6:** Since $\text{GM}_{dB} = 16.90 \text{ dB} > 0$ and $\text{PM} = 55.61° > 0°$, the **closed-loop system is stable** with good relative stability.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[14 Marks]`

**(a)** Discuss how the concept of **relative stability** differs from absolute stability. Explain how the **Nyquist criterion** is used to assess relative stability using GM and PM. Draw a labelled Nyquist plot showing a stable system, a marginally stable system, and an unstable system. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Absolute Stability** is a binary classification — the system is either stable (all closed-loop poles in the left-half s-plane) or unstable.

**Relative Stability** is a quantitative measure — it tells us *how far* the system is from the stability boundary. It is expressed through **Gain Margin (GM)** and **Phase Margin (PM)**, both of which depend on how closely the Nyquist plot approaches the critical point (-1, j0).

**Nyquist criterion recap:** The number of closed-loop poles in the right-half plane is:

$$N = Z - P$$

where $P$ = number of open-loop RHP poles, $Z$ = encirclements of (-1, j0) by the Nyquist plot (clockwise positive).

**Three Cases on the Nyquist Plot:**

| **Case** | **Nyquist Plot Behaviour** | **GM** | **PM** | **System Status** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stable | Does **not** encircle (-1, j0); plot stays well away from it | GM > 0 dB, PM > 0° | Large | Stable |
| Marginally Stable | Passes **exactly** through (-1, j0) | GM = 0 dB, PM = 0° | Zero | Sustained oscillation |
| Unstable | Encircles (-1, j0) | GM < 0 dB or PM < 0° | Negative | Unstable, diverging oscillation |

The **farther** the Nyquist plot stays from (-1, j0), the **greater** the relative stability margins.

---

**(b)** A unity-feedback system has $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(0.1s+1)(s+1)}$. Find the value of **K** such that the Gain Margin is **20 dB**. Determine the corresponding Phase Margin. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Open-loop frequency response:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K}{j\omega(1+j0.1\omega)(1+j\omega)}$$

Magnitude and phase:

$$\left\vert G(j\omega) \right\vert = \frac{K}{\omega\sqrt{1+0.01\omega^{2}}\sqrt{1+\omega^{2}}}$$

$$\angle G(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega) - \tan^{-1}(\omega)$$

**Step 2:** Phase crossover frequency:

Set phase = −180°:

$$\tan^{-1}(0.1\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(\omega_{pc}) = 90°$$

Applying $\tan A \cdot \tan B = 1$:

$$(0.1\omega_{pc})(\omega_{pc}) = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 0.1\omega_{pc}^{2} = 1$$

$$\omega_{pc}^{2} = 10 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 3:** Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$:

$$\left\vert G(j\sqrt{10}) \right\vert = \frac{K}{\sqrt{10}\cdot\sqrt{1+0.1}\cdot\sqrt{1+10}} = \frac{K}{\sqrt{10}\cdot\sqrt{1.1}\cdot\sqrt{11}}$$

$$= \frac{K}{\sqrt{10 \times 1.1 \times 11}} = \frac{K}{\sqrt{121}} = \frac{K}{11}$$

**Step 4:** Apply GM = 20 dB:

$$\text{GM}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{11}{K}\right) = 20$$

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{11}{K}\right) = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{11}{K} = 10$$

$$\boxed{K = 1.1}$$

**Step 5:** Find $\omega_{gc}$ for $K = 1.1$:

$$\left\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \right\vert = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{gc}^{2}(1+0.01\omega_{gc}^{2})(1+\omega_{gc}^{2}) = K^{2} = 1.21$$

Let $x = \omega_{gc}^{2}$:

$$x(1+0.01x)(1+x) = 1.21$$

Trial $x = 1$: $1 \times 1.01 \times 2 = 2.02$ (too high)
Trial $x = 0.6$: $0.6 \times 1.006 \times 1.6 = 0.966$ (slightly low)
Trial $x = 0.75$: $0.75 \times 1.0075 \times 1.75 = 1.323$ (high)
Trial $x = 0.68$: $0.68 \times 1.0068 \times 1.68 = 1.150$
Trial $x = 0.71$: $0.71 \times 1.0071 \times 1.71 = 1.223$
Trial $x = 0.705$: ≈ 1.205
Trial $x = 0.708$: ≈ 1.215

$$\omega_{gc} \approx \sqrt{0.708} \approx 0.841 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 6:** Phase at $\omega_{gc} = 0.841$:

$$\angle G(j0.841) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.0841) - \tan^{-1}(0.841)$$

$$= -90° - 4.81° - 40.07° = -134.88°$$

$$\text{PM} = 180° - 134.88° = \boxed{45.12°}$$

> The system with $K = 1.1$ has GM = 20 dB and PM ≈ 45° — a textbook **optimally designed** control system.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (as seen in KTU valuation scripts):**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in PM:** Students frequently write $\text{PM} = 180° - \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ instead of the correct $\text{PM} = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$. Since $\angle G$ is negative, dropping the plus sign gives an erroneously larger PM. **Always include the "+" sign.**
>
> 2. **Linear vs dB Confusion:** GM is dimensionless (linear ratio), but examiners specifically request dB in many questions. **Convert using $20\log_{10}(\text{GM})$ and label the unit.**
>
> 3. **Solving $\tan^{-1}$ Equations:** When $\tan^{-1}A + \tan^{-1}B = 90°$, students incorrectly use the addition formula $\tan(A+B)$ — remember that for the sum to equal 90°, the denominator must be zero, leading to the elegant identity $A \cdot B = 1$.
>
> 4. **Skipping the Conclusion:** Failing to write a final one-line statement of stability (e.g., *"The system is stable since GM > 0 dB and PM > 0°"*) costs **2 marks** even if all numerical work is correct.
>
> 5. **Mixing $\omega_{gc}$ and $\omega_{pc}$:** Some students compute GM at $\omega_{gc}$ and PM at $\omega_{pc}$. **Memorize:** GM uses $\omega_{pc}$ (phase = −180°), PM uses $\omega_{gc}$ (magnitude = 0 dB).

---

### 📝 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Gain Margin (GM):** Reciprocal of magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ where phase = −180°. Stable if GM > 1 (or GM_dB > 0 dB).
- [x] **Phase Margin (PM):** $180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ where magnitude = 1. Stable if PM > 0°.
- [x] **Stability Condition (Minimum-Phase):** Both GM > 0 dB AND PM > 0° required.
- [x] **Design Targets:** GM ≥ 6 dB, PM = 30°–60° (ideal ≈ 45°).
- [x] **GM → Time Domain:** Larger GM = greater robustness to gain variations.
- [x] **PM → Time Domain:** $\text{PM} \approx 100\zeta$ for typical second-order-like response.
- [x] **Nyquist Link:** Distance and angle from the (-1, j0) critical point on the Nyquist plot directly correspond to GM (distance) and PM (angle).
- [x] **Bode Link:** On a Bode magnitude plot, GM is the *vertical distance* of the magnitude curve from 0 dB at $\omega_{pc}$; PM is the *vertical distance* of the phase curve from −180° at $\omega_{gc}$.
- [x] **Higher-Order Caveat:** GM and PM are *approximate* indicators for higher-order systems; exact stability requires Routh-Hurwitz or root-locus verification.
- [x] **Non-Minimum Phase Warning:** For non-minimum-phase systems, the Nyquist encoding sign convention reverses — GM and PM alone are insufficient; the full Nyquist encirclement count must be examined.
- [x] **Crossover Frequency Relationship:** For a stable system, **always** $\omega_{gc} < \omega_{pc}$. Reversing this inequality is an automatic red flag for instability.
- [x] **Formula Shortcuts:**
  - $\text{GM}_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\left\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \right\vert$
  - $M_r \approx \dfrac{1}{\sin(\text{PM})}$ (resonant peak approximation)
  - $\zeta \approx \dfrac{\text{PM (in degrees)}}{100}$ for $0 < \zeta < 0.7$

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Relative Stability: Gain Margin and Phase Margin**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability Analysis with Bode Plot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT501 - LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stability Analysis with Bode Plot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Bode Plot** is a graphical frequency-response representation of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system, consisting of two semi-logarithmic plots drawn against the logarithm of angular frequency $\omega$:

1. **Magnitude Plot**: $|G(j\omega)|_{dB}$ versus $\log_{10}\omega$
2. **Phase Plot**: $\angle G(j\omega)$ versus $\log_{10}\omega$

**Stability Analysis using Bode Plot** is a frequency-domain technique that determines the **absolute stability** (via **Gain Margin**) and **relative stability** (via **Phase Margin**) of a closed-loop system by examining the open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$ evaluated on the imaginary axis $s = j\omega$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The stability of a closed-loop system is inferred from the *open-loop* frequency response. This is the foundation of the **Nyquist Stability Criterion**, of which the Bode plot is a special simplified form.

## 1.2 Two Critical Frequency Markers

> [!NOTE]
> Before analyzing stability, you **must** know these two "boundary" frequencies — they are the heart of every Bode stability problem in KTU exams.

### (a) Gain Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{gc}$)
The frequency at which the **magnitude plot crosses 0 dB**:

$$|G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})|_{dB} = 0 \text{ dB}$$

Equivalently, the magnitude ratio equals unity: $|G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})| = 1$.

### (b) Phase Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{pc}$)
The frequency at which the **phase plot crosses $-180°$**:

$$\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180° \quad (\text{or equivalently } -π \text{ rad})$$

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Marathon Runner" Picture

Imagine a control system as a **marathon runner** trying to keep pace with a target.

- The **Magnitude Plot** is the runner's **speed log** — how fast the runner is moving at every moment.
- The **Phase Plot** is the runner's **angle of lean** — how tilted (lagging or leading) the runner is relative to the target.

| Situation | Speed Condition | Lean Condition | Verdict |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|---------|
| Runner crosses speed = 1 (0 dB) while still leaning **forward** (phase > $-180°$) | Critical speed reached | Hasn't tipped over | ✅ **Stable** (Positive PM) |
| Runner crosses speed = 1 while already leaning **backward past the danger line** (phase < $-180°$) | Critical speed reached | Already toppling | ❌ **Unstable** (Negative PM) |
| Runner tips over the danger line (phase = $-180°$) while still running **slow** (magnitude < 1) | Tipped, but speed too low to cause damage | Destructive tilt | ✅ **Conditionally stable** (Positive GM) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Margins tell two different stories:**
> - **Gain Margin (GM)** → "How much can I *amplify* the system before it goes unstable?"
> - **Phase Margin (PM)** → "How much extra *delay/phase lag* can the system tolerate before going unstable?"

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Standard Stability Conditions

For a **minimum-phase** system with open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$:

> **Condition 1:** The closed-loop system is **stable** if and only if:
> $$\boxed{GM > 0 \text{ dB} \quad \text{AND} \quad PM > 0°}$$

> **Condition 2:** Strongly recommended design target (good relative stability):
> $$GM \geq 6 \text{ dB}, \quad PM \approx 30° \text{ to } 60°$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Start with the Open-Loop Transfer Function
Given $G(s)H(s)$ (without feedback), substitute $s = j\omega$ to obtain the **frequency response**:

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{K(1 + j\omega T_a)(1 + j\omega T_b)\cdots}{(j\omega)^n (1 + j\omega T_1)(1 + j\omega T_2)\cdots}$$

### Step 2: Compute Magnitude in Decibels
Take the log-magnitude, which **converts products into sums**:

$$|G(j\omega)H(j\omega)|_{dB} = 20\log_{10}|G(j\omega)H(j\omega)|$$

### Step 3: Compute Phase Angle in Degrees
Take the argument of the complex number:

$$\phi(\omega) = \angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\text{Im}}{\text{Re}}\right)$$

### Step 4: Identify the Corner Frequencies
Each factor $(1 + j\omega T)$ introduces a corner at $\omega = 1/T$. Before this corner, magnitude is flat (0 dB/decade); after, it slopes by $\pm 20$ dB/decade.

### Step 5: Construct Asymptotic Bode Plot
- **Low-frequency asymptote**: Set by DC gain $K$ and integrator poles $(j\omega)^{-n}$.
- **Each zero** $(1 + j\omega T_z)$: adds $+20$ dB/decade slope after $\omega = 1/T_z$.
- **Each pole** $(1 + j\omega T_p)$: adds $-20$ dB/decade slope after $\omega = 1/T_p$.

### Step 6: Find $\omega_{gc}$ and $\omega_{pc}$
- $\omega_{gc}$: Solve $|G(j\omega)H(j\omega)|_{dB} = 0$
- $\omega_{pc}$: Solve $\phi(\omega) = -180°$

### Step 7: Compute Margins
- **Gain Margin**: $GM = -|G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc})|_{dB}$ (dB)
- **Phase Margin**: $PM = 180° + \phi(\omega_{gc})$ (degrees)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Stability Analysis with Bode Plot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it covers 90% of KTU Part-B questions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | Magnitude in dB | $\vert G(j\omega)\vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega)\vert$ | dB |
| 2 | Magnitude of product | $\vert AB\vert_{dB} = \vert A\vert_{dB} + \vert B\vert_{dB}$ | dB |
| 3 | Phase of product | $\angle(AB) = \angle A + \angle B$ | degrees |
| 4 | Phase of complex conjugate | $\angle(j\omega T + 1) = \tan^{-1}(\omega T)$ | degrees |
| 5 | Gain Crossover Frequency | $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})\vert_{dB} = 0$ | rad/s |
| 6 | Phase Crossover Frequency | $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$ | rad/s |
| 7 | **Gain Margin (GM)** | $GM = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc})\vert$ | **dB** |
| 8 | Linear Gain Margin | $GM_{linear} = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc})\vert}$ | ratio |
| 9 | **Phase Margin (PM)** | $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ | **degrees** |
| 10 | Slope per pole factor | $-20$ dB/decade | per pole |
| 11 | Slope per zero factor | $+20$ dB/decade | per zero |
| 12 | Integrator $(j\omega)^{-1}$ contribution | $-20\log_{10}\omega$ magnitude, $-90°$ phase | — |
| 13 | Stability condition | $GM > 0$ dB **AND** $PM > 0°$ | — |
| 14 | Ideal design target | $GM \approx 6$ to $12$ dB, $PM \approx 30°$ to $60°$ | — |
| 15 | Maximum overshoot (approx) | $M_p \approx 0.16 + 0.4(M_r - 1)$, where $M_r \approx \dfrac{1}{\sin(PM)}$ for $2^{nd}$ order | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL PITFALL:** The vertical bar `|` symbol breaks markdown tables! In this sheet, I have used `\vert` (LaTeX absolute value) inside table cells. In your exam answers on paper, just write the absolute value symbol normally — no LaTeX needed.

## 2.3 Why Bode Plot Stability Analysis Matters in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> - **Audio amplifier design**: Designers tune feedback to ensure $PM > 45°$ to avoid audible ringing and distortion.
> - **Servo motor position control**: PM directly predicts overshoot in step response — critical for CNC machines and robotics.
> - **Aerospace flight control**: Stability must be guaranteed even with component aging (gain increases) — hence GM ≥ 6 dB safety margin.
> - **Power electronics (SMPS, inverters)**: Bode plot is used to design compensation networks (lead/lag compensators).

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Construction of Bode Plot — Slope Method

Given $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K(1 + sT_a)}{s(1 + sT_b)(1 + sT_c)^2}$, corner frequencies in ascending order are $1/T_a, 1/T_b, 1/T_c$.

| Frequency Band | Slope (dB/dec) |
|----------------|----------------|
| $\omega < 1/T_a$ | $-20$ (from $1/s$) |
| $1/T_a < \omega < 1/T_b$ | $-20 + 20 = 0$ |
| $1/T_b < \omega < 1/T_c$ | $0 - 20 = -20$ |
| $\omega > 1/T_c$ | $-20 - 20(2) = -60$ |

Phase contributions add independently — see Section 3 derivation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Gain Margin and Phase Margin

### Derivation of Phase Margin

**Start:** Phase Margin is defined as the additional phase lag (measured in degrees) that can be introduced at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ before the system becomes marginally stable.

**Step 1:** At marginal stability, the open-loop transfer function evaluated at the critical point $s = j\omega$ satisfies:

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -1$$

This means two simultaneous conditions must hold:
- Magnitude condition: $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega)\vert = 1$, i.e., $0$ dB.
- Phase condition: $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -180°$.

**Step 2:** Define the **gain crossover frequency** $\omega_{gc}$ as the frequency at which only the magnitude condition is satisfied:

$$\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})\vert_{dB} = 0 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3:** At this $\omega_{gc}$, compute the phase angle:

$$\phi_{gc} = \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

**Step 4:** The "phase distance" to the danger line of $-180°$ is:

$$\boxed{PM = 180° + \phi_{gc}}$$

The $+180°$ arises because we measure phase margin **upward** from $\phi_{gc}$ to the marginal stability phase of $-180°$. If $\phi_{gc}$ is already $-180°$, then $PM = 0°$ (marginally stable). If $\phi_{gc} > -180°$, then $PM > 0°$ (stable).

---

### Derivation of Gain Margin

**Step 1:** Define the **phase crossover frequency** $\omega_{pc}$ as the frequency at which only the phase condition is satisfied:

$$\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$$

**Step 2:** At this $\omega_{pc}$, compute the magnitude:

$$M_{pc} = \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc})\vert \quad (\text{a pure number, linear scale})$$

**Step 3:** The "magnitude distance" to the danger point of magnitude = 1 is the **linear gain margin**:

$$GM_{linear} = \frac{1}{M_{pc}}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to decibels:

$$\boxed{GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{M_{pc}}\right) = -20\log_{10}(M_{pc})}$$

If $M_{pc} < 1$, the magnitude is below 0 dB at the dangerous phase, so $GM > 0$ dB (stable). If $M_{pc} > 1$, the system is unstable.

---

### Detailed Worked Example (The "Mother of All KTU Problems")

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** Find GM, PM, and comment on stability for:
> $$G(s)H(s) = \frac{10}{s(1 + 0.1s)(1 + 0.01s)}$$

#### Step A — Substituting $s = j\omega$

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega (1 + 0.1j\omega)(1 + 0.01j\omega)}$$

#### Step B — Compute the magnitude squared

$$\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega)\vert^2 = \frac{100}{\omega^2 (1 + 0.01\omega^2)(1 + 0.0001\omega^2)}$$

#### Step C — Magnitude in dB

$$\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega)\vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(10) - 20\log_{10}(\omega) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.01\omega^2) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.0001\omega^2)$$

This simplifies to:

$$\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega)\vert_{dB} = 20 - 20\log_{10}(\omega) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.01\omega^2) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.0001\omega^2)$$

#### Step D — Find Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$

We need $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega)\vert_{dB} = 0$:

$$20 - 20\log_{10}(\omega_{gc}) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.01\omega_{gc}^2) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.0001\omega_{gc}^2) = 0$$

**Try $\omega_{gc} = 10$ rad/s (a corner frequency):**

At $\omega = 10$:

$$20 - 20\log_{10}(10) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.01 \cdot 100) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.0001 \cdot 100)$$

$$= 20 - 20(1) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 1) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.01)$$

$$= 20 - 20 - 10\log_{10}(2) - 10\log_{10}(1.01)$$

$$= 0 - 10(0.3010) - 10(0.0043)$$

$$= 0 - 3.010 - 0.043$$

$$= -3.053 \text{ dB}$$

Since this is slightly negative, $\omega_{gc}$ is slightly less than 10. **Try $\omega_{gc} = 9$ rad/s:**

$$= 20 - 20\log_{10}(9) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.01 \cdot 81) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.0001 \cdot 81)$$

$$= 20 - 20(0.9542) - 10\log_{10}(1.81) - 10\log_{10}(1.0081)$$

$$= 20 - 19.084 - 10(0.2577) - 10(0.0035)$$

$$= 20 - 19.084 - 2.577 - 0.035$$

$$= -1.696 \text{ dB}$$

**Try $\omega_{gc} = 8$ rad/s:**

$$= 20 - 20\log_{10}(8) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.64) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.0064)$$

$$= 20 - 20(0.9031) - 10(0.3802) - 10(0.0028)$$

$$= 20 - 18.062 - 3.802 - 0.028$$

$$= -1.892 \text{ dB}$$

**Try $\omega_{gc} = 7.5$ rad/s:**

$$= 20 - 17.50 - 10\log_{10}(1.5625) - 10\log_{10}(1.0056)$$

$$= 20 - 17.50 - 10(0.194) - 10(0.0024)$$

$$= 20 - 17.50 - 1.94 - 0.024$$

$$= 0.536 \text{ dB}$$

**Interpolating between 7.5 and 8:**

At $\omega = 7.5$: $M = +0.536$ dB
At $\omega = 8.0$: $M = -1.892$ dB

$$\omega_{gc} \approx 7.5 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{0.536}{0.536 + 1.892} \approx 7.5 + 0.5(0.221) \approx 7.61 \text{ rad/s}$$

So $\omega_{gc} \approx 7.6$ rad/s.

#### Step E — Compute Phase at $\omega_{gc}$

$$\phi(\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.01\omega)$$

At $\omega_{gc} = 7.6$ rad/s:

$$\phi_{gc} = -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.76) - \tan^{-1}(0.076)$$

$$\phi_{gc} = -90° - 37.23° - 4.35°$$

$$\phi_{gc} = -131.58°$$

#### Step F — Phase Margin

$$PM = 180° + \phi_{gc} = 180° + (-131.58°) = \boxed{48.42°}$$

Since $PM > 0°$, the system is **stable**.

#### Step G — Find Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{pc}$

Set $\phi(\omega) = -180°$:

$$-90° - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}(0.01\omega_{pc}) = -180°$$

$$\tan^{-1}(0.1\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(0.01\omega_{pc}) = 90°$$

Using the identity $\tan^{-1}(A) + \tan^{-1}(B) = 90°$ when $AB = 1$:

$$(0.1\omega_{pc})(0.01\omega_{pc}) = 1 \implies 0.001\omega_{pc}^2 = 1 \implies \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{1000} \approx 31.62 \text{ rad/s}$$

#### Step H — Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$

At $\omega = 31.62$:

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc})\vert_{dB} = 20 - 20\log_{10}(31.62) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.01 \cdot 1000) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.0001 \cdot 1000)$$

$$= 20 - 20(1.5) - 10\log_{10}(11) - 10\log_{10}(2)$$

$$= 20 - 30 - 10(1.0414) - 10(0.3010)$$

$$= -10 - 10.414 - 3.010$$

$$= -23.42 \text{ dB}$$

#### Step I — Gain Margin

$$GM = -(-23.42) = \boxed{23.42 \text{ dB}}$$

Since $GM > 0$ dB, the system is **stable** (consistent with PM result).

#### Step J — Verdict

| Parameter | Value | Stability |
|-----------|-------|-----------|
| $PM$ | $48.42°$ | ✅ Well above 0° |
| $GM$ | $23.42$ dB | ✅ Above 6 dB target |
| **Overall** | **Stable with good relative stability** | ✅ |

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of System Modifications

| Modification | Effect on $\omega_{gc}$ | Effect on PM | Effect on GM | Typical Use |
|--------------|------------------------|--------------|--------------|-------------|
| Increase gain $K$ | ↑ (shifts magnitude up) | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases | Speed up response (cautiously) |
| Add integrator ($1/s$) | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases | Eliminate steady-state error |
| Add lead compensator ($1 + aTs)/(1 + Ts)$, $a > 1$ | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | ↓ Slight decrease | Improve transient response |
| Add lag compensator | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Small increase | ↑ Increases | Reduce steady-state error |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow — Bode Plot Stability Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Open-Loop TF G s H s] --> B[Substitute s equals j omega]
    B --> C[Compute Magnitude in dB]
    B --> D[Compute Phase in degrees]
    C --> E[Plot Magnitude vs log omega]
    D --> F[Plot Phase vs log omega]
    E --> G[Find omega_gc: Magnitude equals 0 dB]
    F --> H[Find omega_pc: Phase equals minus 180 deg]
    G --> I[Compute PM equals 180 deg plus Phase at omega_gc]
    H --> J[Compute GM equals minus Magnitude dB at omega_pc]
    I --> K{Check PM greater than 0?}
    J --> L{Check GM greater than 0 dB?}
    K -->|Yes| M[Phase Stable]
    K -->|No| N[Phase Unstable]
    L -->|Yes| O[Gain Stable]
    L -->|No| P[Gain Unstable]
    M --> Q{Both Stable?}
    O --> Q
    N --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q -->|Yes| R[Closed Loop System is STABLE]
    Q -->|No| S[Closed Loop System is UNSTABLE]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bode Plot Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input: Open Loop TF]
        A1[Numerator Coefficients]
        A2[Denominator Coefficients]
        A3[Corner Frequencies]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer[Frequency Domain Processing]
        B1[Substitute s equals j omega]
        B2[Convert to Polar Form]
        B3[Apply 20 log10 to Magnitude]
        B4[Extract Argument for Phase]
    end

    subgraph PlottingLayer[Asymptotic Plot Generation]
        C1[Low Frequency Slope from K and integrators]
        C2[Apply Corner Frequency Slopes plus minus 20 dB per decade]
        C3[Correct with 3 dB and 45 deg at each corner]
    end

    subgraph AnalysisLayer[Stability Metric Extraction]
        D1[Identify omega_gc where magnitude crosses 0 dB]
        D2[Identify omega_pc where phase crosses minus 180 deg]
        D3[Calculate PM equals 180 plus phase at omega_gc]
        D4[Calculate GM equals minus magnitude dB at omega_pc]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Final Output]
        E1[Stability Verdict]
        E2[Recommended Compensator if needed]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> C2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B2 --> B4
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    B4 --> D1
    B4 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D4
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Margin Visualization on Bode Plot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph MagPlot[Magnitude Plot in dB vs log omega]
        M1[0 dB Axis horizontal line]
        M2[Magnitude Curve descending from K at low omega]
        M3[omega_gc point: Magnitude Curve crosses 0 dB]
    end

    subgraph PhasePlot[Phase Plot in deg vs log omega]
        P1[minus 180 deg Axis horizontal line]
        P2[Phase Curve descending from 0 deg or minus 90 deg]
        P3[omega_pc point: Phase Curve crosses minus 180 deg]
    end

    M3 -->|Read phase here| PMcalc[PM equals 180 plus Phase at omega_gc]
    P3 -->|Read magnitude here| GMcalc[GM equals minus Magnitude dB at omega_pc]

    PMcalc --> Final[System is Stable if PM greater than 0 and GM greater than 0 dB]
    GMcalc --> Final
```

## 4.4 Stability Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{PM greater than 0 deg?} -->|Yes| R1[Phase OK]
    Q1 -->|No| R2[Phase FAIL]
    S1{GM greater than 0 dB?} -->|Yes| T1[Gain OK]
    S1 -->|No| T2[Gain FAIL]
    R1 --> Z{Combine}
    T1 --> Z
    R2 --> Z
    T2 --> Z
    Z -->|Both OK| Result1[STABLE]
    Z -->|Either FAIL| Result2[UNSTABLE]
    Z -->|Both FAIL| Result3[Definitely UNSTABLE]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q:** Define Gain Margin (GM) and Phase Margin (PM) with reference to Bode plot. What are their typical desired values for a well-designed control system?

**Model Answer:**

**Gain Margin (GM):** It is defined as the amount of gain (in dB) by which the open-loop gain can be increased at the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$ before the closed-loop system becomes marginally unstable.

$$GM = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc})\vert \quad \text{(in dB)}$$

**Phase Margin (PM):** It is defined as the additional phase lag (in degrees) that can be introduced at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ before the closed-loop system becomes marginally unstable.

$$PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

**Typical desired values:**
- $PM \approx 30°$ to $60°$ (commonly $45°$)
- $GM \geq 6$ dB (commonly 8 to 12 dB)

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q:** Distinguish between Gain Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{gc}$) and Phase Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{pc}$).

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | $\omega_{gc}$ | $\omega_{pc}$ |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Definition | Frequency where magnitude = 0 dB | Frequency where phase = $-180°$ |
| Mathematical condition | $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})\vert_{dB} = 0$ | $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$ |
| Used to find | Phase Margin (PM) | Gain Margin (GM) |
| Typical magnitude behavior | Always exists if system type ≤ 1 | Exists only if phase actually reaches $-180°$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Examination — Module 3 Style]**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q:** For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function
$$G(s) = \frac{20}{s(1 + 0.5s)(1 + 0.05s)}$$
**(a)** Sketch the Bode plot and determine the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ and phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$.
**(b)** Calculate the Gain Margin and Phase Margin. Comment on the stability of the closed-loop system.

#### Model Solution

**Given:**
$$G(s) = \frac{20}{s(1 + 0.5s)(1 + 0.05s)}$$

**Type:** Type-1 system (one pole at origin). Corner frequencies: $\omega_1 = 2$ rad/s, $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s.

**Part (a) — Bode Plot Construction (7 marks)**

**Magnitude:**
$$\vert G(j\omega)\vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(20) - 20\log_{10}(\omega) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.25\omega^2) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.0025\omega^2)$$

$$\vert G(j\omega)\vert_{dB} = 26.02 - 20\log_{10}(\omega) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.25\omega^2) - 10\log_{10}(1 + 0.0025\omega^2)$$

**Slope Table:**

| Frequency Band | Slope (dB/decade) |
|----------------|-------------------|
| $\omega < 2$ | $-20$ |
| $2 < \omega < 20$ | $-40$ |
| $\omega > 20$ | $-60$ |

**Magnitude at corners:**
- At $\omega = 0.1$: $M = 26.02 - 20\log_{10}(0.1) = 26.02 + 20 = 46.02$ dB
- At $\omega = 2$ (just before corner): $M = 26.02 - 20\log_{10}(2) = 26.02 - 6.02 = 20$ dB
- At $\omega = 2$ (just after, corrected): $M \approx 20 - 3 = 17$ dB
- At $\omega = 20$ (just before): $M = 17 - 40\log_{10}(20/2) = 17 - 40 = -23$ dB
- At $\omega = 20$ (just after, corrected): $M \approx -23 - 3 = -26$ dB

**Phase:**
$$\phi(\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.05\omega)$$

**Phase at corners:**
- At $\omega = 0.1$: $\phi = -90° - 2.86° - 0.29° = -93.15°$
- At $\omega = 2$: $\phi = -90° - 45° - 5.71° = -140.71°$
- At $\omega = 20$: $\phi = -90° - 84.29° - 45° = -219.29°$

**Phase crossover:** $\phi = -180°$ between $\omega = 2$ and $\omega = 20$.

By interpolation, $\omega_{pc} \approx 9$ rad/s.

**Gain crossover:** From magnitude table, $M = 0$ dB between $\omega = 2$ and $\omega = 20$.

Linear interpolation: $20$ dB at $\omega = 2$, $-23$ dB at $\omega = 20$:

$$\log_{10}(\omega_{gc}) = \log_{10}(2) + \frac{20}{20 + 23} \cdot \log_{10}(20/2) = 0.301 + 0.465 \cdot 1 = 0.766$$

$$\omega_{gc} \approx 10^{0.766} \approx 5.83 \text{ rad/s}$$

So $\omega_{gc} \approx 5.8$ rad/s, $\omega_{pc} \approx 9$ rad/s.

**Part (b) — Margin Calculation (7 marks)**

**Phase Margin:**
$$PM = 180° + \phi(\omega_{gc})$$

At $\omega_{gc} = 5.83$:

$$\phi(\omega_{gc}) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.5 \cdot 5.83) - \tan^{-1}(0.05 \cdot 5.83)$$

$$\phi(\omega_{gc}) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(2.915) - \tan^{-1}(0.2915)$$

$$\phi(\omega_{gc}) = -90° - 71.07° - 16.26° = -177.33°$$

$$PM = 180° + (-177.33°) = \boxed{2.67°}$$

**Gain Margin:**
$$GM = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc})\vert$$

At $\omega_{pc} = 9$:

$$\vert G(j9)\vert = \frac{20}{9\sqrt{1 + 0.25 \cdot 81}\sqrt{1 + 0.0025 \cdot 81}}$$

$$= \frac{20}{9 \cdot \sqrt{21.25} \cdot \sqrt{1.2025}}$$

$$= \frac{20}{9 \cdot 4.61 \cdot 1.0966}$$

$$= \frac{20}{45.49} = 0.4397$$

$$GM = -20\log_{10}(0.4397) = -20 \cdot (-0.3568) = \boxed{7.14 \text{ dB}}$$

**Stability Comment:**

| Parameter | Value | Verdict |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| $PM$ | $2.67°$ | ⚠️ Positive but very small — marginally stable, large overshoot expected |
| $GM$ | $7.14$ dB | ✅ Above 6 dB target |
| **Conclusion** | **Marginally stable closed-loop system** — high overshoot, oscillatory response. **Compensation recommended** to improve PM to at least $30°$. |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**Q:** Consider a unity feedback system having open-loop transfer function
$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+4)(s+10)}$$
**(a)** Find the value of $K$ for which the system is marginally stable using Bode plot analysis.
**(b)** For $K = 50$, determine the Gain Margin and Phase Margin. State whether the system is stable.

#### Model Solution

**Given:**
$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+4)(s+10)} = \frac{K}{40 \cdot s \cdot (1 + 0.25s)(1 + 0.1s)}$$

In standard time-constant form:
$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K/40}{s(1 + 0.25s)(1 + 0.1s)}$$

**Part (a) — Marginal Stability Condition (7 marks)**

For marginal stability, the phase plot must cross $-180°$ exactly at the gain crossover frequency, i.e., $PM = 0°$ at $\omega_{gc}$ where magnitude = 0 dB.

Equivalently, at $\omega = \omega_{pc}$ (where phase = $-180°$), the magnitude must be exactly 0 dB (i.e., $GM = 0$ dB).

**Step 1:** Find phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$ (independent of $K$, since $K$ only affects magnitude):

$$\phi(\omega_{pc}) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.25\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega_{pc}) = -180°$$

$$\tan^{-1}(0.25\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega_{pc}) = 90°$$

Using $\tan^{-1}(A) + \tan^{-1}(B) = 90° \iff AB = 1$:

$$(0.25\omega_{pc})(0.1\omega_{pc}) = 1$$

$$0.025\omega_{pc}^2 = 1$$

$$\omega_{pc}^2 = 40$$

$$\boxed{\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{40} = 2\sqrt{10} \approx 6.32 \text{ rad/s}}$$

**Step 2:** At marginal stability, magnitude = 0 dB at $\omega = \omega_{pc}$:

$$\left|\frac{K/40}{j\omega_{pc}(1 + j0.25\omega_{pc})(1 + j0.1\omega_{pc})}\right| = 1$$

$$\frac{K/40}{\omega_{pc}\sqrt{1 + 0.0625\omega_{pc}^2}\sqrt{1 + 0.01\omega_{pc}^2}} = 1$$

Substitute $\omega_{pc}^2 = 40$:

$$\omega_{pc}\sqrt{1 + 2.5}\sqrt{1 + 0.4} = \sqrt{40}\sqrt{3.5}\sqrt{1.4}$$

$$= \sqrt{40 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 1.4} = \sqrt{196} = 14$$

So:
$$\frac{K/40}{14} = 1 \implies K = 560$$

**Marginal stability gain:** $K_{max} = 560$.

**Part (b) — Stability Analysis for K = 50 (7 marks)**

For $K = 50$, the gain is well below 560, so we expect a stable system.

**Step 1:** Compute DC magnitude in dB:

$$\frac{K/40}{1} = \frac{50}{40} = 1.25$$

$$\text{DC magnitude (low-freq asymptote)} = 20\log_{10}(1.25) = 1.94 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2:** The phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$ is **independent of $K$**:

$$\omega_{pc} = 6.32 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ for $K = 50$:

At $\omega_{pc}$, the magnitude (excluding $K$) equals $\frac{1/40}{14} = \frac{1}{560}$. So:

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc})\vert = \frac{K}{560} = \frac{50}{560} = 0.0893$$

**Step 4:** Gain Margin:

$$GM = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{560}{50}\right) = 20\log_{10}(11.2) = 20 \cdot 1.0492$$

$$GM = \boxed{20.98 \text{ dB}}$$

**Step 5:** Find gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ (magnitude = 0 dB, i.e., $|G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})| = 1$):

$$\frac{50}{40 \cdot \omega_{gc}\sqrt{1 + 0.0625\omega_{gc}^2}\sqrt{1 + 0.01\omega_{gc}^2}} = 1$$

For low $\omega$ approximation (before corner $\omega = 4$ rad/s):

$$\frac{50}{40\omega_{gc}} = 1 \implies \omega_{gc} \approx 1.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

Refining by including the corner $\omega_1 = 4$ (since $1.25 < 4$, approximation is valid). So $\omega_{gc} \approx 1.25$ rad/s.

**Step 6:** Phase at $\omega_{gc}$:

$$\phi(\omega_{gc}) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.25 \cdot 1.25) - \tan^{-1}(0.1 \cdot 1.25)$$

$$= -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.3125) - \tan^{-1}(0.125)$$

$$= -90° - 17.35° - 7.13°$$

$$= -114.48°$$

**Step 7:** Phase Margin:

$$PM = 180° + (-114.48°) = \boxed{65.52°}$$

**Final Stability Verdict:**

| Parameter | Value | Verdict |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| $PM$ | $65.52°$ | ✅ Excellent — well above 30° |
| $GM$ | $20.98$ dB | ✅ Excellent — well above 6 dB |
| **Conclusion** | **Closed-loop system is STABLE with very good relative stability** | ✅ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Confusing $\omega_{gc}$ and $\omega_{pc}$:** Many students compute magnitude at the wrong frequency. Remember: **GM uses $\omega_{pc}$** (where phase = $-180°$), and **PM uses $\omega_{gc}$** (where magnitude = 0 dB).
> 2. **Sign error in PM formula:** PM = $180° + \phi(\omega_{gc})$ — the $+180°$ is **always positive**. If your PM comes out negative, you likely used $-180°$ instead of $+180°$.
> 3. **dB conversion error:** $\log$ base 10, multiplied by 20. Always check: $\log_{10}(10) = 1$, so $|10|_{dB} = 20$ dB (not 10).
> 4. **Ignoring the $-3$ dB correction at corners:** Asymptotic Bode plots differ from exact by $\pm 3$ dB at each corner. For exam precision, KTU accepts asymptotic answers, but you should mention this caveat for full marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the integrator contribution:** A system with $1/s$ always has $-90°$ starting phase. Two integrators give $-180°$ — instant marginal stability, no GM exists!

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — Stability Analysis with Bode Plot**

- [ ] **Open-loop transfer function** $G(s)H(s)$ is required (NOT the closed-loop).
- [ ] **Substitute $s = j\omega$** before computing magnitude and phase.
- [ ] **Magnitude in dB** = $20\log_{10}|G(j\omega)H(j\omega)|$ — convert products to sums.
- [ ] **Phase in degrees** = sum of individual phase angles.
- [ ] **Corner frequencies**: $\omega = 1/T$ for each time constant $T$.
- [ ] **Slope rules**: integrator → $-20$ dB/dec; simple pole → $-20$ dB/dec after corner; simple zero → $+20$ dB/dec after corner; double pole → $-40$ dB/dec, etc.
- [ ] **$\omega_{gc}$**: Where magnitude = **0 dB** (NOT $\omega = 1$). Used to find PM.
- [ ] **$\omega_{pc}$**: Where phase = **$-180°$**. Used to find GM.
- [ ] **PM formula**: $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$
- [ ] **GM formula**: $GM = -20\log_{10}|G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc})|$ dB
- [ ] **Stability**: $GM > 0$ dB **AND** $PM > 0°$ — both must hold simultaneously.
- [ ] **Design targets**: $PM \approx 45°$ (transient response), $GM \approx 10$ dB (robustness).
- [ ] **Type-0 system** → Bode plot starts flat, phase starts at $0°$.
- [ ] **Type-1 system** → Bode plot starts with $-20$ dB/dec slope, phase starts at $-90°$.
- [ ] **Type-2 system** → Bode plot starts with $-40$ dB/dec slope, phase starts at $-180°$ → **always marginally stable or unstable** if no zeros are added.
- [ ] **Increase $K$** → shifts magnitude plot up → $\omega_{gc}$ increases → $PM$ decreases (and $GM$ decreases).
- [ ] **Lead compensator** → increases $PM$ (improves transient response).
- [ ] **Lag compensator** → increases $GM$ (reduces steady-state error).

> **Final Tip:** In KTU exams, always **state the stability verdict explicitly** at the end of your solution — full 14 marks depend on it!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Cascade Compensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT501 - LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Need for Cascade Compensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cascade Compensation** is a frequency-domain control design technique in which an additional linear, time-invariant transfer function block — called the **compensator** $G_c(s)$ — is inserted in **series (cascade)** with the plant/process transfer function $G_p(s)$ inside the forward path of a closed-loop control system, so that the overall open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$ is reshaped to satisfy transient-response, steady-state accuracy, and stability-margin specifications simultaneously.

The complete cascade-compensated single-loop feedback system takes the canonical form:

$$
T(s) \;=\; \frac{G_c(s)\,G_p(s)}{1 \;+\; G_c(s)\,G_p(s)\,H(s)}
$$

where:
- $G_c(s)$ = transfer function of the **cascade compensator** (the newly introduced block).
- $G_p(s)$ = transfer function of the **original plant** (uncompensated process).
- $H(s)$ = transfer function of the **feedback element**.

> [!NOTE]
> The term *cascade* literally means "falling in sequence like a waterfall." In control engineering, two blocks are said to be in cascade when the output of one feeds directly into the input of the next, and their individual transfer functions **multiply** to form the equivalent block. This serial multiplication is precisely what allows $G_c(s)$ to "re-shape" the plant's dynamics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving Instructor" Model

Imagine a beginner driver (the **plant**) attempting to drive a car smoothly through a winding mountain road. The beginner, on their own, oscillates between over-steering and under-steering (poor damping), takes too long to settle into the correct lane (slow transient response), and cannot hold a steady average speed on uphill gradients (steady-state error).

A **driving instructor** sits beside the driver and **anticipatorily corrects** the steering wheel **before** the car deviates too far. The instructor doesn't replace the driver (plant) — they merely **modify** the control action the driver provides. This is exactly what a cascade compensator does:

| Driving Instructor Analogy | Control-System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Beginner driver | Plant $G_p(s)$ with poor dynamics |
| Instructor sitting beside (in series with) | Cascade compensator $G_c(s)$ |
| Steering corrections **anticipating** future deviation | **Phase-lead** action (advances phase) |
| Verbal coaching to maintain steady speed | **Phase-lag** action (boosts low-frequency gain) |
| Combined proactive coaching | **Lag-Lead compensator** |

> [!TIP]
> Think of $G_c(s)$ as a *frequency-selective amplifier*: it amplifies certain frequency bands and attenuates others. A **lead** compensator boosts high-frequency phase (speed of response + stability), while a **lag** compensator boosts low-frequency magnitude (steady-state accuracy). Together, they tame both the "wobble" and the "drift" — which is precisely **why** cascade compensation is needed.

## 1.3 The Fundamental Need — Why Not Just Increase the Loop Gain?

A common student question is: *"If performance is poor, why not just turn up the proportional gain $K$?"* This single-knob approach fails for a deep, structural reason:

> [!WARNING]
> **A single gain $K$ cannot independently control both transient response and steady-state error.** Increasing $K$ speeds up the response *and* reduces steady-state error, but it also *reduces phase margin*, potentially destabilising the system. Conversely, lowering $K$ stabilises the response but worsens accuracy. Cascade compensation introduces **frequency-dependent shaping** that decouples these conflicting objectives — something a single scalar $K$ fundamentally cannot do.

This is the **central motivation** for cascade compensation and the most heavily examined concept in KTU Module 4.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structured Reasoning — The Three Pillars of "Need"

The need for cascade compensation is justified by **three independent engineering deficiencies** observed in uncompensated feedback systems. Each deficiency maps to one or more design specifications that a simple gain cannot satisfy.

### Pillar 1 — Transient-Response Deficiency

The uncompensated closed-loop system exhibits:
- Excessive **rise time** $t_r$ (sluggish start-up).
- Large **peak overshoot** $M_p$ (oscillatory behaviour).
- Long **settling time** $t_s$ (persistent ringing).

**Root cause:** Insufficient phase margin $PM$ at the gain-crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$. The Bode plot of $G_p(j\omega)$ lacks the required phase boost.

**Compensator remedy:** A **lead compensator** $G_c(s)$ supplies a *positive phase contribution* $\phi_m$ near $\omega_{gc}$, raising $PM$ without changing low-frequency gain.

### Pillar 2 — Steady-State Accuracy Deficiency

The uncompensated system exhibits:
- Unacceptably large **position constant** $K_p$, **velocity constant** $K_v$, or **acceleration constant** $K_a$ — *equivalently*, large steady-state error $e_{ss}$.
- Insufficient **error coefficients** $K_p, K_v, K_a$.

**Root cause:** The low-frequency (DC) magnitude $\vert G_p(j0) \vert$ is too small to drive the error towards zero for the prescribed input class.

**Compensator remedy:** A **lag compensator** $G_c(s)$ introduces a pole-zero pair near the origin that *boosts low-frequency gain* by a factor of $\alpha$ without disturbing the high-frequency phase.

### Pillar 3 — Stability / Robustness Deficiency

The uncompensated system exhibits:
- Low **gain margin** $GM$ and **phase margin** $PM$.
- Closed-loop poles too close to (or outside) the unit circle / $j\omega$-axis.
- High **sensitivity** $S(s)$ to plant parameter variations.

**Root cause:** The open-loop frequency response crosses through $-1$ with the wrong slope.

**Compensator remedy:** A carefully designed lead or lag-lead compensator **re-shapes** the Nyquist / Bode contour so that it encircles $-1$ with adequate clearance.

## 2.2 Performance Objectives Mapped to Compensator Type

| Performance Objective | Specification | Required Action | Compensator Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Improve transient response | Higher $\omega_{gc}$, larger $PM$ | Add **positive phase** at $\omega_{gc}$ | **Lead** compensator |
| Reduce steady-state error | Larger $K_p, K_v$ or $K_a$ | Boost low-frequency magnitude | **Lag** compensator |
| Improve both transient & steady-state | Simultaneously raise $PM$ and $K_v$ | Phase lead near $\omega_{gc}$ + DC gain boost | **Lag-Lead** compensator |
| Reject high-frequency noise | Attenuate above desired bandwidth | Roll-off magnitude at high $\omega$ | Output RC filter / lag section |
| Robustness to plant variation | Smaller sensitivity peak $M_p$ | Reshape crossover region | **Lead** or **Lag-Lead** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula required for cascade-compensation design questions that appear in the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE. Master these before attempting any exam problem.

### 2.3.1 Steady-State Error Constants

| Error Constant | Definition (Unity Feedback) | Test Input | Steady-State Error Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Position constant $K_p$ | $K_p \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0}\, G(s)$ | Step $R(s) = \tfrac{1}{s}$ | $e_{ss} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ |
| Velocity constant $K_v$ | $K_v \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0}\, s\,G(s)$ | Ramp $R(s) = \tfrac{1}{s^2}$ | $e_{ss} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{K_v}$ |
| Acceleration constant $K_a$ | $K_a \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0}\, s^2\,G(s)$ | Parabolic $R(s) = \tfrac{1}{s^3}$ | $e_{ss} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{K_a}$ |

Here $G(s) = G_c(s)\,G_p(s)$ is the cascade-compensated open-loop transfer function.

### 2.3.2 Phase & Gain Margin Relations

| Quantity | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Gain Margin $GM$ | $GM_{dB} \;=\; -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ where $\omega_{pc}$ is phase-crossover frequency | Magnitude reserve at $\omega$ where phase $= -180^\circ$ |
| Phase Margin $PM$ | $PM \;=\; 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ where $\omega_{gc}$ is gain-crossover frequency | Phase reserve at $\omega$ where $\vert G \vert = 1$ (0 dB) |
| Maximum phase-lead $\phi_m$ | $\phi_m \;=\; \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}\right)$, with $\alpha < 1$ | Peak phase contributed by a single lead section |

### 2.3.3 Compensator Transfer Functions (Canonical Forms)

| Compensator Type | Transfer Function $G_c(s)$ | Frequency Response $G_c(j\omega)$ | Typical $\alpha$ Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lead | $G_c(s) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\alpha}\,\dfrac{s + \tfrac{1}{T}}{s + \tfrac{1}{\alpha T}}$, $\;0 < \alpha < 1$ | $G_c(j\omega) \;=\; \dfrac{1 + j\omega T}{1 + j\omega \alpha T}$ | $0.05 \leq \alpha \leq 0.2$ for $30^\circ\!-\!55^\circ$ lead |
| Lag | $G_c(s) \;=\; \dfrac{s + \tfrac{1}{T}}{s + \tfrac{1}{\beta T}}$, $\;\beta > 1$ | $G_c(j\omega) \;=\; \dfrac{1 + j\omega T}{1 + j\omega \beta T}$ | $\beta = 5$ to $20$ typically |
| Lag-Lead | $G_c(s) \;=\; G_{\text{lag}}(s)\,\cdot\,G_{\text{lead}}(s)$ | Product of above | Tuned separately |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the lead form, the zero is at $s = -\tfrac{1}{T}$ and the pole is at $s = -\tfrac{1}{\alpha T}$. Because $\alpha < 1$, the zero lies **closer to the origin** than the pole — this asymmetry is what produces the positive phase contribution. Memorise this pole-zero geometry; it is a recurring KTU question.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Cascade Compensation

Cascade compensation is the **workhorse** of classical control design across virtually every industrial sector:

- **Aerospace:** Autopilot gain-phase shaping for aircraft pitch/yaw control.
- **Robotics:** Joint-position servos where speed and accuracy must coexist.
- **Process control:** Temperature, pressure, and flow loops in chemical plants where disturbance rejection is paramount.
- **Power electronics:** Voltage regulators and motor drives requiring fast dynamic response with low DC error.
- **Automotive:** Active suspension, electronic stability control, and cruise-control throttle positioning.

In every case, the underlying *need* is identical: **decouple the conflicting demands of speed, accuracy, and stability** — a feat a single proportional gain cannot accomplish.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Cascade Compensation Decouples Transient and Steady-State Performance

Consider an uncompensated unity-feedback system with open-loop transfer function $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$, a Type-1 system.

### Step 1: Compute the uncompensated velocity constant

$$
K_v^{\text{(uncomp)}} \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0}\, s\,G_p(s) \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0}\, s \cdot \frac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)} \;=\; K
$$

So the ramp steady-state error is:

$$
e_{ss}^{\text{(uncomp)}} \;=\; \frac{1}{K_v^{\text{(uncomp)}}} \;=\; \frac{1}{K}
$$

### Step 2: Suppose the design specification demands $e_{ss} \leq 0.02$

This requires:

$$
K_v^{\text{(required)}} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.02} \;=\; 50
$$

Hence the new loop gain must satisfy $K \geq 50$.

### Step 3: Examine the transient response at $K = 50$

The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$
T(s) \;=\; \frac{50}{s^2 + s + 50}
$$

Comparing with the standard form $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2$:

$$
\omega_n \;=\; \sqrt{50} \;\approx\; 7.07 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\zeta \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{50}} \;\approx\; 0.0707
$$

### Step 4: Interpret the damping ratio

A damping ratio of $\zeta \approx 0.07$ produces severe overshoot:

$$
M_p \;=\; \exp\!\left(\frac{-\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\right) \;\approx\; \exp\!\left(\frac{-\pi(0.0707)}{\sqrt{1-0.005}}\right) \;\approx\; \exp(-0.223) \;\approx\; 0.80
$$

Thus **80% overshoot** — the system is practically unstable in a practical sense.

### Step 5: Conclude the failure of single-gain design

To meet the steady-state spec ($K \geq 50$), we are forced into a region where transient response is unacceptable. **This is the precise mathematical demonstration of why cascade compensation is needed:** a single scalar gain cannot independently satisfy both specs.

### Step 6: Show how a lag compensator resolves the conflict

Insert a lag compensator:

$$
G_c(s) \;=\; \frac{s + \tfrac{1}{T}}{s + \tfrac{1}{\beta T}}, \qquad \beta = 10
$$

At DC ($s = 0$):

$$
G_c(0) \;=\; \frac{1/T}{1/(\beta T)} \;=\; \beta \;=\; 10
$$

Hence the new velocity constant is:

$$
K_v^{\text{(comp)}} \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0}\, s\,G_c(s)\,G_p(s) \;=\; \beta \cdot K_v^{\text{(uncomp)}}
$$

If we keep $K = 5$ (preserving transient performance), the lag compensator boosts $K_v$ to $10 \times 5 = 50$, **simultaneously satisfying both specifications**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Phase Contribution of a Lead Compensator

The lead compensator is:

$$
G_c(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{1 + j\omega T}{1 + j\omega \alpha T}, \qquad 0 < \alpha < 1
$$

### Step 1: Write the magnitude and phase

$$
\vert G_c(j\omega) \vert \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{1 + (\omega T)^2}}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega \alpha T)^2}}
$$

$$
\angle G_c(j\omega) \;=\; \tan^{-1}(\omega T) \;-\; \tan^{-1}(\omega \alpha T) \;\equiv\; \phi(\omega)
$$

### Step 2: Differentiate $\phi(\omega)$ to find the maximum

$$
\frac{d\phi}{d\omega} \;=\; \frac{T}{1 + (\omega T)^2} \;-\; \frac{\alpha T}{1 + (\omega \alpha T)^2}
$$

### Step 3: Set the derivative to zero

Setting $\dfrac{d\phi}{d\omega} = 0$:

$$
\frac{T}{1 + (\omega T)^2} \;=\; \frac{\alpha T}{1 + (\omega \alpha T)^2}
$$

$$
1 + \omega^2 \alpha^2 T^2 \;=\; \alpha \left[1 + \omega^2 T^2\right]
$$

$$
1 + \alpha^2 \omega^2 T^2 \;=\; \alpha + \alpha \omega^2 T^2
$$

$$
\omega^2 T^2 (\alpha - \alpha^2) \;=\; \alpha - 1
$$

$$
\omega^2 T^2 \alpha(1 - \alpha) \;=\; -(1 - \alpha)
$$

### Step 4: Solve for the frequency of maximum phase lead

$$
\omega_m^2 T^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{\alpha}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\omega_m \;=\; \frac{1}{T\sqrt{\alpha}}\;}
$$

This is the **geometric mean** of the pole and zero frequencies — a memorable result.

### Step 5: Substitute back to find the maximum phase

$$
\phi_m \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}\right) \;-\; \tan^{-1}(\sqrt{\alpha})
$$

Using the identity $\tan^{-1}(x) - \tan^{-1}(y) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{x - y}{1 + xy}\right)$:

$$
\phi_m \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}} - \sqrt{\alpha}}{1 + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}\cdot\sqrt{\alpha}}\right) \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\tfrac{1-\alpha}{\sqrt{\alpha}}}{2}\right)
$$

### Step 6: Simplify using half-angle substitution

Let $\sqrt{\alpha} = \tan(\theta)$, then the above reduces to:

$$
\tan(\phi_m) \;=\; \frac{1 - \alpha}{2\sqrt{\alpha}} \;=\; \frac{(1-\sqrt{\alpha})(1+\sqrt{\alpha})}{2\sqrt{\alpha}}
$$

Apply the half-angle identity with $\sin^2(\phi_m/2) = \dfrac{1 - \cos\phi_m}{2}$ and after standard algebraic simplification:

$$
\boxed{\;\sin \phi_m \;=\; \frac{1 - \alpha}{1 + \alpha}\;}
$$

Equivalently, solving for $\alpha$:

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha \;=\; \frac{1 - \sin\phi_m}{1 + \sin\phi_m}\;}
$$

### Step 7: Magnitude correction at $\omega_m$

At $\omega_m = \tfrac{1}{T\sqrt{\alpha}}$, the magnitude is:

$$
\vert G_c(j\omega_m) \vert \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{1 + 1/\alpha}}{\sqrt{1 + \alpha}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}} \;\equiv\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}} \;\text{(in linear)} \;\equiv\; -\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 20\log_{10}\alpha \text{ dB}
$$

This attenuation at $\omega_m$ is called the **lead-network magnitude penalty**, and must be compensated by an upward shift of the gain $K$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Compensation Strategy Selection

| Observed Symptom | Diagnosed Root Cause | Verification Test | Cascade Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| $e_{ss}$ too large (ramp input) | $K_v$ insufficient | Compute $K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} sG(s)H(s)$ | Add **lag** section |
| Excessive overshoot $M_p$ | Phase margin $PM < 30^\circ$ | Plot Bode, read $PM$ | Add **lead** section |
| Slow rise time $t_r$ | Gain-crossover $\omega_{gc}$ too low | Read $\omega_{gc}$ from Bode | Add **lead** section (raises $\omega_{gc}$) |
| Oscillation near crossover | $PM$ negative or marginal | Check phase at $\omega_{gc}$ | **Lead** with adequate $\phi_m$ |
| High-frequency noise pickup | Insufficient high-$\omega$ roll-off | Inspect $\vert G \vert$ at high $\omega$ | Add output RC / passive lag |
| Both $e_{ss}$ and overshoot poor | Conflicting $K$ & $\zeta$ | Check $K_v$ vs. $\zeta$ simultaneously | **Lag-Lead** compensator |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Cascade-Compensated Single-Loop Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the standard architecture of a cascade-compensated feedback control system. The compensator $G_c(s)$ sits in series with the plant $G_p(s)$ in the forward path; $H(s)$ provides feedback; and $R(s)$, $E(s)$, $U(s)$, $Y(s)$, and $D(s)$ denote the reference, error, control, output, and disturbance signals respectively.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Input R s] --> SUM1{Adder}
    D[Disturbance D s] --> SUM2{Adder}
    SUM1 --> E[Error E s]
    E --> Gc[Compensator Gc s]
    Gc --> U[Control Signal U s]
    U --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> Gp[Plant Gp s]
    Gp --> Y[System Output Y s]
    Y --> H[Feedback Sensor H s]
    H --> SUM1
```

**Reading the diagram:** The reference $R(s)$ and the (negated) feedback $H(s)Y(s)$ are differenced at the first summing junction to produce the error $E(s) = R(s) - H(s)Y(s)$. The compensator $G_c(s)$ acts on $E(s)$ to produce the control signal $U(s)$, which is summed with the disturbance $D(s)$ before driving the plant $G_p(s)$. The output $Y(s)$ is sampled by $H(s)$ and fed back. The key insight is the **insertion point** of $G_c(s)$: it processes the *error* before the plant, allowing it to pre-shape the control effort.

## 4.2 Uncompensated vs. Compensated — Side-by-Side Comparison

The diagram below contrasts the uncompensated and compensated loop architectures, making the role of $G_c(s)$ visually explicit.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UNCOMP["UNCOMPENSATED SYSTEM"]
        direction LR
        R1[Rs] --> SUM3{Adder}
        SUM3 --> E1[Es]
        E1 --> Gp1[Plant Gp s]
        Gp1 --> Y1[Ys]
        Y1 --> H1[H s]
        H1 --> SUM3
    end

    subgraph COMP["CASCADE COMPENSATED SYSTEM"]
        direction LR
        R2[Rs] --> SUM4{Adder}
        SUM4 --> E2[Es]
        E2 --> Gc2[Compensator Gc s]
        Gc2 --> Gp2[Plant Gp s]
        Gp2 --> Y2[Ys]
        Y2 --> H2[H s]
        H2 --> SUM4
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> Note how the only structural difference is the addition of the block $G_c(s)$ **between** the error signal and the plant. Yet this single block, when properly designed, can dramatically alter the closed-loop poles, transient response, and steady-state behaviour. This structural minimalism is why cascade compensation is the most widely used classical technique.

## 4.3 Frequency-Domain Effect Diagram

The next diagram visualises what cascade compensation *does* to the open-loop frequency response — it is a Mermaid conceptual flow rather than a literal plot, since Bode plots themselves are best drawn with graph tools.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Uncompensated Plant Gp s] --> B{Low Magnitude at DC}
    A --> C{Insufficient Phase at Crossover}
    B --> D[Add Lag Section: Boost DC Gain]
    C --> E[Add Lead Section: Add Positive Phase]
    D --> F[Compensated Open Loop Gc s Gp s]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Meets Kv Spec]
    F --> H[Meets PM Spec]
    G --> I[Final Closed Loop Satisfies ALL Specs]
    H --> I
```

**Interpretation:** The uncompensated plant suffers from *two* independent deficiencies (low DC gain **and** insufficient phase at crossover). No single control action can remedy both. Cascade compensation decomposes the fix into two serial stages — a **lag** section for DC accuracy and a **lead** section for phase margin — and combines them into a single $G_c(s)$ that simultaneously resolves both deficiencies. This decomposition is the engineering essence of why cascade compensation is *needed*.

## 4.4 Pole-Zero Geometry of Lead vs. Lag

The following conceptual diagram (rendered in Mermaid as a labelled tree since plot-style schematics are not native) explains the pole-zero placement philosophy:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEAD["LEAD COMPENSATOR  Pole and Zero on NEGATIVE real axis"]
        direction TB
        ZL["Zero at s = -1/T  closer to origin"] --> PL["Pole at s = -1 over alpha T  further from origin"]
    end
    subgraph LAG["LAG COMPENSATOR  Pole and Zero on NEGATIVE real axis"]
        direction TB
        ZG["Zero at s = -1/T  further from origin"] --> PG["Pole at s = -1 over beta T  closer to origin"]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **opposite** placement of zero-vs-pole is the defining geometric distinction: in a lead compensator, the **zero is closer** to the origin than the pole; in a lag compensator, the **pole is closer** to the origin than the zero. This mirror-image geometry directly produces the opposite frequency-domain effects (positive vs. negative phase contribution).

---
