# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the need for cascade compensation in feedback control systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A feedback control system with only a proportional gain $K$ cannot simultaneously satisfy conflicting specifications on transient response and steady-state accuracy. Increasing $K$ reduces steady-state error but degrades phase margin and may cause overshoot or instability; decreasing $K$ stabilises the response but worsens accuracy.

**Cascade compensation** addresses this by inserting a compensator $G_c(s)$ in series with the plant, providing *frequency-dependent shaping*: a **lead section** supplies positive phase to improve transient response and phase margin, while a **lag section** boosts low-frequency gain to reduce steady-state error. This decoupling of conflicting objectives — impossible with a single gain — is the fundamental need for cascade compensation. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between lead and lag cascade compensators in terms of pole-zero placement and frequency response.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Lead Compensator | Lag Compensator |
|---|---|---|
| Form | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}\cdot\dfrac{s + 1/T}{s + 1/(\alpha T)}$, $\alpha < 1$ | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{s + 1/T}{s + 1/(\beta T)}$, $\beta > 1$ |
| Zero location | At $s = -1/T$ (closer to origin) | At $s = -1/T$ (further from origin) |
| Pole location | At $s = -1/(\alpha T)$ (further from origin) | At $s = -1/(\beta T)$ (closer to origin) |
| Phase contribution | Positive (lead) | Negative (lag) |
| Magnitude effect | High-frequency amplification | Low-frequency amplification |
| Primary use | Improve transient response & phase margin | Reduce steady-state error |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** For a unity-feedback system with open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s + 2)(s + 5)}$, explain **why cascade compensation is necessary** when specifications demand a velocity constant $K_v \geq 50$ and a damping ratio $\zeta \geq 0.4$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **cascade lag compensator** so that the compensated system has $K_v = 50$ while preserving the uncompensated damping ratio of the dominant poles. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Why cascade compensation is necessary (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Identify the system type.**
$G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$ is a Type-1 system (one pole at origin).

**Step 2: Compute $K_v$ in terms of $K$.**

$$
K_v \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0}\, s\,G(s) \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0}\, s \cdot \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)} \;=\; \frac{K}{10}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the steady-state spec $K_v \geq 50$.**

$$
\frac{K}{10} \geq 50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K \geq 500
$$

**Step 4: Examine the characteristic equation at $K = 500$.**

$$
s(s+2)(s+5) + 500 \;=\; 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + 500 \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 5: Construct a Routh array.**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $10$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $7$ | $500$ | $0$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{7 \cdot 10 - 1 \cdot 500}{7} = \dfrac{-430}{7} \approx -61.4$ | $0$ | |
| $s^0$ | $500$ | | |

The $s^1$ row contains a **negative** entry $\Rightarrow$ the system is **unstable** at $K = 500$. **(3 marks)**

**Step 6: Conclude the conflict.**
To meet $K_v \geq 50$, we need $K \geq 500$, but this $K$ destabilises the closed loop. Hence a **single-gain design fails**, and cascade compensation is necessary to boost low-frequency gain *without* raising the crossover frequency into the destabilising region. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Lag compensator design (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Choose a baseline $K$ that yields acceptable damping.**

A Routh-based analysis (or root-locus sketching) shows that $K = 10$ gives $\zeta \approx 0.4$. We adopt $K = 10$ as our baseline.

**Step 2: Compute the uncompensated $K_v$ at $K = 10$.**

$$
K_v^{\text{(uncomp)}} \;=\; \frac{K}{10} \;=\; \frac{10}{10} \;=\; 1
$$

**Step 3: Determine the lag-network boost factor $\beta$.**

We require $K_v^{\text{comp}} = 50$ while keeping $K = 10$:

$$
K_v^{\text{comp}} \;=\; \beta \cdot K_v^{\text{(uncomp)}} \;=\; \beta \cdot 1 \;=\; \beta
$$

Thus:

$$
\boxed{\;\beta \;=\; 50\;}
$$

**Step 4: Choose the lag time constant $T$ such that the lag pole-zero pair lies well below the new crossover frequency.**

Estimate the uncompensated crossover at $K = 10$:

$$
\vert G(j\omega) \vert \;=\; \frac{10}{\omega \sqrt{\omega^2 + 4}\sqrt{\omega^2 + 25}} \;=\; 1
$$

Iterating: $\omega_{gc} \approx 0.9$ rad/s. To avoid disturbing the phase near $\omega_{gc}$, choose $\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{\omega_{gc}}{10} = 0.09$ rad/s, so:

$$
T \;=\; \frac{1}{0.09} \;\approx\; 11.1 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5: Write the lag compensator transfer function.**

$$
G_c(s) \;=\; \frac{s + 0.09}{s + 0.0018}
$$

**Step 6: Verify $K_v$.**

$$
K_v^{\text{comp}} \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0}\, s \cdot G_c(s)\,G_p(s) \;=\; \frac{1}{0.0018} \cdot \frac{10}{10} \;\cdot\; 0.09 \;=\; 50 \;\checkmark
$$

The dominant closed-loop poles are essentially unchanged from the $K = 10$ case (because the lag pole-zero pair is far to the left of $\omega_{gc}$), preserving $\zeta \approx 0.4$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** A unity-feedback system has $G(s) = \dfrac{4}{s(s + 0.5)}$. The specifications are: phase margin $PM \geq 45^\circ$ and velocity constant $K_v \geq 4$. Justify the **need for cascade compensation** and identify which type (lead, lag, or lag-lead) is most appropriate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a suitable **cascade lead compensator** to meet the phase-margin specification, and verify the $K_v$ specification. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Need identification and compensator selection (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute the uncompensated velocity constant.**

$$
K_v^{\text{(uncomp)}} \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0}\, s \cdot \frac{4}{s(s + 0.5)} \;=\; \frac{4}{0.5} \;=\; 8
$$

This **already exceeds** the $K_v \geq 4$ specification. So steady-state accuracy is *not* the binding constraint. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Compute the uncompensated phase margin.**

The open-loop frequency response is:

$$
G(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{4}{j\omega(j\omega + 0.5)}
$$

The gain-crossover frequency satisfies $\vert G(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$:

$$
\frac{4}{\omega_{gc}\sqrt{\omega_{gc}^2 + 0.25}} \;=\; 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_{gc}^2(\omega_{gc}^2 + 0.25) \;=\; 16
$$

Iterating: $\omega_{gc} \approx 1.86$ rad/s.

**Step 3: Compute the phase at $\omega_{gc}$.**

$$
\angle G(j\omega_{gc}) \;=\; -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_{gc}}{0.5}\right) \;=\; -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(3.72) \;\approx\; -90^\circ - 75^\circ \;=\; -165^\circ
$$

**Step 4: Phase margin.**

$$
PM^{\text{(uncomp)}} \;=\; 180^\circ - 165^\circ \;=\; 15^\circ
$$

This is **far below** the required $45^\circ$, indicating highly oscillatory transient response. **(3 marks)**

**Step 5: Identify the binding constraint and choose the compensator.**

- Steady-state accuracy is already met ($K_v = 8 > 4$).
- Phase margin is deficient ($15^\circ < 45^\circ$).
- Therefore the binding constraint is **transient response / phase margin**, which is best addressed by a **lead compensator**.

A lead compensator adds positive phase at $\omega_{gc}$, raising $PM$ from $15^\circ$ to $\geq 45^\circ$ without appreciably disturbing $K_v$ (since a lead section's DC gain is unity for the form $G_c(s) = \dfrac{s + 1/T}{s + 1/(\alpha T)}$). **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Lead compensator design (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute the required phase boost.**

$$
\phi_m^{\text{required}} \;=\; PM^{\text{spec}} - PM^{\text{uncomp}} + 5^\circ \;\text{(safety margin)} \;=\; 45^\circ - 15^\circ + 5^\circ \;=\; 35^\circ
$$

The extra $5^\circ$ compensates for the gain-shift effect that slightly changes the crossover. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Compute $\alpha$ from $\phi_m$.**

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{1 - \sin\phi_m}{1 + \sin\phi_m} \;=\; \frac{1 - \sin 35^\circ}{1 + \sin 35^\circ} \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.574}{1 + 0.574} \;=\; \frac{0.426}{1.574} \;\approx\; 0.271
$$

**Step 3: Determine the magnitude penalty at $\omega_m$.**

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.271}} \;\approx\; 1.92 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 20\log_{10}(1.92) \;\approx\; 5.67 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4: Find the new gain-crossover frequency.**

Since the magnitude penalty at $\omega_m$ must be cancelled by raising the gain, the new $\omega_{gc}^{\text{new}}$ satisfies:

$$
20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{gc}^{\text{new}}) \vert \;=\; -5.67 \text{ dB} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert G(j\omega_{gc}^{\text{new}}) \vert \;\approx\; 0.521
$$

Solving $\dfrac{4}{\omega\sqrt{\omega^2 + 0.25}} = 0.521$ by iteration:

$$
\omega_{gc}^{\text{new}} \;\approx\; 2.55 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 5: Place $\omega_m$ at the new $\omega_{gc}$.**

$$
\omega_m \;=\; \frac{1}{T\sqrt{\alpha}} \;=\; \omega_{gc}^{\text{new}} \;\approx\; 2.55
$$

$$
T \;=\; \frac{1}{\omega_m \sqrt{\alpha}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2.55 \cdot \sqrt{0.271}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2.55 \cdot 0.520} \;\approx\; 0.754 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6: Compute zero and pole frequencies.**

Zero at $s = -1/T$: $\;\;-1/T \;\approx\; -1.326$ rad/s.

Pole at $s = -1/(\alpha T)$: $\;\;-1/(\alpha T) \;\approx\; -4.89$ rad/s.

**Step 7: Write the compensator and the compensated open-loop transfer function.**

$$
\boxed{\;G_c(s) \;=\; \frac{s + 1.326}{s + 4.89}\;}
$$

$$
G_c(s)\,G_p(s) \;=\; \frac{4(s + 1.326)}{s(s + 0.5)(s + 4.89)}
$$

**Step 8: Verify $K_v$.**

$$
K_v^{\text{comp}} \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0}\, s \cdot \frac{4(s + 1.326)}{s(s + 0.5)(s + 4.89)} \;=\; \frac{4 \cdot 1.326}{0.5 \cdot 4.89} \;\approx\; 2.17
$$

Wait — this is **less** than the required 4. The uncompensated $K_v = 8$ has been *reduced* by the lead compensator. We must **boost** the gain by $4 / 2.17 \approx 1.84$, i.e., multiply by $1.84$, which in dB is $20\log_{10}(1.84) \approx 5.3$ dB.

**Step 9: Rescale the compensator to meet both specs simultaneously.**

Use a lead compensator of the form $G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \dfrac{s + 1.326}{s + 4.89}$ with $K_c = 1.84$:

$$
\boxed{\;G_c(s) \;=\; 1.84 \cdot \frac{s + 1.326}{s + 4.89}\;}
$$

Final verification:

- $K_v^{\text{comp}} = 1.84 \times 2.17 \approx 4.0 \;\geq\; 4\;\checkmark$
- $PM^{\text{comp}} \approx 45^\circ\;\checkmark$ **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add a "safety margin" (5°–12°) to the required phase boost.** The new gain-crossover shifts after compensation, slightly reducing the realised PM. Students who design exactly for the nominal $\phi_m$ typically end up with $PM$ a few degrees short, and lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing the pole-zero order in lead vs. lag compensators.** Lead: zero closer to origin than pole. Lag: pole closer to origin than zero. Examiners explicitly test this. Drawing the wrong picture costs 2 marks instantly.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the magnitude penalty of a lead network.** After placing the lead, the magnitude at $\omega_m$ is $1/\sqrt{\alpha}$, not 1 (0 dB). Failing to compensate this shift by raising $K$ (or $K_c$) leaves the design with a lower $\omega_{gc}$ than intended.
>
> 4. **Placing the lag pole-zero pair too close to $\omega_{gc}$.** This introduces a phase lag right where phase margin is being measured, *eroding* $PM$. Rule of thumb: $\omega_z$ of lag section $\leq \omega_{gc}/10$. Violating this rule costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 5. **Stating $K_v$ or $K_p$ without specifying the input type.** $K_v$ corresponds to ramp input only; $K_p$ to step; $K_a$ to parabolic. Writing "$K_v = 4$" without mentioning "for a unit ramp input" is incomplete and loses half a mark.
>
> 6. **Using the wrong formula for $\alpha$ in the lead network.** It is $\alpha = \frac{1 - \sin\phi_m}{1 + \sin\phi_m}$, *not* $\frac{1 + \sin\phi_m}{1 - \sin\phi_m}$. The latter gives $\alpha > 1$, which is invalid for a lead compensator.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Need for Cascade Compensation"**

- **Core Need (1-liner):** Cascade compensation is needed because a single gain $K$ cannot simultaneously satisfy transient-response and steady-state-accuracy specifications that fundamentally conflict.

- **Three Pillars of Need:**
  1. Transient-response deficiency → requires **phase lead** → **lead** compensator.
  2. Steady-state-error deficiency → requires **low-frequency gain boost** → **lag** compensator.
  3. Combined deficiency → requires **lag-lead** compensator.

- **Canonical transfer functions:**
  - Lead: $\;G_c(s) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}\cdot\dfrac{1 + sT}{1 + s\alpha T},\;\alpha < 1.$
  - Lag: $\;G_c(s) = \dfrac{1 + sT}{1 + s\beta T},\;\beta > 1.$
  - Lag-Lead: product of the two above with non-overlapping pole-zero pairs.

- **Key results (must memorise):**
  - Frequency of maximum lead: $\;\omega_m = \dfrac{1}{T\sqrt{\alpha}}.$
  - Maximum phase lead: $\;\sin\phi_m = \dfrac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}\;$ and $\;\alpha = \dfrac{1-\sin\phi_m}{1+\sin\phi_m}.$
  - Magnitude penalty at $\omega_m$: $\;\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}\;$ (linear) or $\;-10\log_{10}\alpha\;$ dB.

- **Error constants (unity feedback):**
  - $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)$, $\;K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} sG(s)$, $\;K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 G(s)$.
  - Ramp $e_{ss} = 1/K_v$, Parabolic $e_{ss} = 1/K_a$, Step $e_{ss} = 1/(1+K_p)$.

- **Pole-zero geometry (do not mix up):**
  - Lead: $\;\vert \text{zero} \vert < \vert \text{pole} \vert$ (zero near origin).
  - Lag: $\;\vert \text{pole} \vert < \vert \text{zero} \vert$ (pole near origin).

- **Design procedure summary:**
  1. Determine $K$ to meet $K_v$ spec → check stability/transient.
  2. If transient is poor, **add lead** to boost $PM$.
  3. If transient is OK but $K_v$ is poor, **add lag** to boost DC gain.
  4. If both are poor, **add lag-lead**.
  5. Always verify the final design against all specifications.

- **Real-world utility:** Autopilots, robotic servos, motor drives, process control loops, voltage regulators — anywhere a system must be both **fast** and **accurate** without becoming **unstable**.

- **Exam mantra:** *"Cascade compensation decouples what a single gain cannot."* If your exam answer captures this single sentence well, you have answered the conceptual heart of the question.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT501 - LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cascade Compensation - PI, PD, and PID Controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Cascade Compensation?

**Cascade (Series) Compensation** is a design technique in control engineering where a compensator $G_c(s)$ is placed in **series (cascade)** with the plant $G(s)$ in the forward path of a closed-loop system to shape the dynamic response and meet performance specifications (steady-state accuracy, transient response, and stability).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Cascade compensation involves placing a controller $G_c(s)$ in series with the plant $G_p(s)$ such that the total forward-path transfer function becomes $G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)$. The controller adds **poles and/or zeros** to alter the root locus or frequency response.

The three fundamental cascade compensators built from proportional, integral, and derivative actions are:

| Compensator | Output Law | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | $u(t) = K_p e(t)$ | Basic gain adjustment |
| **PI** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt$ | Eliminate steady-state error |
| **PD** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt$ | Improve transient response |
| **PID** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt$ | Combine both benefits |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control & Suspension System

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: Driving a Car on a Hilly Road**
>
> Imagine driving a car and trying to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a road that goes up and down hills.
>
> - **Proportional (P) Action:** You press the accelerator harder the more you fall below 60 km/h. Big error → Big correction. This reacts to the **present** error.
> - **Integral (I) Action:** You notice that even after pressing the accelerator, you are consistently 2 km/h below target. The I-action keeps accumulating this small error and gradually increases the throttle until the average error becomes **zero**. This fixes **historical (past)** errors.
> - **Derivative (D) Action:** You see a steep hill coming. Before the car even slows down, you press the accelerator more (anticipation). The D-action reacts to the **rate of change** of error (future prediction).
>
> - **PI Controller** = P + I → Eliminates the persistent speed offset (steady-state error).
> - **PD Controller** = P + D → Smooths the response to sudden disturbances (better damping).
> - **PID Controller** = P + I + D → Maintains speed accurately AND smoothly under all conditions.

## 1.3 Why Are These Controllers Important in KTU?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCMRT501 - Module 4):**
> "Design of cascade compensation networks — lead, lag, lag-lead using root locus and Bode plot methods. Introduction to PI, PD, and PID controllers and their effect on system performance."

PI, PD, and PID controllers are the **industrial workhorses** — over **95% of all process control loops** in industry use some form of PID. Understanding their effect on transient and steady-state performance is a mandatory KTU outcome.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Transfer Functions of the Three Controllers

### 2.1.1 PI Controller (Proportional-Integral)

A PI controller has the transfer function in the **s-domain**:

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s} = K_p \left( \frac{s + z_c}{s} \right)$$

where the controller zero is located at:

$$z_c = \frac{K_i}{K_p} = \frac{1}{T_i}$$

- Adds a **pole at the origin** $(s = 0)$ → increases system type by 1.
- Adds a **finite zero** at $s = -z_c$ (typically placed near the dominant plant pole to partially cancel its sluggish effect).

### 2.1.2 PD Controller (Proportional-Derivative)

$$G_c(s) = K_p + K_d s = K_p(1 + T_d s) = K_p T_d (s + z_c)$$

where the controller zero is located at:

$$z_c = \frac{1}{T_d} = \frac{K_p}{K_d}$$

- Adds **only a zero** (no new pole) at $s = -z_c$ → system type is **unchanged**.
- The zero is typically placed on the negative real axis to pull the root locus **leftward**, increasing damping.

> [!NOTE]
> A pure PD controller is **not physically realizable** in practice (it has more zeros than poles). For KTU exam purposes, the ideal PD form is acceptable unless specifically asked for a "realizable" controller.

### 2.1.3 PID Controller (Proportional-Integral-Derivative)

The **parallel form** of the PID controller is:

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

The **standard/series form** of the PID controller is:

$$G_c(s) = K_p \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right) = \frac{K_p T_i T_d s^2 + K_p T_i s + K_p}{T_i s}$$

where $K_i = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}$ and $K_d = K_p T_d$.

- Adds **one pole at the origin** and **two finite zeros** → system type increases by 1.
- The two zeros are designed to improve transient response while the integrator kills the steady-state error.

## 2.2 Effect on System Type and Static Error Constants

For a unity feedback system with plant $G(s)$ and cascade controller $G_c(s)$, the **static error constants** are:

$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G_c(s) G(s), \quad K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s G_c(s) G(s), \quad K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 G_c(s) G(s)$$

| Original Plant Type | Controller Added | New System Type | Effect on $e_{ss}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type 0 | PI | **Type 1** | Step error → 0, Ramp error → finite |
| Type 1 | PI | **Type 2** | Step & Ramp error → 0 |
| Type 0 | PD | **Type 0** | No change in $e_{ss}$ (only P-action scales) |
| Type 1 | PD | **Type 1** | No change in $e_{ss}$ (transient only) |
| Type 0 | PID | **Type 1** | Step error → 0, Ramp error → finite |
| Type 1 | PID | **Type 2** | Step & Ramp error → 0 |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master This Table — It Is the Core of All PI/PD/PID Numerical Problems.**

| Controller | Transfer Function $G_c(s)$ | Pole(s) Added | Zero(s) Added | Effect on System Type | Improves |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $K_p$ | None | None | No change | Loop gain only |
| **PI** | $K_p \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right) = \dfrac{K_p(s + 1/T_i)}{s}$ | $s=0$ | $s = -1/T_i$ | **+1** | Steady-state error |
| **PD** | $K_p(1 + T_d s) = K_p T_d \left(s + \dfrac{1}{T_d}\right)$ | None | $s = -1/T_d$ | **No change** | Transient response (damping) |
| **PID** | $K_p \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$ | $s=0$ | Two zeros from quadratic | **+1** | Both transient & steady-state |

### 2.3.1 Steady-State Error Formulas (Unity Feedback)

For input $R(s)$ and error $E(s) = \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G_c(s)G(s)}$, the steady-state error is:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s R(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G(s)}$$

| Input | $R(s)$ | $e_{ss}$ Formula (Type 0) | $e_{ss}$ Formula (Type 1) | $e_{ss}$ Formula (Type 2) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Step | $\frac{1}{s}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| Ramp | $\frac{1}{s^2}$ | $\infty$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | $0$ |
| Parabola | $\frac{1}{s^3}$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_a}$ |

### 2.3.2 Effect on Root Locus and Transient Specifications

| Specification | PD Effect | PI Effect | PID Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Damping ratio $\zeta$ | **Increases** significantly | Slightly decreases | Increases (with proper design) |
| Natural frequency $\omega_n$ | Increases | Slightly decreases | Can be tuned |
| Settling time $t_s$ | Decreases (faster) | Slightly increases | Decreases (with proper design) |
| Overshoot $M_p$ | **Reduces** | Slightly increases | Reduces |
| Steady-state error $e_{ss}$ | No change for step/ramp | **Eliminates** for step | **Eliminates** for step |
| Stability | Improves (zero adds phase lead) | Degrades (pole pulls locus right) | Depends on zero placement |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PI Controllers**: Used in **process control** (chemical plants, temperature control) where steady-state accuracy is critical.
- **PD Controllers**: Used in **motion control** (robotics, CNC machines) where fast, well-damped response is required.
- **PID Controllers**: The universal controller — used in **cruise control, aircraft autopilots, industrial motor drives, and HVAC systems**.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU questions, you will frequently be asked: *"Justify why a PI controller is preferred over a P controller for eliminating steady-state offset"* or *"Explain how a PD controller improves transient response using root locus."* — these are direct application questions.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Loop Transfer Function with Cascade Controller

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a **unity negative feedback** system with:

- Plant transfer function: $G(s)$
- Cascade controller: $G_c(s)$
- Reference input: $R(s)$
- Output: $C(s)$
- Error: $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the forward-path signal flow:**

The controller output is $U(s) = G_c(s) \cdot E(s)$.

**Step 2 — Write the plant output equation:**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot U(s) = G(s) \cdot G_c(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the error definition $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$:**

$$C(s) = G(s) G_c(s) \left[ R(s) - C(s) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Expand the right-hand side:**

$$C(s) = G(s) G_c(s) R(s) - G(s) G_c(s) C(s)$$

**Step 5 — Collect the $C(s)$ terms on the left-hand side:**

$$C(s) + G(s) G_c(s) C(s) = G(s) G_c(s) R(s)$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $C(s)$:**

$$C(s) \left[ 1 + G(s) G_c(s) \right] = G(s) G_c(s) R(s)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = C(s)/R(s)$:**

$$\boxed{T(s) = \frac{G_c(s) G(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G(s)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental closed-loop transfer function** for a unity feedback system with cascade controller $G_c(s)$. The characteristic equation $1 + G_c(s) G(s) = 0$ governs stability.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effect of PI Controller on Steady-State Error

### 3.2.1 Setup

Consider a **Type 0** plant $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{(s+a)(s+b)}$ with a cascade PI controller $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$.

The step-input steady-state error for a unity feedback system is:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s R(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G_c(s) G(s)}$$

### 3.2.2 Without Controller (P-only)

With $G_c(s) = K_p$ and the plant above:

$$G_c(s) G(s) = \frac{K_p K}{(s+a)(s+b)}$$

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K_p K}{(s+a)(s+b)}} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{K_p K}{ab}}$$

This is a **finite, non-zero** steady-state error — the system has a static position error.

### 3.2.3 With PI Controller

With $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$:

$$G_c(s) G(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} \right) \cdot \frac{K}{(s+a)(s+b)} = \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{s(s+a)(s+b)}$$

Now evaluate the limit:

$$\lim_{s \to 0} G_c(s) G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{s(s+a)(s+b)} = \frac{K \cdot K_i}{0 \cdot a \cdot b} = \infty$$

Therefore:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The PI controller **completely eliminates the steady-state error** for a step input because the integrator forces $\lim_{s \to 0} G_c(s) = \infty$, driving $e_{ss} \to 0$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effect of PD Controller on Transient Response (Root Locus)

### 3.3.1 Setup

Consider a **second-order** plant $G(s) = \dfrac{\omega_n^2}{s(s + 2\zeta \omega_n)}$ (a Type 1 system) with a PD controller $G_c(s) = K_p(1 + T_d s) = K_p T_d (s + 1/T_d)$.

The closed-loop characteristic equation is:

$$1 + G_c(s) G(s) = 0 \implies 1 + \frac{K_p T_d \omega_n^2 (s + 1/T_d)}{s(s + 2\zeta \omega_n)} = 0$$

Multiplying out:

$$s(s + 2\zeta \omega_n) + K_p T_d \omega_n^2 (s + 1/T_d) = 0$$

$$s^2 + 2\zeta \omega_n s + K_p T_d \omega_n^2 s + K_p \omega_n^2 = 0$$

$$s^2 + \left( 2\zeta \omega_n + K_p T_d \omega_n^2 \right) s + K_p \omega_n^2 = 0$$

### 3.3.2 Identifying the New Damping Ratio

Compare with the standard second-order form $s^2 + 2\zeta_{\text{new}} \omega_{n,\text{new}} s + \omega_{n,\text{new}}^2 = 0$:

$$\omega_{n,\text{new}} = \omega_n \sqrt{K_p}$$

$$2\zeta_{\text{new}} \omega_{n,\text{new}} = 2\zeta \omega_n + K_p T_d \omega_n^2$$

Dividing both sides by $2\omega_{n,\text{new}} = 2\omega_n \sqrt{K_p}$:

$$\zeta_{\text{new}} = \frac{2\zeta \omega_n + K_p T_d \omega_n^2}{2\omega_n \sqrt{K_p}} = \zeta \sqrt{\frac{1}{K_p}} + \frac{\sqrt{K_p} T_d \omega_n}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** For a fixed $K_p > 1$, increasing $T_d$ **increases the effective damping ratio** $\zeta_{\text{new}}$. This physically corresponds to **reduced overshoot** and **faster settling** — the key benefit of a PD controller.

### 3.3.3 Steady-State Error with PD Controller

For a Type 1 system with PD: $G_c(s) = K_p(1 + T_d s)$ has $\lim_{s \to 0} G_c(s) = K_p$ (finite, non-zero).

The velocity error constant becomes:

$$K_{v,\text{new}} = \lim_{s \to 0} s G_c(s) G(s) = K_p \cdot K_{v,\text{old}}$$

Ramp error:

$$e_{ss,\text{ramp}} = \frac{1}{K_{v,\text{new}}} = \frac{1}{K_p \cdot K_{v,\text{old}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The PD controller **does not eliminate** the ramp steady-state error; it only scales it. To eliminate ramp error, an **integrator (PI or PID)** is mandatory.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Combined Numerical Problem — Effect of PID on a Type 1 Plant

### 3.4.1 Problem

A unity feedback system has plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$. A PID controller $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ is added in cascade. For $K_p = 20$, $K_i = 10$, $K_d = 5$, compute:

1. The new open-loop transfer function.
2. The new system type.
3. The steady-state error for step and ramp inputs.
4. The new static error constants.

### 3.4.2 Solution — Step 1: New Open-Loop Transfer Function

$$G_c(s) = 20 + \frac{10}{s} + 5s = \frac{5s^2 + 20s + 10}{s} = \frac{5(s^2 + 4s + 2)}{s}$$

Open-loop transfer function:

$$G_c(s) G(s) = \frac{5(s^2 + 4s + 2)}{s} \cdot \frac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)} = \frac{50(s^2 + 4s + 2)}{s^2(s+2)(s+5)}$$

### 3.4.3 Solution — Step 2: System Type

The denominator has $s^2$ as a factor → **Type 2 system** (original was Type 1).

### 3.4.4 Solution — Step 3: Steady-State Errors

Position error constant:

$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G_c(s) G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{50(s^2 + 4s + 2)}{s^2(s+2)(s+5)} = \frac{50 \cdot 2}{0 \cdot 2 \cdot 5} = \infty$$

Velocity error constant:

$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s G_c(s) G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{50(s^2 + 4s + 2)}{s(s+2)(s+5)} = \frac{50 \cdot 2}{0 \cdot 2 \cdot 5} = \infty$$

Acceleration error constant:

$$K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 G_c(s) G(s) = \frac{50 \cdot 2}{2 \cdot 5} = \frac{100}{10} = 10$$

Steady-state errors:

$$e_{ss}(\text{step}) = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

$$e_{ss}(\text{ramp}) = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{\infty} = 0$$

$$e_{ss}(\text{parabola}) = \frac{1}{K_a} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The PID controller upgrades the system from Type 1 → Type 2, eliminating both the step and ramp errors. The parabolic error becomes finite ($= 0.1$).

### 3.4.5 Solution — Step 4: Controller Zeros

The PID numerator $s^2 + 4s + 2 = 0$ has zeros at:

$$s = \frac{-4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 8}}{2} = \frac{-4 \pm \sqrt{8}}{2} = -2 \pm \sqrt{2}$$

So zeros are at $s \approx -0.586$ and $s \approx -3.414$ — both on the negative real axis, attracting the root locus to the left and improving damping.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: PID Controller Definition
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Q: What is a PID controller? Write its transfer function in both parallel and standard form.**

**Model Answer:**

A **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller** is a feedback controller that computes the control signal as a weighted sum of the error, its integral, and its derivative.

**Parallel form:**

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

**Standard/Series form:**

$$G_c(s) = K_p \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right)$$

where $K_p$ is the proportional gain, $K_i$ is the integral gain, $K_d$ is the derivative gain, $T_i = K_p/K_i$ is the integral time, and $T_d = K_d/K_p$ is the derivative time. The controller adds **one pole at the origin** and **two finite zeros**, improving both steady-state accuracy and transient response.

---

### Question 2: PI vs PD Comparison
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]**

**Q: Compare PI and PD controllers with respect to their effect on steady-state error and transient response.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PI Controller | PD Controller |
|---|---|---|
| **Steady-state error** | Eliminates step-input error (system type +1) | Does not eliminate error (only scales) |
| **Transient response** | May increase overshoot; can be sluggish | Reduces overshoot, increases damping |
| **Pole-zero addition** | Pole at $s=0$ + finite zero | Only one finite zero |
| **System type** | Increases by 1 | Unchanged |
| **Typical use** | Process control, servo position | Motion control, robotics |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A: Effect of PI Controller on Steady-State Error

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Modified for Module 4 Alignment]**

**A (a)** For a unity feedback control system with open-loop plant $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+1)(s+4)}$, derive the steady-state error for unit step and unit ramp input **without** any controller. (7 marks)

**A (b)** Now a PI controller $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} = 1 + \dfrac{0.5}{s}$ is added in cascade. Recompute the steady-state errors and comment on the results. Also verify using the static error constant method. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to A(a): Without Controller

**Step 1 — Identify the system type:**

The open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+1)(s+4)}$ has **one pole at the origin** → **Type 1 system**.

**Step 2 — Compute the static error constants:**

Position constant:

$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+4)} = \frac{K}{0} = \infty$$

Velocity constant:

$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K}{(s+1)(s+4)} = \frac{K}{1 \times 4} = \frac{K}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the steady-state errors:**

For unit step input $R(s) = 1/s$:

$$e_{ss}(\text{step}) = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

For unit ramp input $R(s) = 1/s^2$:

$$e_{ss}(\text{ramp}) = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{4}{K}$$

If $K = 4$, then $e_{ss}(\text{ramp}) = 1$.

---

#### Solution to A(b): With PI Controller

**Step 1 — Write the new open-loop transfer function:**

$$G_c(s) G(s) = \left(1 + \frac{0.5}{s}\right) \cdot \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+4)} = \frac{(s + 0.5)}{s} \cdot \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+4)}$$

$$G_c(s) G(s) = \frac{K(s + 0.5)}{s^2(s+1)(s+4)}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the new system type:**

The denominator now has $s^2$ as a factor → **Type 2 system**.

**Step 3 — Recompute the static error constants:**

Position constant:

$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G_c(s) G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K(s + 0.5)}{s^2(s+1)(s+4)} = \frac{0.5 K}{0} = \infty$$

Velocity constant:

$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s G_c(s) G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K(s + 0.5)}{s(s+1)(s+4)} = \frac{0.5 K}{0} = \infty$$

Acceleration constant:

$$K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 G_c(s) G(s) = \frac{0.5 K}{1 \times 4} = \frac{0.5 K}{4} = \frac{K}{8}$$

**Step 4 — Recompute the steady-state errors:**

$$e_{ss}(\text{step}) = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

$$e_{ss}(\text{ramp}) = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{\infty} = 0$$

$$e_{ss}(\text{parabola}) = \frac{1}{K_a} = \frac{8}{K}$$

For $K = 4$: $e_{ss}(\text{parabola}) = 2$.

**Comment:** The PI controller upgrades the system from Type 1 → Type 2, completely eliminating both the step and ramp errors. The system can now track a parabolic input with a finite, non-zero error.

---

### Question B: Effect of PD Controller on Transient Response

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023 — Internal Choice Pattern]**

**B (a)** A unity feedback system has plant $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+2)}$. Design a PD controller of the form $G_c(s) = K_p(1 + T_d s)$ such that the **damping ratio** $\zeta = 0.7$ and **natural frequency** $\omega_n = 4$ rad/s. (7 marks)

**B (b)** For the designed PD controller, compute the steady-state error for unit step and unit ramp input. Comment on whether PD can eliminate steady-state error. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to B(a): PD Design

**Step 1 — Write the open-loop transfer function with PD controller:**

$$G_c(s) G(s) = K_p(1 + T_d s) \cdot \frac{1}{s(s+2)} = \frac{K_p(1 + T_d s)}{s(s+2)}$$

**Step 2 — Write the characteristic equation:**

$$1 + G_c(s) G(s) = 0 \implies s(s+2) + K_p(1 + T_d s) = 0$$

$$s^2 + 2s + K_p T_d s + K_p = 0$$

$$s^2 + (2 + K_p T_d) s + K_p = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compare with standard second-order form $s^2 + 2\zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$:**

$$\omega_n^2 = K_p \implies K_p = \omega_n^2 = 16$$

$$2\zeta \omega_n = 2 + K_p T_d \implies 2(0.7)(4) = 2 + 16 T_d$$

$$5.6 = 2 + 16 T_d \implies 16 T_d = 3.6 \implies T_d = 0.225 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Final designed PD controller:**

$$\boxed{G_c(s) = 16(1 + 0.225s) = 16 + 3.6 s}$$

---

#### Solution to B(b): Steady-State Error Analysis

**Step 1 — Compute static error constants with PD:**

$$G_c(s) G(s) = \frac{16(1 + 0.225s)}{s(s+2)} = \frac{16 + 3.6s}{s(s+2)}$$

$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G_c(s) G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{16 + 3.6s}{s(s+2)} = \frac{16}{0} = \infty$$

$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s G_c(s) G(s) = \frac{16}{2} = 8$$

**Step 2 — Compute steady-state errors:**

$$e_{ss}(\text{step}) = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = 0$$

$$e_{ss}(\text{ramp}) = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$

**Step 3 — Comment:**

The PD controller **does not change the system type** (still Type 1). It eliminates the step error (already zero for Type 1) but **cannot eliminate the ramp error**. To eliminate the ramp error, an **integrator** (PI or PID controller) must be added.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write that "PD eliminates steady-state error" — this is **WRONG**. PD only scales the error, it does not eliminate it. Only the **integrator (I-action)** can eliminate steady-state error.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** When computing $K_v$ with PD, students forget to include $K_p$ in the limit. Always write $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G_c(s) \cdot G(s)$ — do not drop $G_c(s)$.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing the **system type** with the **order**. System type = number of poles at origin. A Type 2 system has $s^2$ in the denominator of the open-loop transfer function.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Writing the PID transfer function as $K_p + K_i s + K_d / s$ (inverted). The correct form is $K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$.
> 5. **Mark Loss Zone (14-mark questions):** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by not commenting on the **transient vs steady-state trade-off** in their final conclusion. Always add a concluding remark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- **PI Controller:** $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$ → Adds **pole at origin + one zero** → System type **+1** → **Eliminates step error**.
- **PD Controller:** $G_c(s) = K_p(1 + T_d s)$ → Adds **one zero (no pole)** → System type **unchanged** → **Improves damping, reduces overshoot**.
- **PID Controller:** $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ → Adds **pole at origin + two zeros** → System type **+1** → **Best of both worlds**.
- **Static Error Constants:**
  - $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G_c(s) G(s)$ → governs step error.
  - $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s G_c(s) G(s)$ → governs ramp error.
  - $K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 G_c(s) G(s)$ → governs parabolic error.
- **Steady-State Error Formulas (Unity Feedback):**
  - Step: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$
  - Ramp: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$
  - Parabolic: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_a}$
- **PD Effect on Damping:** The added zero **attracts the root locus leftward**, increasing $\zeta$ and reducing overshoot.
- **PI Effect on Stability:** The added pole at origin **degrades relative stability**; the zero must be placed near the slowest plant pole to compensate.
- **Realizability:** Pure PD is not physically realizable (use $G_c(s) = K_p(1 + T_d s)/(1 + \alpha T_d s)$ with $\alpha \ll 1$ for practical implementation).
- **Tuning Rule of Thumb (Ziegler-Nichols):** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$ where $K_u$ is the ultimate gain and $T_u$ is the ultimate period.
- **Key Distinction:** PI = **accuracy** (steady-state), PD = **speed** (transient), PID = **accuracy + speed**.
- **Exam Tip:** Always identify the **system type** first, then compute the static error constants, then determine $e_{ss}$. This 3-step approach guarantees full marks on any $e_{ss}$ problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tuning of PID Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT501 - LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tuning of PID Controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 : Tuning of PID Controller

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **PID Controller** is a generic control loop feedback mechanism widely used in industrial control systems. The PID algorithm involves three separate constant parameters: the **Proportional (P)**, the **Integral (I)**, and the **Derivative (D)** values. The proportional term depends on the present error, the integral term depends on the accumulation of past errors, and the derivative term is a prediction of future errors based on the current rate of change.

Mathematically, the PID controller in the **ideal parallel (non-interacting) form** is expressed as:

$$C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

Where:
- $K_p$ is the **Proportional Gain** (dimensionless)
- $K_i$ is the **Integral Gain** (units: $\text{sec}^{-1}$)
- $K_d$ is the **Derivative Gain** (units: sec)
- $s$ is the **Laplace Operator**

An alternative representation, called the **standard ISA form** (or interacting form), uses the proportional gain $K_c$ along with integral time $T_i$ and derivative time $T_d$:

$$C(s) = K_c \left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The KTU syllabus specifically expects students to be proficient in the **Tuning of PID Controllers**, which is the process of determining the optimal values of $K_p$, $K_i$, and $K_d$ to achieve a desired closed-loop response. The two principal methods prescribed are the **Ziegler-Nichols Tuning Methods** (both closed-loop and open-loop variants).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Car Cruise Control"

Imagine you are driving a car and trying to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** (this is your *setpoint*).

- **Proportional (P) Action**: The further you are from 60 km/h, the harder you press the accelerator. If you are at 40 km/h, you press much harder than when you are at 55 km/h. This is "proportional" to the error. However, if you only press based on error, you'll never quite reach 60 km/h because pressing force becomes zero as error becomes zero. You will always have a small *offset*.

- **Integral (I) Action**: Realizing you're always 2 km/h slow, you slowly press the accelerator a *little* more over time, eventually eliminating that small constant offset. The integral term "remembers" past errors and adds a corrective push that accumulates until the error is zero.

- **Derivative (D) Action**: Approaching a steep hill, you anticipate the speed drop and press the accelerator *before* the car actually slows down. The derivative term predicts future errors based on how fast the error is changing, allowing for a smooth, anticipatory response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tuning** is the engineering process of finding the right balance of these three "driver behaviors" so the car (the *plant*) reaches and maintains 60 km/h (the *setpoint*) as quickly and smoothly as possible.

### 1.3 Effects of P, I, and D Gains on System Response

| Gain Increased | Rise Time | Overshoot | Settling Time | Steady-State Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$K_p$** | Decreases | Increases | Small change | Decreases (but never zero) |
| **$K_i$** | Decreases | Increases | Increases | **Eliminated (zero)** |
| **$K_d$** | Minor decrease | Decreases | Decreases | No effect |

> [!TIP]
> A good KTU strategy is to first use **P-control** to get a fast response, then add **I-control** to remove the steady-state error, and finally add **D-control** to dampen overshoot and reduce settling time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The PID Control Loop Architecture

A standard **unity-feedback** control system with a PID controller consists of:

1. **Reference Input** $R(s)$ — the desired value
2. **Error Signal** $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$ — the difference between desired and actual output
3. **PID Controller** $C(s)$ — generates the control signal
4. **Plant / Process** $G_p(s)$ — the system being controlled
5. **Feedback Element** $H(s) = 1$ — typically unity in single-loop systems

The **closed-loop transfer function** is:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s) G_p(s)}{1 + C(s) G_p(s) H(s)}$$

The **characteristic equation** that determines stability is:

$$1 + C(s) G_p(s) H(s) = 0$$

### 2.2 Ziegler-Nichols Tuning Method — Overview

The Ziegler-Nichols (Z-N) method is a heuristic tuning technique that produces reasonable controller settings for a wide range of processes. There are **two distinct methods**:

- **Method 1 (Open-Loop / Reaction Curve Method)**: Applied directly to the open-loop step response of the plant.
- **Method 2 (Closed-Loop / Ultimate Gain Method)**: Performed with the controller connected in a closed loop using only proportional control.

Both methods produce the same type of empirical rules that target a **quarter-decay ratio** (i.e., the second overshoot is roughly one-quarter the magnitude of the first overshoot).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both Z-N methods target a specific damping ratio of approximately $\zeta \approx 0.21$, which corresponds to a **quarter-amplitude decay** per cycle. This means the response will have **significant overshoot** (~25%), which is acceptable for setpoint tracking but may be unsuitable for processes where overshoot is dangerous (e.g., temperature control).

### 2.3 Ziegler-Nichols First Method — Reaction Curve (Open-Loop)

This method is applied to systems whose open-loop step response is **S-shaped** (i.e., exhibits a first-order-plus-dead-time, or **FOPDT**, behavior). The transfer function of such a process is:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K e^{-Ls}}{\tau s + 1}$$

Where:
- $K$ is the steady-state process gain (dimensionless)
- $L$ is the **apparent dead time** (sec) — the time the output takes to begin responding to the step input
- $\tau$ is the **process time constant** (sec)

The procedure is:
1. **Step Test**: Apply a step input of magnitude $A$ to the open-loop plant.
2. **Record Output**: Note the steady-state change in output, $\Delta Y$.
3. **Compute Process Gain**: $K = \Delta Y / A$
4. **Draw Tangent**: At the inflection point of the S-shaped curve, draw a tangent line.
5. **Measure Parameters**:
   - $L$ is the time-axis intercept of the tangent.
   - $T$ (or $\tau$) is the time required for the tangent to go from 0 to its final value, minus $L$.

**Z-N First Method Tuning Formulas (for a unit-step input of magnitude $A$):**

If $R = L/T$ is the ratio of dead time to time constant, the table below provides the recommended controller parameters.

### 2.4 Ziegler-Nichols Second Method — Ultimate Gain (Closed-Loop)

This method requires the plant to be stable in open-loop and the controller to be operated in **proportional-only mode** ($K_i = K_d = 0$).

The procedure is:
1. **Connect the controller in a closed loop** with only the proportional term active.
2. **Gradually increase $K_p$** from zero until the system exhibits **sustained oscillations** (constant amplitude sinusoidal output).
3. The critical value of $K_p$ at which this occurs is the **Ultimate Gain $K_u$**.
4. The period of these sustained oscillations is the **Ultimate Period $P_u$** (in seconds).
5. **Compute the controller parameters** using the Z-N table.

> [!WARNING]
> **Stability Caveat**: If the system is unstable for all values of $K_p$ (e.g., first-order plants with no dead time), or if sustained oscillations cannot be safely achieved, the Z-N second method **cannot be used**. In such cases, alternative methods (e.g., the reaction curve method) must be employed.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

**Table 1: Z-N Tuning Formulas**

| Controller Type | $K_p$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ | $K_i$ | $K_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $0.5 K_u$ | $\infty$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| **PI** | $0.45 K_u$ | $P_u / 1.2$ | $0$ | $K_p / T_i$ | $0$ |
| **PID** | $0.60 K_u$ | $P_u / 2$ | $P_u / 8$ | $K_p / T_i$ | $K_p \cdot T_d$ |
| **P (Reaction Curve)** | $T / (K L)$ | $\infty$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| **PI (Reaction Curve)** | $0.9 T / (K L)$ | $3 L$ | $0$ | $K_p / T_i$ | $0$ |
| **PID (Reaction Curve)** | $1.2 T / (K L)$ | $2 L$ | $0.5 L$ | $K_p / T_i$ | $K_p \cdot T_d$ |

**Table 2: Key Equations**

| Equation | Description |
|---|---|
| $C(s) = K_p(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s)$ | PID in standard ISA form |
| $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | PID in ideal parallel form |
| $K_i = K_p / T_i$ | Conversion from ISA to parallel form |
| $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$ | Conversion from ISA to parallel form |
| $G_p(s) = \frac{K e^{-Ls}}{\tau s + 1}$ | FOPDT model for reaction curve |
| $1 + K_c G_p(j\omega_u) = 0$ | Condition for sustained oscillation (Routh-Hurwitz or substitution) |

**Table 3: Physical Constants and Standard Metrics**

| Symbol | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $K_p, K_c$ | Proportional gain | dimensionless |
| $K_i$ | Integral gain | $\text{sec}^{-1}$ |
| $K_d$ | Derivative gain | sec |
| $T_i$ | Integral (reset) time | sec |
| $T_d$ | Derivative (rate) time | sec |
| $K_u$ | Ultimate gain | dimensionless |
| $P_u$ | Ultimate period | sec |
| $L$ | Dead time / transportation lag | sec |
| $T, \tau$ | Process time constant | sec |
| $K$ | Steady-state process gain | output unit / input unit |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

PID tuning is foundational in virtually every modern industrial control application:
- **Chemical Process Control**: Temperature, pressure, flow, and level control in refineries and chemical plants.
- **Robotics**: Joint position and velocity control in robotic arms and mobile platforms.
- **Aerospace**: Flight control systems, autopilot design, and engine control (FADEC).
- **Automotive**: Cruise control, anti-lock braking systems (ABS), electronic stability control.
- **Power Systems**: Excitation control of synchronous generators, automatic voltage regulators (AVR).
- **Manufacturing**: CNC machine tool position control, conveyor speed regulation.

The Z-N method remains popular in industry because it requires minimal process information (a single open-loop step test or a closed-loop oscillation test) and produces a usable controller in a matter of minutes, even for highly nonlinear processes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stability Criterion for the Ultimate Gain Method

Consider a plant with open-loop transfer function $G_p(s)$ connected in a closed loop with a pure proportional controller of gain $K_c$. The closed-loop characteristic equation is:

$$1 + K_c G_p(s) = 0$$

For the system to exhibit **sustained oscillations at frequency $\omega_u$**, the characteristic equation must have roots on the imaginary axis at $s = \pm j \omega_u$. Substituting $s = j\omega_u$:

$$1 + K_c G_p(j \omega_u) = 0$$

This means:

$$G_p(j \omega_u) = -\frac{1}{K_c}$$

The **phase condition** for oscillation requires the total phase of $K_c G_p(j \omega_u)$ to be $-180^\circ$:

$$\angle G_p(j \omega_u) = -180^\circ$$

The **magnitude condition** gives the ultimate gain:

$$K_u = \frac{1}{\vert G_p(j \omega_u) \vert}$$

The **ultimate period** is:

$$P_u = \frac{2 \pi}{\omega_u}$$

**Worked Example**: Consider $G_p(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$.

**Step 1**: Substitute $s = j\omega$ into $G_p(s)$:

$$G_p(j\omega) = \frac{1}{j\omega(j\omega + 1)(j\omega + 2)}$$

**Step 2**: Expand the denominator:

$$(j\omega + 1)(j\omega + 2) = j^2 \omega^2 + 3j\omega + 2 = (2 - \omega^2) + 3j\omega$$

**Step 3**: Multiply by $j\omega$:

$$j\omega[(2 - \omega^2) + 3j\omega] = j\omega(2 - \omega^2) + 3j^2 \omega^2 = -3\omega^2 + j\omega(2 - \omega^2)$$

**Step 4**: The full denominator is:

$$D(j\omega) = -3\omega^2 + j(2\omega - \omega^3)$$

**Step 5**: Apply the phase condition $\angle G_p(j\omega_u) = -180^\circ$. Since the numerator is real and positive, this requires the real part of the denominator to be zero:

$$\text{Re}[D(j\omega_u)] = -3 \omega_u^2 = 0$$

This gives $\omega_u = 0$ (trivial) or... wait, we need to set the phase of $1/D$ to $-180^\circ$, which means $D$ must have phase $+180^\circ$ (i.e., be a negative real number with positive imaginary part). Setting the **imaginary part to zero**:

$$\text{Im}[D(j\omega_u)] = 2\omega_u - \omega_u^3 = 0$$

Solving:

$$\omega_u(2 - \omega_u^2) = 0 \implies \omega_u^2 = 2 \implies \omega_u = \sqrt{2} \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 6**: Calculate the magnitude at $\omega_u = \sqrt{2}$:

$$\vert D(j\sqrt{2}) \vert = \sqrt{(-3(2))^2 + 0^2} = 6$$

Therefore:

$$K_u = \frac{1}{\vert D(j\sqrt{2}) \vert} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$$

Wait, we need to also check the sign of the real part. Since $\text{Re}[D] = -3 \omega_u^2 = -6$ (negative), and the imaginary part is zero, the phase of $D$ is indeed $+180^\circ$ (or $-180^\circ$), which is exactly what we need. ✓

**Step 7**: Calculate the ultimate period:

$$P_u = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_u} = \frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{2}} = \pi \sqrt{2} \approx 4.443 \text{ sec}$$

**Step 8**: Apply Z-N tuning formulas for a PID controller:

$$K_p = 0.6 K_u = 0.6 \times \frac{1}{6} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1$$

$$T_i = \frac{P_u}{2} = \frac{\pi \sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 2.221 \text{ sec}$$

$$T_d = \frac{P_u}{8} = \frac{\pi \sqrt{2}}{8} \approx 0.555 \text{ sec}$$

**Step 9**: Convert to parallel form:

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{0.1}{2.221} \approx 0.0450 \text{ sec}^{-1}$$

$$K_d = K_p \cdot T_d = 0.1 \times 0.555 \approx 0.0555 \text{ sec}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step Reaction Curve Method

Consider a process whose open-loop step response (to a unit step) yields the following S-curve parameters:
- Process gain: $K = 2$
- Dead time: $L = 1$ sec
- Time constant: $T = 5$ sec
- Ratio: $R = L/T = 0.2$

**Step 1**: Compute $K_p$ using the Z-N reaction curve formula for PID:

$$K_p = \frac{1.2 T}{K L} = \frac{1.2 \times 5}{2 \times 1} = \frac{6}{2} = 3.0$$

**Step 2**: Compute $T_i$:

$$T_i = 2 L = 2 \times 1 = 2.0 \text{ sec}$$

**Step 3**: Compute $T_d$:

$$T_d = 0.5 L = 0.5 \times 1 = 0.5 \text{ sec}$$

**Step 4**: Compute $K_i$:

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{3.0}{2.0} = 1.5 \text{ sec}^{-1}$$

**Step 5**: Compute $K_d$:

$$K_d = K_p \cdot T_d = 3.0 \times 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ sec}$$

**Step 6**: Write the final PID transfer function in parallel form:

$$C(s) = 3.0 + \frac{1.5}{s} + 1.5 s$$

### 3.3 Verification of Tuning: Performance Analysis

For the example in Section 3.1, we can verify the closed-loop step response characteristics:

**Closed-loop transfer function** with $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$ and $G_p(s) = 1/[s(s+1)(s+2)]$:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s) G_p(s)}{1 + C(s) G_p(s)} = \frac{(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s + (K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}$$

Substituting $K_p = 0.1$, $K_i = 0.0450$, $K_d = 0.0555$:

$$T(s) = \frac{0.0555 s^2 + 0.1 s + 0.0450}{s^3 + 3.0555 s^2 + 2.1 s + 0.0450}$$

The expected performance based on Z-N tuning philosophy is:
- **Overshoot**: ~25% (quarter-decay ratio)
- **Settling time**: moderate
- **Steady-state error**: 0 (due to integral action)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Closed-Loop PID Control System Architecture

The diagram below illustrates the canonical closed-loop architecture for a PID-controlled system. Every block represents a transfer function in the Laplace domain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R((R s)) --> sumNode1(("+"))
    sumNode1 --> E["E s"]
    E --> PIDBlock["PID Controller C s"]
    PIDBlock --> U["Control Signal U s"]
    U --> Plant["Plant Gp s"]
    Plant --> Y["Output Y s"]
    Y --> sumNode2(("+"))
    sumNode12(("+")) --> sumNode1
    Yfeedback["Feedback H s"] --> sumNode2
    Dist["Disturbance D s"] --> Plant
    style R fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style Y fill:#2d5a2d,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style E fill:#5a4a1f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style U fill:#5a1f5a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style PIDBlock fill:#7a1f1f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style Plant fill:#1f5a5a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style Dist fill:#5a3a1f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style Yfeedback fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style sumNode1 fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style sumNode2 fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style sumNode12 fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The summing junction (gold circle) computes the error $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$ when configured as a negative feedback loop. The disturbance $D(s)$ enters the plant and represents real-world perturbations (load changes, noise, environmental effects).

### 4.2 Z-N Ultimate Gain Tuning Procedure (Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["START: Connect PID in Closed Loop"]) --> SetP["Set Ki equals 0 and Kd equals 0"]
    SetP --> SetKpSmall["Set Kp to small positive value"]
    SetKpSmall --> Observe["Observe step response on oscilloscope"]
    Observe --> OscCheck{"Sustained oscillations?"}
    OscCheck -- "No, damped" --> IncKp["Increase Kp incrementally"]
    IncKp --> Observe
    OscCheck -- "No, diverging" --> StopGain["STOP: System is open-loop unstable"]
    OscCheck -- "Yes, constant amplitude" --> RecordKu["Record Ku and Pu"]
    RecordKu --> Compute["Compute Kp, Ti, Td from Z-N Table"]
    Compute --> Implement["Implement PID with new gains"]
    Implement --> Test["Test closed-loop performance"]
    Test --> Accept{"Satisfactory?"}
    Accept -- "No" --> FineTune["Apply fine-tuning adjustments"]
    FineTune --> Test
    Accept -- "Yes" --> End(["END: Tuning Complete"])
    style Start fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style End fill:#2d5a2d,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style StopGain fill:#7a1f1f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style RecordKu fill:#5a4a1f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style Compute fill:#5a1f5a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style OscCheck fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style Accept fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style Implement fill:#1f5a5a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style Test fill:#1f5a5a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style FineTune fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style SetP fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style SetKpSmall fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style Observe fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style IncKp fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Reaction Curve Method — Geometric Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C["Identify Inflection Point of S-Curve"]
    C --> D["Draw Tangent Line at Inflection Point"]

    D --> F["Tangent Reaches Final Value at t equals L plus T"]
    E --> G["Dead Time L seconds"]
    F --> H["Time Constant T seconds"]
    G --> I["Compute Process Gain K equals Delta Y over A"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Apply Z-N Reaction Curve Formulas"]
    J --> K["Obtain Kp, Ti, Td for P, PI, or PID"]
```
